1-6.q: Which one of the following would not be classified as a current liability?
o: Ten-year bonds sold 110 months ago.
Asecurity deposit received from a renter for a one-year lease.
Undistributed stock dividends.
Rent for the current year received on January 2 of the current year. P
1-6.e: Undistributed stock dividends do not create any liability, current or non-current. A stock dividend by a corporation is a distribution of additional shares of stock to qualifying existing stockholders, and all the transactions to record it take place in the equity section of the balance sheet. If the stock dividend is a large stock dividend (usually more than 20-25% of the total shares outstanding), on the declaration date a debit transaction for the par value of the new shares is recorded to Retained Earnings and the opposing credit transaction is to Common Shares Issuable as a Dividend, another equity account. If the stock dividend is a small stock dividend (usually less than 20-25% of the total shares outstanding), the stock dividend is valued at the fair value of the shares on their date of declaration. The fair value of the new shares is debited to Retained Earnings, the par value of the new shares is credited to Common Shares Issuable as a Dividend, and the difference is credited to Additional Paid-in Capital, all equity accounts.
wrongWhy: Ten-year bonds sold 110 months ago will have less than one year to maturity (110 months is equal to 9.17 years), so the bonds are a current liability for the issuer.
A security deposit received from a renter for a one-year lease is a current liability.

Rent for the current year received on January 2 of the current year is a current liability. ee ————————————————___€i_i_______________F hitps:ihwww. NGeMinterhational. co pimiyetoelasietne BOE par PEN BBTP ONG 330 O oo 112

1-6.q: Current Assets are listed on the Balance Sheet in order of:
o: Alphabetical order
Liquidity — most liquid first
Size — largest first
Date of acquisition
1-6.e: Current Assets are listed in order of Liquidity (most liquid first): Cash → Marketable Securities → Ar → Inventory → Prepaid Expenses. This ordering reflects how quickly each asset can be converted to cash.

1-6.q: Deferred Revenue is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: An asset — the company has the cash
A liability — the company owes goods or services
Equity — additional paid-in capital
Other comprehensive income
1-6.e: Deferred Revenue (unearned revenue) is a Liability. The company has received cash but has Not Yet Performed the service or delivered the product. It represents an obligation to deliver future goods/services.

1-6.q: The Current Portion of Long-Term Debt is classified as:
o: Non-current liability
Current liability
Long-term debt that stays non-current
Equity
1-6.e: The portion of long-term debt due Within One Year is reclassified as a Current Liability. This properly reflects that this portion requires cash payment in the near term and affects liquidity analysis.

1-6.q: Working Capital = $400,000. Current Liabilities = $150,000. Current Assets =
1-6.e: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. $400,000 = Ca − $150,000. Ca = $400,000 + $150,000 = $550,000.

1-6.q: Prepaid Insurance is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: A liability — insurance not yet used
A current asset — a future economic benefit
An expense immediately
Non-current asset always
1-6.e: Prepaid Insurance is a Current Asset — the company has paid for future insurance coverage that hasn't been used yet. It represents a Future Economic Benefit (the right to coverage). As time passes, it is expensed (Insurance Expense).

1-6.q: Non-current assets include all of the following Except:
o: Pp&E (net of depreciation)
Goodwill
Accounts Receivable
Equity method investments
1-6.e: Accounts Receivable is typically a Current Asset (expected to be collected within one year). Pp&E, goodwill, and equity method investments are all Non-Current assets because they are held for longer-term benefit.

1-6.q: A company has Current Assets of $600,000 and Current Liabilities of $400,000. Current Ratio =
1-6.e: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities = $600,000 / $400,000 = 1.5. A ratio > 1 means the company has more current assets than current liabilities — generally a sign of adequate short-term liquidity.

1-6.q: Current Assets are defined as assets that are expected to be:
o: Used in the production of goods within 1 year
Converted To Cash, Sold, Or Consumed within One Year or the Operating Cycle — whichever is Longer
Recorded at their original purchase price
Tangible assets only
1-6.e: Current Asset Classification: converted to cash, sold, or used within One Year Or The Operating Cycle, whichever is Longer. The operating cycle = time from purchasing inventory → selling → collecting cash. For most companies < 1 year, so 1 year applies. For tobacco, liquor, or lumber companies, operating cycle > 1 year — then Oc applies. Order on balance sheet: most liquid first (Cash, Short-term investments, Ar, Inventory, Prepaid expenses).

1-6.q: Working Capital is calculated as:
o: Total Assets minus Total Liabilities
Net income plus depreciation
Current Assets Minus Current Liabilities — measures the company's ability to pay short-term obligations with short-term resources
Cash and cash equivalents only
1-6.e: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Positive working capital = more Ca than Cl → generally good liquidity. Negative working capital = more Cl than Ca → potential liquidity problem. Key metric for: creditors (can the company pay short-term debts?), analysts (operational efficiency), covenant compliance (many loan covenants include minimum working capital requirements). Related: Current Ratio = Ca/Cl; Quick Ratio = (Cash + St Investments + Ar)/Cl.

1-7.q: When a fixed asset is sold for less than book value, which one of the following will decrease?

Total current assets.
Net working capital.
Current ratio.

1-7.q: According to the FASB conceptual framework, revenue may result from
o: An increase in an asset from an incidental transaction.
An increase in a liability from an incidental transaction.
Adecrease in a liability from delivering to a customer an item sold.
Adecrease in an asset resulting from delivering to a customer an item sold. this
1-7.e: According to the FASB’s Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8, Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, revenues are inflows or other enhancements of assets or settlements of liabilities (or a combination of both) that result from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities. “Other activities” are things like permitting others to use the company’s resources, resulting in interest, rent, royalties, and fees. For a charitable organization, “other activities” includes contributions received. If a customer pays a deposit in advance of receiving the goods or services contracted for, the amount received is debited to cash and credited to a current liability account such as deposits or contract liabilities, because the company has an obligation to provide the goods or services represented by that deposit. The company cannot recognize the revenue until that obligation has been fulfilled and the customer has received the goods or services paid for. When the goods or services are provided to the customer and the customer obtains control of them, the company can recognize the revenue. At that time, the company will debit the liability account for the amount of the deposit and credit revenue. Thus, the decrease in the liability will result in recognizing revenue.
wrongWhy: According to the FASB’s Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8, Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, revenues are inflows or other enhancements of assets or settlements of liabilities (or a combination of both) that result from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities. “Other activities” are things like permitting others to use the company’s resources, resulting in interest, rent, royalties, and fees. For a charitable organization, “other activities” includes contributions received. Therefore, an increase in an asset that results from incidental transactions is not revenue according to the definition of revenue in Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8. An example of an increase in an asset from an incidental transaction is the sale of some land sold at a gain, if the seller is not in the business of selling land. The receipt of cash for the sale would /2 Ci MA PART 1 SECTION (A ) External Financail Reporting ***** Income Statement 11 q 6 would involve decreasing the fixed asset, "land," by the book value of the land, which would be less than the cash received for it because the land was sold at a gain. The difference would be the gain. The result of recording the transaction would be a net increase in assets. Since according to Concepts Statement No. 8, revenue results from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities, and the company is not in the business of selling land, the gain from the sale of the land would be recognized as a gain but it would not be considered revenue.

According to the FASB’s Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8, Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, revenues are inflows or other enhancements of assets or settlements of liabilities (or a combination of both) that result from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities. “Other activities” are things like permitting others to use the company’s resources, resulting in interest, rent, royalties, and fees. For a charitable organization, “other activities” includes contributions received. An example of an increase in a liability from an incidental transaction is a cash deposit received in advance of the sale of a vehicle that was used in the business, to "hold" the vehicle and not sell it to someone else. The deposit is recorded by debiting cash and crediting a liability account. The deposit received does represents a liability to the seller until the transfer of title to the vehicle takes place; but when the sale is finalized and the title is transferred, the deposit along with the rest of the payment from the buyer, minus the book value of the vehicle, is recorded as a gain or a loss. Even if the seller has a gain on the sale, it would not be considered revenue even then, because it did not result from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities as defined above.

A decrease in an asset resulting from delivering to a customer an item sold cannot result in revenue. According to the FASB’s Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8, Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, revenues are inflows or other enhancements of assets or settlements of liabilities (or a combination of both) that result from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or other activities. “Other activities” are things like permitting others to use the company’s resources, resulting in interest, rent, royalties, and fees. For a charitable organization, “other activities” includes contributions received. However, a decrease in an asset resulting from delivering to a customer an item sold would result in recognizing an expense, for example selling inventory that results in reducing the inventory account and increasing the cost of goods sold expense account. Although the related revenue from the sale would be recognized along with an increase in an asset account, such as an increase in cash or an increase in accounts receivable, that is not what the question asks. The question is asking only what kind of a transaction would, by itself, result in recognizing revenue. And a decrease in an asset from delivering to a customer an item sold results in an expense, not revenue. -testbank/part- 2/2

1-7.q: Acompany received an invoice in January for the electricity used by its warehouse in December, and it recorded the expense in January. The company uses the accrual basis of accounting. What is the impact to the company’s December financial statements?
o: Accrued expenses were overstated and retained earnings were understated.
Cash and cash equivalents were overstated and retained earnings were understated.
Current liabilities were understated and retained earnings were overstated.
Operating expenses were overstated and retained earnings were overstated. this
1-7.e: The expense for the electricity used by the warehouse in December, a period cost, should have been recognized and accrued as a December expense because that is when it was incurred. Because it was not, current liabilities (accounts payable) were understated as of December 31. Because the expense was not recognized in December, net income was overstated and retained arnings were thus also overstated.
wrongWhy: The expense for the electricity used by the warehouse in December, a period cost, should have been recognized and accrued as a December expense when it was incurred. Failing to recognize the expense in December would not have caused accrued expenses to be overstated (rather, they were understated), nor would it have caused retained earnings to be understated (rather, they were overstated). Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The expense for the electricity used by the warehouse in December, a period cost, should have been recognized and accrued as a December expense when it was incurred. The payment for the lectricity was not made in December, so cash and cash equivalents as of the end of December were stated correctly and were not overstated. Failing to recognize the expense in December would not have caused retained earnings to be understated (rather, they were overstated). Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The expense for the electricity used by the warehouse in December, a period cost, should have been recognized and accrued as a December expense when it was incurred. Failing to recognize the expense in December would not have caused operating expenses to be overstated (rather, they were understated). Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. -testbank/part- 1/2

1-7.q: All of the following are elements of an income statement except
o: gains and losses.
shareholders’ equity.
expenses.
revenue..
1-7.e: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not an element of an income statement. Shareholders’ equity is an element of a balance sheet (also called the statement of financial position).
wrongWhy: This question is asking for the answer choice that is not an element of an income statement. Gains and losses are elements of an income statement.
This question is asking for the answer choice that is not an element of an income statement. Expenses are an element of an income statement.
This question is asking for the answer choice that is not an element of an income statement. Revenue is an element of an income statement. Ci

1-7.q: Because of inexact estimates of the service life and the residual value of a plant asset, a fully depreciated asset was sold in the current year at a material gain. This gain should be reported:
o: Inthe income from continuing operations section of the current year income statement.
As an adjustment to prior periods' depreciation on the statement of retained arnings.
As part of sales revenue on the current year income statement.
As an unusual or infrequent event in the unusual and infrequent events section of the current year income statement..
1-7.e: The gain on the sale of an asset is reported in the income statement in the section of continuing operations in the year in which it is sold.
wrongWhy: The sale of a fixed asset is not recorded as an adjustment to prior periods’ depreciation in the financial statements.
The sale of a fixed asset is not recorded as revenue in the financial statements.

The sale of a fixed asset is not recorded as an unusual or infrequent event in the financial statements, and unusual or infrequent events are not reported in a separate section of the income statement. Unusual or infrequent events are reported as separate line items within the income from continuing operations section of the income statement or, alternatively, included in some other line of the income from continuing operations section and disclosed in the notes to financial statements. Ci

1-7.q: Acompany uses the calendar year as its financial results reporting time period. On May 31 of the prior year, the company committed to a plan to sell a line of business. The sale represents a strategic shift that will have a major effect on the company's operations and financial results. For the period January 1 through May 31 of the prior year, the line of business had revenues of $1,000,000 and expenses of $1,600,000. The assets of the line of u business were sold on November 30, at a loss for which no tax benefit is available. In its income statement for the year ended December 31 of the prior year, how should the company report the line of business operations from January 1 through May 31? id e A. $600,000 should be reported as an unusual or infrequent loss.
o: $600,000 should be reported as an unusual or infrequent loss.
$1,000,000 and $1,600,000 should be included with revenues and expenses, respectively, as part of continuing operations.
$600,000 should be included in the determination of income or loss from operations of a discontinued component.
$600,000 should be reported as part of the loss on disposal of a component.
1-7.e: The sale of the line of business represents a strategic shift that will have a major effect on the company's operations and financial results. Therefore, it qualifies for financial reporting as a discontinued operation. All gains or losses that are incurred by a discontinued segment are reported in the period in which the gain or loss occurred and the gains or losses are reported net of associated taxes. Operating results of a discontinued component or group of components must be reported on the income statement as discontinued operations in the period when management commits to a plan to sell the entity; the entity to be sold is available for immediate sale; an active program to locate a buyer or buyers and other actions required to complete the plan to sell the entity have been initiated; the sale is probable within one year, unless events beyond the ntity's control occur; the entity is being actively marketed at a reasonable price in relation to its fair value; or actions required to complete the plan to sell the entity make it unlikely that the plan will be withdrawn or significantly changed. The period from January 1 through May 371 is in the period in which the criteria were met. Therefore, the operating results of the discontinued component must be reported net of tax in the discontinued operations section of the income statement.
wrongWhy: Operating results of a discontinued component or group of components must be reported net of associated taxes on the income statement as discontinued operations, not as an unusual or infrequent loss. An unusual or infrequent loss is reported as part of income from continuing operations.

Operating results of a discontinued component or group of components must be reported net of associated taxes as discontinued operations on the income statement. ASC 205-20-45-1E requires the held-for-sale classification in the period in which all of the following criteria are met: Management commits to a plan to sell the entity. The entity to be sold is available for immediate sale. An active program to locate a buyers or buyers and other actions required to complete the plan to sell the entity have been initiated. The sale is probable within one year, unless events beyond the entity's control occur. The entity is being actively marketed at a reasonable price in relation to its fair value. Actions required to complete the plan to sell the entity make it unlikely that the plan will be withdrawn or significantly changed. Since the period from January 1 through May 371 is in the period in which the criteria were met, the operating results of the discontinued component must be reported net of tax in the discontinued operations section of the income statement.

All gains or losses that are incurred by a discontinued segment are reported in the period in which the gain or loss occurred and the gains or losses are reported net of associated taxes. Operating results of a discontinued component or group of components must be reported on the income statement as discontinued operations. The gain or loss from the actual disposal of the entity, which did not take place until November 30, should be reported separately along with the related results of operations of the discontinued component in the discontinued operations section of the income statement in the period when the sale takes place. Therefore, for the period January 1 through May 31, a $600,000 loss should not be reported as part of the loss on disposal of a component. -testbank/part-

1-7.q: According to U.S. GAAP, where on the income statement should a multinational company report the loss from the disposal sale of a major operating unit?
o: Report the loss, net of tax, in a separate section between income before tax and net income.
Report the loss, pretax, in a separate section between income from continuing operations and net income.
Report the loss, net of tax, ina separate section between income from continuing operations and net income.
Report the loss, pretax, in a separate section between income from operations and income before income tax.
1-7.e: The gain or loss from the disposal of a major operating unit should be reported net of tax separately along with the related results of operations of the discontinued component in the discontinued operations section of the income statement. The discontinued operations section of the income statement follows income from continuing operations (after tax). For example, a loss on a discontinued component would be presented in the discontinued operations section of the income statement as: Income from continuing operations (after tax) XXXXXK Loss from operations of discontinued Component X (including loss on disposal of $XXXX) XXXXX Income tax benefit on loss from discontinued Component X XXXX Loss on discontinued operation XXXX Net income XXXXXK
wrongWhy: The discontinued operations section of the income statement follows income from continuing operations (after tax).
The loss is reported net of tax.
The loss is reported net of tax. The discontinued operations section of the income statement follows income from continuing operations (after tax). -testba: 1/2

1-7.q: The financial statement that provides a summary of the firm’s operations for a period of time is the
o: statement of retained earnings.
income statement.
statement of shareholders’ equity.
statement of financial position.
1-7.e: The income statement reports the results of operations for a period of time.
wrongWhy: The statement of retained earnings does not report the results of operations for a period of time.
The statement of shareholders’ equity does not report the results of operations for a period of time.

1-7.q: During the month of October, a company purchased 1,000 units of inventory for $500 per unit and sold 900 of these units which represented 10% of the company’s annual sales budget in units. The company also incurred administrative costs of $300,000 during October. By applying the matching principle, the total amount of the company’s expenses on its October income statement is
1-7.e: The matching principle requires that a company recognize the expenses that generate revenue in the same period that the revenue is recognized. Or, looking at it in another way, a company should recognize revenue in the same period that the expenses that generate that revenue are expensed. For this question, the company needs to recognize two expenses. The first is the cost of goods sold for the 900 units that were sold. These 900 units were purchased for $500 each, for a total of $450,000. The second expense is the $300,000 of administrative costs that need to be expensed as they are incurred. These two expenses together total $750,000.
wrongWhy: This answer recognizes the cost of all 1,000 units purchased as an expense, not only for the 900 units sold.

1-7.q: Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) includes unrealized gains/losses on Available-for-Sale (AFS) securities. This means these gains/losses:
o: Are included in net income on the income statement
Bypass the income statement but are reported in OCI and affect equity
Are never reported until the securities are sold
Are reported only in the notes
1-7.e: AFS security unrealized gains/losses Bypass the income statement and are reported directly in OCI (Other Comprehensive Income). They are accumulated in Aoci (equity) until the security is sold, at which point they are reclassified to net income.

1-7.q: Trading securities report unrealized gains/losses:
o: In OCI (bypasses income statement)
In net income on the income statement
Only when sold
In retained earnings directly
1-7.e: Trading securities are reported at fair value with unrealized gains/losses flowing Through Net Income each period. This is different from AFS securities, where unrealized changes go to OCI.

1-7.q: Comprehensive Income =
o: Net Income only
Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income
Gross Profit + OCI
Net Income − Dividends
1-7.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income (OCI). It captures All changes in equity during a period Except owner transactions (investments and dividends). OCI items include AFS unrealized G/L, foreign currency translation, pension adjustments.

1-7.q: Discontinued operations are presented on the income statement:
o: As part of operating income
Within cost of goods sold
Separately, After income from continuing operations, Net of tax
Only in the notes — not on the face
1-7.e: Discontinued operations are presented Separately at the bottom of the income statement, After income from continuing operations. They are shown Net Of Tax. This separate presentation allows users to better assess the results of Continuing operations.

1-7.q: In a multi-step income statement, Gross Profit is calculated as:
o: Net Sales − All Operating Expenses
Net Sales − Cost of Goods Sold
Operating Income − Non-operating items
Net Income + Tax Expense
1-7.e: Gross Profit = Net Sales Revenue − Cost Of Goods Sold. It represents profit before operating expenses (Sga, R&D). It is the first key subtotal in a multi-step income statement.

1-7.q: Foreign currency translation adjustments are reported in:
o: Net income on the income statement
Cost of goods sold
OCI — not net income
Retained earnings directly
1-7.e: Foreign currency Translation adjustments (translating foreign subsidiary FS into the parent's reporting currency) are reported in OCI, not in net income. They are accumulated in Aoci until the foreign subsidiary is sold.

1-7.q: Pension Liability adjustments (actuarial gains/losses) are reported in:
o: Operating expenses
OCI — Other Comprehensive Income
Net income directly
Notes only
1-7.e: Pension actuarial gains/losses and prior service costs are reported in OCI (accumulated in Aoci). They may be subsequently amortized into net income via the corridor method or immediately recognized in OCI under IFRS.

1-7.q: Comprehensive Income is equal to:
o: Net income plus revenues minus expenses
Only items recorded on the income statement
Net Income Plus All Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) Items — the total change in equity during a period excluding transactions with owners
Operating income plus non-operating items
1-7.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income. It captures All Changes In Equity during a period Except transactions with owners (issuing stock, dividends). OCI items: unrealized G/L on AFS securities, pension actuarial G/L and prior service cost, foreign currency translation adjustments, unrealized G/L on qualifying cash flow hedges. Aoci (Accumulated OCI) on the balance sheet = cumulative total of past OCI items.

1-7.q: Discontinued Operations on the income statement are reported:
o: As part of continuing operations within operating income
Only in footnotes — not on the face of the income statement
Net Of Tax as a Separate Line Item below income from continuing operations — showing only the net effect of the discontinued component
Above gross profit as a separately identified revenue stream
1-7.e: Discontinued Operations (ASC 205-20): reported Separately from continuing operations on the income statement: (1) Income from continuing operations (shown first); (2) Discontinued operations — shown Net Of Tax as one line item (or two: results of operations + gain/loss on disposal). Rationale: investors should be able to see what the Continuing business earns vs. the discontinued segment, for better predictive value.

Comprehensive Income includes all transactions of the company except for transactions made with the owners of the company (such as distribution of dividends or the sale or repurchase of shares). It includes everything on the Income Statement plus some specific items that do not appear on the Income Statement that are called “Other Comprehensive Income.” Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income Thus, only items included as Other Comprehensive Income will cause Comprehensive Income reported in the Statement of Comprehensive Income to differ from Net Income. In a fair value hedge, when the relationship between the hedged item and the hedging ~ instrument is highly effective, gains and losses on the derivative and the hedged item are both recognized currently in Net Income. Since gains and losses on a fair value hedge are recognized in Net Income and also in Comprehensive Income, they will not cause a difference between Comprehensive Income and Net Income. Only gains and losses on derivative instruments that are designated as, and qualify as, cash “ flow hedges will create a difference between Net Income and Comprehensive Income, because gains and losses on cash flow hedges are reported as Other Comprehensive Income.

1-8.q: Fi Which of the following statements is accurate with respect to the reporting of Comprehensive Income?
o: Comprehensive Income Per Share must be reported.
The balance of Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income is represented in the Shareholders' Equity section of the balance sheet.
Comprehensive Income must be presented as a single continuous statement including both Net Income and Other Comprehensive Income.
The Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income account is a temporary account and is closed out every year.
1-8.e: Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income is a line in the Equity section of the balance sheet that includes the items that are not reflected on the Income Statement. Net Income flows to Retained Earnings in Equity when the year-end close is performed, so Net Income ends up in Equity. However, certain specific items are considered “Other Comprehensive Income” items, and they are not presented on the Income Statement. Instead, they go straight to Equity by means of the Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income account.
wrongWhy: Comprehensive Income Per Share is not reported.

The Statement of Comprehensive Income can be presented as a single continuous statement, including Net Income followed by Other Comprehensive Income. Or, it can be presented as two separate but consecutive financial statements: a Statement of Net Income and a Statement of Comprehensive Income presenting the components of Other Comprehensive Income. The Statement of Comprehensive Income should immediately follow the Statement of Net Income and begin with the Net Income figure.

The word “accumulated” in “Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income” means that the balance in the account keeps accumulating. As Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income is a balance sheet account, it is a permanent account. It is never closed out, and the balance in it keeps accumulating just like any other balance sheet account. The Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income balance in the account may be referred to as AOCI. Other Comprehensive Income for a year is the amount of change in the Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income account.

1-8.q: Which of the following is a part of Other Comprehensive Income?
o: Year-end accruals of liabilities.
Allowance for credit losses on accounts receivable under the Current Expected Credit Losses model.
Unrealized gains and losses on a debt security transferred from the held-to-maturity classification to the available-for-sale classification.
Impairment losses on fixed assets.
1-8.e: Unrealized gains and losses on a debt security transferred from the held-to-maturity classification to the available-for-sale classification are reported as Other Comprehensive Income. If a held-to-maturity debt security is transferred into the available-for-sale classification, the entity must e Reverse in earnings any allowance for credit losses previously recorded on the security at the transfer date e Reclassify the debt security to the available-for-sale classification at its amortized cost basis (reduced by any previous writeoffs but excluding any allowance for credit losses). The amortized cost basis is used for subsequent amortization of the historical premium or discount and for comparisons of fair value and amortized cost for determining unrealized holding gains and losses going forward. e Determine if an allowance for credit losses is necessary e Report in Other Comprehensive Income any unrealized gain or loss on the available-for-sale debt security at the date of transfer, excluding any amount recorded in the Allowance for Credit Losses e Consider whether the transfer calls into question the entity’s intent and ability to continue to classify as held-to-maturity the securities remaining in the held-to-maturity category.
wrongWhy: Year-end accruals of liabilities are reported on the balance sheet and the accrued expense is reported in the income statement. Neither one is part of Other Comprehensive Income.
The Allowance for Credit Losses is a contra-asset account on the balance sheet. Credit Loss Expense is an Income Statement item. Neither one is part of Other Comprehensive Income.

Under U.S. GAAP, fixed assets are not written up to recognize any increase in the fair value of the asset over time. The amount by which an impaired asset is written down is reported as a current period loss, so it is reported in earnings and not as Other Comprehensive Income. hitps:ihwww. The Statement of Cash Flows.
The Statement of Comprehensive Income.
The income statement.

1-8.q: A reclassification adjustment is needed when OCI items are realized because:
o: GAAP requires it for tax purposes
It prevents Double-Counting when gains/losses move from OCI to Net Income
It increases retained earnings
It eliminates foreign currency risk
1-8.e: Reclassification Adjustment: when an OCI item is realized (e.g., AFS security sold), it moves to Net Income. The reclassification adjustment simultaneously Removes it from OCI to prevent counting it twice in comprehensive income.

1-8.q: Under the two-statement approach for comprehensive income:
o: Only one combined statement is prepared
A separate income statement And a separate Statement of Comprehensive Income are prepared
OCI is shown within operating expenses
Net income is not reported separately
1-8.e: Two-statement approach: a Separate Income Statement (showing net income) followed by a Separate Statement of Comprehensive Income (beginning with net income, then adding OCI items to arrive at comprehensive income).

1-8.q: OCI items on the Statement of Comprehensive Income are shown:
o: At gross amounts
Net of tax effects
Only as positive amounts
In alphabetical order
1-8.e: OCI items are presented Net Of Their Tax Effects. Either individual items can be shown net of tax, or they can be shown gross with a single aggregate tax amount. The net-of-tax presentation is most common.

1-8.q: Foreign currency translation adjustments are reclassified from Aoci to Net Income when:
o: The exchange rate changes by more than 10%
The foreign subsidiary is completely liquidated or sold
At the end of each reporting period
When declared by management
1-8.e: Foreign currency Translation adjustments are reclassified from Aoci to Net Income Only when the foreign subsidiary is completely disposed of (sold or liquidated). Cumulative translation adjustments stay in Aoci until that complete disposition.

1-8.q: Comprehensive Income for a period = $200,000. Net Income = $180,000. OCI =
1-8.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI. $200,000 = $180,000 + OCI. OCI = $200,000 − $180,000 = $20,000.

1-8.q: Under US GAAP, companies may present comprehensive income using which method(s)?
o: Only the one-statement approach (combining IS and OCI)
Only the two-statement approach (separate OCI statement)
Either the One-Statement Approach (single combined statement) Or the Two-Statement Approach (separate IS followed by a separate OCI statement) — both are acceptable
Only in the notes to financial statements
1-8.e: Presentation Options (ASC 220): (1) One-Statement: continues from Net Income directly into OCI items, ending with Total Comprehensive Income; (2) Two-Statement: first statement ends at Net Income; immediately following second statement starts with Net Income, adds OCI, shows Total Comprehensive Income. Both are acceptable under US GAAP. OCI cannot be presented Only in the statement of stockholders' equity — must be on a statement of its own or combined with the IS.

1-9.q: Financial Statements) The statement of changes in stockholders' equity shows a
o: computation of the number of shares outstanding used for earnings per share calculations.
reconciliation of the beginning and ending balances in the individual stockholders' equity accounts.
reconciliation of the beginning and ending balances in the Retained Earnings account.
listing of all stockholders’ equity accounts and their corresponding dollar amounts.
1-9.e: Firms are required to present a reconciliation of the beginning and ending balances of their stockholders' equity accounts. The statement of changes in stockholders’ equity reports the changes in each stockholders' equity account and in total stockholders’ equity during the year and reconciles the beginning balance in each account with the ending balance.
wrongWhy: The computation of the number of shares outstanding used for earnings per share calculations is not shown on the statement of changes in stockholders’ equity.
The statement of changes in stockholders’ equity shows more than just a reconciliation of the beginning and ending balances in the Retained Earnings account.
This is a description of the equity section of the balance sheet. The statement of changes in stockholders' equity shows more than just the ending balances of the stockholders' equity accounts.

1-9.q: Financial Statements) Which of the following is the best definition of the going concern concept?
o: The entity will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future.
The entity will continue to make profits for the foreseeable future.
The entity will not incur losses in the next three years.
The entity will continue in existence forever.
1-9.e: The idea of going concern is the assumption that the company will continue in operations for the foreseeable future. Nothing in this assumption implies that the company will be profitable in the future or that it will continue in existence forever, only that it will continue to operate for the foreseeable future.
wrongWhy: The going concern concept does not include any assumption of profitability.
The going concern concept does not include any assumption of profitability.
It is unlikely that many entities will continue in existence forever.

1-9.q: Financial Statements) All of the following are limitations to the information provided on the statement of financial position except the
o: lack of current valuation for most assets and liabilities.
quality of the earnings reported for the enterprise.
omission of items that are of financial value to the business such as the worth of the employees.
judgments and estimates used regarding the collectibility, salability, and longevity of assets.
1-9.e: The statement of financial position, or balance sheet, does not report earnings for the enterprise at all. Earnings are reported on the income statement.
wrongWhy: Values of certain assets are measured at historical cost, not fair (or market) value, replacement cost, or their value to the firm, for the balance sheet. For example, property, plant and equipment are reported on the balance sheet at their historical cost minus accumulated depreciation, although the assets’ value in use may be significantly greater.

Many assets are not reported on the balance sheet, even though they do have value and will generate future cash flows. Examples of these include the company’s employees, or its human resources, its processes and procedures, and its competitive advantages.

Judgments and estimates are used in determining many of the items reported in the balance sheet. For example, estimates of the amount of receivables the company will collect are used to value the accounts receivable; the expected useful life of fixed assets is used to determine the amount of depreciation; and the company’s liability for future warranty claims is estimated by projecting the number and the cost of the future claims. Because these are estimates, they are by nature not precise. hitps:ihwww. Noe Minterhational. co pimiyeloelnsietnd ESE par PE BBTPO ING 330 O pair 112

1-9.q: Financial Statements) All of the following are limitations of the balance sheet except that
o: the balance sheet provides information on the liquidity and solvency of the company.
the balance sheet omits many items that cannot be recorded objectively but which have financial value to the company.
the balance sheet is prepared using management judgments and estimates.
assets and liabilities are usually recorded at historical cost, which might differ significantly from current fair value.
1-9.e: Providing information on the liquidity and solvency of the company is not a limitation of the balance sheet. It is a characteristic of the balance sheet. The balance sheet helps to assess the company’s liquidity, financial flexibility, solvency, and risk.
wrongWhy: This is a limitation of the balance sheet. Examples of items that cannot be recorded objectively on the balance sheet but which have financial value to the company include its human assets, that is, its employees; money it has spent on research and development efforts that must be expensed according to GAAP; and its customers.

This is a limitation of the balance sheet. Examples of management judgments and estimates include the planned life of assets over which their cost is depreciated, estimated warranty expenses that are charged to expense as the sales are made, and estimated credit losses that are charged to expense as the sales are made.
This is a limitation of the balance sheet.

1-9.q: The First note in financial statements is always:
o: Related party transactions
Contingencies
Summary of significant accounting policies
Earnings per share
1-9.e: The Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies is always the First note. It describes key accounting choices: inventory costing method, depreciation method, revenue recognition policy, consolidation policy, etc. — essential for interpreting all other financial data.

1-9.q: A fire destroys a warehouse two weeks after the Balance Sheet date but before the FS are issued. This is a:
o: Recognized subsequent event — adjust the FS
Non-recognized subsequent event — disclose in notes only
Not required to be disclosed
Extraordinary item
1-9.e: The fire occurred After the Balance Sheet date — the condition did Not exist at BS date. This is a Non-Recognized subsequent event: Disclose in notes but do Not adjust the FS. The opposite (adjust) applies if the condition existed at the BS date.

1-9.q: A lawsuit was filed before year-end. After year-end but before FS issuance, the court rules and orders payment of $500,000. This is a:
o: Non-recognized event — disclose only
Recognized subsequent event — Adjust the FS to record the liability
Not required to be disclosed
Treated as a prior period adjustment
1-9.e: The lawsuit Existed at the BS date — only the final amount became known after. This is a Recognized subsequent event: Adjust the FS to record the $500,000 liability. The condition (the lawsuit) existed; the post-BS event just clarified the amount.

1-9.q: The Statement of Changes in Stockholders' Equity shows which of the following?
o: Only retained earnings changes
Changes in All equity components: common stock, Apic, Re, Aoci, treasury stock
Cash flow information
Income tax calculations
1-9.e: The Statement of Changes in Equity shows All Changes in Every equity component: common stock (par), Apic, retained earnings (net income minus dividends), accumulated OCI (other comprehensive income), and treasury stock. It bridges beginning and ending equity balances.

1-9.q: Fair value Level 1 inputs are Best described as:
o: Management's internal estimates
Unobservable inputs
Quoted prices in active markets for identical assets
Prices from similar but not identical assets
1-9.e: Level 1 = Quoted Prices in Active Markets for Identical assets or liabilities. Most reliable — directly observable market prices (e.g., stock prices). Level 2 = observable but indirect. Level 3 = unobservable (management estimates) — least reliable.

1-9.q: The Statement Of Changes In Stockholders' Equity shows:
o: Only the change in retained earnings during the period
Only net income and dividends declared
The Beginning And Ending Balances Of All Equity Components and All Changes during the period — including net income, OCI, dividends, stock issuances, and treasury stock transactions
Only transactions that affect cash
1-9.e: Statement Of Changes In Stockholders' Equity: a rollforward of Each equity component (Common Stock, Apic, Retained Earnings, Aoci, Treasury Stock). Shows: beginning balance + all additions (NI, OCI, stock issuances) − all deductions (dividends, treasury stock purchases) = ending balance. Required under both GAAP and IFRS. Provides: complete history of equity changes; reconciliation that the BS equity totals are correct; details on dividend policy.

1-9.q: The First Note to financial statements is Always:
o: Contingencies and legal proceedings
Subsequent events disclosure
The Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies — always presented as Note 1, disclosing the key choices (revenue recognition, inventory method, depreciation method, consolidation policy, etc.)
Earnings per share calculation
1-9.e: First Note = Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies: always presented first. Content: (1) Basis of presentation (GAAP or IFRS); (2) Consolidation principles; (3) Revenue recognition; (4) Inventory valuation method (FIFO, LIFO, weighted avg); (5) Depreciation methods and useful lives; (6) Any other significant accounting policy choices. This note allows users to understand How the numbers were prepared — essential context for all other notes.

1-9.q: A Subsequent Event that provides additional evidence about Conditions That Existed at the balance sheet date should be:
o: Disclosed in notes but not adjusted in the financial statements
Ignored — only events during the reporting period matter
Recorded As An Adjustment to the financial statements — because the condition existed at the balance sheet date, the financial statements should reflect this information
Reported to the SEC within 4 business days
1-9.e: Adjusting Subsequent Events (Type I): events occurring After the balance sheet date but Before the financial statements are issued that provide Evidence about conditions that Existed at the balance sheet date → Adjust the financial statements. Examples: settling a lawsuit that existed at year-end for a different amount than estimated; determining a customer that owed Ar at year-end has filed for bankruptcy. The condition (lawsuit, credit risk) Existed at year-end — new information just changes the measurement.

1-9.q: A Subsequent Event that provides evidence about Conditions That Arose after the balance sheet date should be:
o: Recorded as an adjustment to the financial statements
Ignored — it is not part of the reporting period
Disclosed In The Notes only (not adjusted) — the condition did not exist at the balance sheet date, so financial statements should not be changed
Included in next period's financial statements only
1-9.e: Non-Adjusting Subsequent Events (Type Ii): events occurring After the balance sheet date that provide evidence about conditions that Arose After the balance sheet date → Disclose In Notes Only — do not adjust the statements. Examples: major natural disaster (flood destroys plant after year-end), major acquisition announced after year-end, significant new debt issuance. The condition Did Not Exist at year-end — no adjustment appropriate, but disclosure needed for complete picture.

1-9.q: The Fair Value Hierarchy under ASC 820 ranks inputs into Three levels. Level 1 inputs are:
o: Management's own assumptions and models
Observable inputs from comparable but not identical assets
Quoted Prices In Active Markets For Identical Assets Or Liabilities — the most reliable evidence of fair value
Historical cost of the asset
1-9.e: Fair Value Hierarchy (ASC 820): Level 1 (most reliable): quoted prices in Active Markets for Identical assets/liabilities — e.g., share price of Apple stock on Nasdaq. Level 2: observable inputs (not Level 1) — similar assets, market corroborated data, yield curves. Level 3 (least reliable): Unobservable inputs — management's own assumptions, discounted cash flow models. Notes must disclose: level of FV hierarchy used for each class of asset/liability, and reconciliation of Level 3 inputs.

1-9.q: Level 3 Fair Value Measurements are considered Least reliable because:
o: They are based on complex mathematical formulas
They require external appraiser involvement
They Use Unobservable Inputs — management's assumptions and models, not verifiable market data
They are calculated only at year-end
1-9.e: Level 3 FV: unobservable inputs — management cannot find observable market data and must use their own Internal Assumptions (DCF models with management's estimates of future cash flows and discount rates). Less reliable because: (1) management may be biased; (2) cannot be verified against market; (3) small changes in assumptions produce large FV changes. Required disclosures: description of valuation technique, significant unobservable inputs used, sensitivity of FV to changes in unobservable inputs.

1-9.q: Related Party Transactions must be disclosed in the notes because:
o: They are always fraudulent or illegal
They are always material to the financial statements
Related Party Transactions May Not Be On Arm'S Length Terms — they could be structured to benefit insiders at the expense of the company or other shareholders
They require SEC approval before completion
1-9.e: Related Party Disclosures (ASC 850): transactions between the reporting entity and parties that have a Special Relationship (parent-subsidiary, officer, director, significant shareholder, family members of officers). Required disclosures: nature of the relationship, description of transactions, amounts, any amounts still outstanding. Why: related party transactions may Not reflect arm's length pricing — management could manipulate earnings by pricing inter-company transactions favorably. Users need this context to evaluate the financial statements.

1-9.q: Contingent Liabilities must be disclosed in the Notes when the loss is classified as:
o: Remote — disclose all possible losses
Reasonably Possible — possible but not probable, requiring note disclosure without balance sheet recognition
Probable and estimable — these are recorded on the balance sheet instead
Both probable and reasonably possible — all contingent liabilities require notes
1-9.e: Contingency Disclosure: (1) Probable + Estimable → Accrue on balance sheet (And disclose in notes); (2) Reasonably Possible → Disclose In Notes Only (not accrued); (3) Remote → generally no disclosure. Note disclosure for reasonably possible/accrued: nature of contingency, estimate of possible loss range (or statement that estimate cannot be made). The notes fill in the detail that users need to understand the potential financial impact.

1-9.q: A company discovers a Material Subsequent Event before the financial statements are issued (but after the balance sheet date). The period during which subsequent events must be considered is:
o: Only the 5 business days after fiscal year-end
Between The Balance Sheet Date And The Date The Financial Statements Are Issued Or Available To Be Issued
Unlimited — all future events must be considered
Only events occurring in the same fiscal year
1-9.e: Subsequent Events Period: from the Balance Sheet Date to the date the financial statements are Issued (public companies) or Available To Be Issued (non-public companies). Management must actively search for events in this window. Examples: if fiscal year ends Dec 31 and FS issued Feb 28 — events occurring Jan 1–Feb 28 are potential subsequent events. The period can be weeks to months depending on how quickly the FS are completed.

1-9.q: Segment Information in the notes (ASC 280) is required for public companies to disclose:
o: Segment information only if segments are unprofitable
Information about each country of operation only
Information About Operating Segments that the Chief Operating Decision Maker (Codm) uses internally — revenue, profit/loss, and assets for each reportable segment
Only the most profitable segment's details
1-9.e: ASC 280 Segment Reporting: public companies must disclose information about Reportable Operating Segments based on How Management Internally Reports (the 'management approach'). Reportable if: revenue ≥ 10% of total revenue, Or profit/loss ≥ 10% of combined segment profit, Or assets ≥ 10% of total assets. Required disclosures per segment: revenues, profit/loss, assets, and reconciliation to consolidated totals. Enables users to assess which parts of the business drive performance.

1-9.q: Accounting Policy Disclosures in the notes include information about:
o: Only changes to accounting policies during the current period
Estimates only — not policies
Significant Policies That Management Has Chosen where alternatives exist — such as inventory method (FIFO vs. weighted avg), depreciation method (Sl vs. Ddb), and revenue recognition approach
All policies from the company's founding date
1-9.e: Accounting Policy Disclosures: Note 1 discloses the Significant Accounting Policies — the choices management made from acceptable alternatives under GAAP/IFRS. Why Important: two companies can report very different numbers for identical economic events if they use different (both acceptable) accounting methods. Knowing FIFO vs. LIFO, straight-line vs. accelerated depreciation, or specific revenue recognition methods is Essential for meaningful comparison.

1-10.q: Which one of the following should be classified as an operating activity on the statement of cash flows?
o: An increase in cash resulting from the issuance of previously authorized common stock.
Adecrease in accounts payable during the year.
The payment of a cash dividend from money arising from current operations.
The purchase of additional equipment needed for current production.
1-10.e: Operating activities are generally part of the company's main business activities and central operations. These are essentially items that generate revenues and expenses. When accounts payable decreases, it means cash has been disbursed for operating activities. Thus a decrease in accounts payable during the year should be classified as an operating activity on the statement of cash flows.
wrongWhy: An increase in cash resulting from the issuance of previously authorized common stock is classified on the statement of cash flows as a financing activity. Financing activities are the activities that a company undertakes to raise capital to finance the business, and issuing stock _ raises capital to finance the business.

The payment of a cash dividend is classified on the statement of cash flows as a financing activity, regardless of where the money to pay the dividend came from. Financing activities are the activities that a company undertakes to raise capital to finance the business, and paying a cash dividend is a financing activity.

The purchase of additional equipment needed for current production is classified on the statement of cash flows as an investing activity. Investing activities are those activities that the company undertakes to generate a future profit, or return, such as purchasing fixed assets.

This presentation is different from the indirect method of presentation because the indirect method begins with net income and adjusts net income to (1) include net changes in operating cash that do not appear on the income statement and (2) to remove noncash items that are included in the income statement. "

The cash method of determining income does not present the major classes of operating cash receipts less the major classes of operating cash disbursements.

financing activity.
investing activity.

1-10.e: ta Investing activities are those activities that the company undertakes to generate a future profit, or return, such as purchasing and selling fixed assets, purchasing and selling stock of other companies, purchasing and selling debt instruments, and purchasing and selling available-for- sale or held-to-maturity securities. Therefore, the sale of available-for-sale debt securities should be classified on the statement of cash flows as an investing activity. 1-10.q: When using the statement of cash flows to evaluate a company’s continuing solvency, the most important factor to consider is the cash
o: flows from (used for) financing activities.
flows from (used for) operating activities.
balance at the end of the period.
flows from (used for) investing activities.
1-10.e: Solvency refers to a firm's ability to cover its liabilities with its assets. If a firm is not able to generate a positive cash flow from its operating activities, it is or soon will be insolvent. Therefore, cash flows from and used for operating activities is the most important factor to consider when using the statement of cash flows to evaluate a company's continuing solvency.

determine a firm's components of income from operations.
determine whether insiders have sold or purchased the firm's stock.

1-11.q: When using the indirect method to prepare a statement of cash flows, net cash flows from operating activities are determined by adding back or deducting from net income those items included in net income that had no effect on cash. 1-11.e: A premium on a bond payable is an amount received over and above the bond's par value when the bond is originally issued (sold). Only the par value of the bond will be repayable as principal when the bond matures. The additional amount (the premium) received from its sale is not repaid, so it is a gain. The premium is amortized over the life of the bond as a reduction of interest expense on the bond. The amortization of the premium on a bond payable is a noncash transaction that reduces interest expense on the bond, so it increases net income. Therefore, the amount of the periodic amortization needs to be deducted from net income when determining net cash flows from operating activities using the indirect method. au

1-11.q: F Below are the balances for the following accounts on the balance sheet of a company as of the end of Year 20X2 and Year 20X1. Year 20X2 Year 20X1 Cash $1,000 $ 800 Marketable securities ? 7,200 Inventory 500 500 Wages and salaries payable 2,000 2,500 Accounts payable 1,300 1,000 u Accounts receivable 5,000 6,000 Prepaids 1,200 500 a8 The marketable securities consist of money market funds, commercial paper, and U.S. Treasury Bills that all had a maturity of 3 months or less from the date they were acquired. The company has net income for Year 20X2 of $1,900, has no fixed assets, and has no investing or financing az activities. Using the indirect method for preparing the cash flow statement, what is the balance in the marketable securities account as of the end of Year 20X2?
1-11.e:
In order to answer this question, it is necessary to understand three things: 1. "Cash" on the SCF is the total of cash and cash equivalents (also restricted forms of both, though that is not needed for this question). 2. Cash equivalents are marketable securities that had a maturity date of 3 months or less when they were acquired, and thus the marketable securities in this question are all classified as cash equivalents. 3. The net increase (decrease) in cash and cash equivalents on the SCF during the period from all sources —operating, investing, and financing activities—must reconcile to the amount of change in the sum of cash and cash equivalents from the end of the previous period to the end of the current period. Since there were no investing or financing activities, the net cash provided by operating activities is the same as the net increase (decrease) in cash and cash equivalents from all sources. Net cash provided by operating activities is calculated as follows: Net income $1,900 Decrease in A/R 1,000 Increase in prepaids (760
Increase in A/P 300 Decrease in wages and salaries payable _(500) Net cash provided by operating activities $2,000 Cash and cash equivalents at the end of 20X1 were $8,000 ($800 + $7,200). Since there were no investing or financing activities, the net increase in cash and cash equivalents during the period is the same as the net cash provided by operating activities, or $2,000. Therefore, the sum of cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet at the end of 20X2 must be $10,000: $8,000 at the end of 20X1 plus $2,000 net cash provided by operating activities (which is also the net increase in cash and cash equivalents on the SCF during 20X2). Cash at the end of 20X2 was $1,000. Therefore, marketable securities classified as cash equivalents at the end . of 20X2 were $9,000 ($10,000 - $1,000).

1-12.e: Financing activities are activities a company undertakes to raise capital to finance the business and the repayment of the same. Common events that are financing activities according to the Accounting Standards Codification® include the following, and the second and third items are the two items in this answer choice: e Proceeds from issuance of stock. e Treasury stock transactions (treasury stock is shares that have been issued and then later repurchased by the issuer.) e Paying cash dividends. e Proceeds from issuing debt such as bonds, mortgages, notes, and payments for debt issuance costs and repayment of principal on debt obligations. e Obtaining a loan and repaying the principal of the loan. e Debt prepayment or debt extinguishment costs, including call premiums paid and fees paid to lenders or third parties that are directly related to the debt prepayment or debt extinguishment, excluding accrued interest. e Payments not made soon after the acquisition date of a business combination by an acquirer to settle a contingent consideration liability, up to the amount of liability recognized at the acquisition date. e Payments for the settlement of the principal of zero-coupon debt instruments. e Alessee classifies repayment of the principal portion (lease liability) of finance lease payments as a financing activity.
wrongWhy: The selling of plant and equipment is a cash flow from investing activities, not a cash flow from financing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The dividends are not the only item that is classified as a cash flow from financing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Investment in the stock of other corporations is a cash flow from investing activities, not a cash flow from financing activities. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. hitps:iiwww. Noe Minterhational. co imiyetoalnsietne EEO par APE BBTPO ING 330 O ad 112

1-12.q: An accountant is preparing the statement of cash flows using the indirect method. She found on the balance sheet that the prior year’s balance of net equipment was $295,700 and the current year’s balance of net equipment is $304,000. Depreciation expense during the current year was $22,400. During the year, the company sold equipment for $40,000, resulting in a gain of $21,600. On the statement of cash flows, what is the cash outflow for the purchase of equipment this year?
1-12.e: The beginning balance of net fixed assets minus the current year's depreciation recorded minus the net book value of the equipment sold plus the cost of the equipment purchased equals the ending balance of net fixed assets. Beginning balance of net fixed assets = $295,700 Current year's depreciation = $22,400 Net book value of equipment sold ($40,000 sale price - $21,600 gain) = $18,400 The unknown is the cost of the equipment purchased. Therefore, letting X = the cash outflow for purchase of equipment, the formula is: $295,700 - $22,400 — $18,400 + X = $304,000 X = $49,100
wrongWhy: The beginning balance of net fixed assets minus the current year's depreciation recorded minus the net book value of the equipment sold plus the cost of the equipment purchased equals the ending balance of net fixed assets. The cost of the equipment purchased is the unknown. This answer results from subtracting the sale price of the equipment sold instead of subtracting the net book value of the equipment sold. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The beginning balance of net fixed assets minus the current year's depreciation recorded minus the net book value of the equipment sold plus the cost of the equipment purchased equals the ending balance of net fixed assets. The cost of the equipment purchased is the unknown. This answer results from subtracting the gain on the sale of equipment instead of subtracting the net book value of the equipment sold. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The beginning balance of net fixed assets minus the current year's depreciation recorded minus the net book value of the equipment sold plus the cost of the equipment purchased equals the ending balance of net fixed assets. The cost of the equipment purchased is the unknown. This answer results from adding the current year depreciation instead of subtracting it. 1-12.q: z Kelli Company acquired land by assuming a mortgage for the full acquisition cost. This transaction should be disclosed on Kelli’s statement of cash flows as a(n)
o: Noncash financing and investing activity.
Operating activity.
Investing activity.
Financing activity.
1-12.e: The transaction should be disclosed in the statement of cash flows as a noncash financing activity and a noncash investing activity. When real estate is purchased with borrowed funds, the borrower/buyer signs the mortgage documents and the payment for the real estate is sent by the lender directly to the seller of the real estate. The cash is never recorded in the borrower’s accounts. Thus, the company acquired land, an investing activity, without any cash payment; and it became liable for a mortgage, a financing activity, without receiving any cash. Since the company did not pay or receive any cash, the two activities will not be included in the line items on the statement of cash flows. However, they must be disclosed in the statement of cash flows as noncash financing and investing activities.
wrongWhy: This is not an operating activity.
While acquiring land is an investing activity, the transaction is a noncash transaction and should be reported as a noncash financing and investing activity.
While taking on a mortgage is a financing activity, the transaction is a noncash transaction and should be reported as a noncash financing and investing activity.

The company did not declare or pay dividends for fiscal 20X0. e Sold a truck with a net book value of $7,000 for $5,000 cash, reporting a loss of $2,000. e Paid interest to bondholders of $780,000. e The cash balance was $106,000 on December 31, 20X0 and $284,000 on December 31, 20X1. The total of cash provided/used by operating activities plus cash provided/used by investing activities plus cash provided/used by financing activities is

cash provided of $284,000.

cash used of $582,000.
1-12.e: This question is made simple by the fact that the beginning and ending cash balances are given. Since the beginning cash was $106,000 and the ending balance was $284,000, we know that the cash increased by $178,000 during the year. All transactions that involved cash will be classified as either operating, investing or financing activities, so this question is actually just asking what the change in the cash balance was during the year, which is the ending cash balance minus the beginning cash balance. The correct answer cannot be calculated using the other amounts given, because not all of the transactions that affected cash have been given. The amounts given net to a cash outflow of $760,000. Since that does not reconcile to the amount of change in the cash balance (a net cash inflow of $178,000), we know that not all the transactions that affected cash are given.

1-12.q: F Royce Company had the following transactions during the fiscal year ended December 31, 20X1: e Accounts receivable decreased from $115,000 on December 31, 20X0 to $100,000 on December 31, 20x1. e Royce's board of directors declared dividends on December 31, 20X1 of $0.05 per share on the 2.8 million shares outstanding, payable to shareholders of record on January 31, 20X2. o: deduction of $15,000 in the operating section, representing the decrease in year-end accounts receivable.
source or inflow of funds of $5,000 from the sale of the truck in the financing section.

use or outflow of funds of $140,000 in the financing section, representing dividends.

1-12.q: Three years ago, James Company purchased stock in Zebra Inc. at a cost of $100,000. The stock was sold for $150,000 during the current fiscal year. The result of this transaction should be shown in the investing activities section of James’s statement of cash flows as:

The cost is relevant to calculate the gain on the sale that needs to be adjusted for in the operating activities section, but this question is about the investing activities section.

1-12.q: A company sells equipment with a book value of $50,000 for $65,000 cash. In the Scf, the Investing Activities section shows:
o: $50,000 inflow
$65,000 inflow
$15,000 inflow (the gain only)
$0 — it's in operating
1-12.e: The Full Cash Proceeds of $65,000 appear as an Investing inflow. The $15,000 gain is removed from Operating Activities (subtracted in indirect method). Never show just the book value or just the gain — always the Full Cash Received.

1-12.q: Repayment of long-term debt Principal is classified as:
o: Operating outflow
Investing outflow
Financing outflow
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Repayment of debt Principal is a Financing outflow — it reduces the financing liability. Note: Interest Payments on the debt are Operating (under GAAP). The two components of a debt payment are classified differently.

1-12.q: A company acquires $100,000 of equipment by signing a note payable. On the Scf, this is reported:
o: $100,000 investing outflow and $100,000 financing inflow
As a $100,000 investing outflow only
As a non-cash investing and financing disclosure — no cash section
Not reported at all
1-12.e: No cash changed hands — the equipment was financed entirely by a note. This Non-Cash transaction is disclosed in a Supplementary Schedule or footnote to the Scf. It does Not appear in any of the three operating/investing/financing sections.

1-12.q: Issuance of common stock for cash is classified as:
o: Operating inflow
Investing inflow
Financing inflow
Non-cash transaction
1-12.e: Issuing stock for cash is a Financing inflow — it represents receiving cash from equity investors (a financing source). Stock repurchases (treasury stock) are financing outflows.

1-12.q: The Scf requires disclosure of cash paid for interest and income taxes when using:
o: The direct method only
The indirect method for operating activities
Either method
Only if amounts are material
1-12.e: When using the Indirect Method for operating activities, companies must disclose (supplementally) actual Cash Paid for Interest and Income Taxes. The indirect method doesn't directly show these amounts, so separate disclosure is required.

1-12.q: Which of the following is an Investing activity?
o: Paying dividends to shareholders
Borrowing from a bank
Purchasing equity securities as long-term investments
Paying interest on a bond
1-12.e: Purchasing equity securities as Long-Term Investments is an Investing activity (acquiring/disposing of long-term assets and investments). Paying dividends = Financing. Borrowing = Financing. Paying interest = Operating (GAAP).

1-12.q: Purchase of land for cash is classified in the Scf as:
o: Operating activity
Financing activity
Investing activity
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Purchases and sales of Long-Term Assets (land, buildings, equipment, securities) are Investing activities. Cash paid to acquire land is an investing cash Outflow.

1-12.q: Dividends Paid to a company's own shareholders are classified as:
o: Operating activity under US GAAP
Financing activity under US GAAP
Investing activity under both GAAP and IFRS
Either operating or financing under US GAAP
1-12.e: Under US GAAP, dividends Paid are a Financing outflow (returning capital to owners). Under IFRS, companies may classify dividends paid as either financing Or operating. US GAAP = financing only.

1-12.q: A company collects the Principal on a loan it had made to another company. This is classified as:
o: Operating activity
Financing activity
Investing inflow
Non-cash activity
1-12.e: Making and collecting loans to Others is an Investing activity. Lending money = investing outflow. Collecting the principal back = investing Inflow. (Note: interest received = operating or investing depending on GAAP vs IFRS.)

1-13.q: AAA Construction Company was hired by the California state government on January 1, Year One to build a section of a new highway. The contract price was $100 million and AAA estimated that the work would cost $96 million. During Year One, $18 million was spent on the work and the company's engineers believed that work costing $72 million remained to complete the work and satisfy the performance obligation. During Year Two, another $45 million was spent and $42 million of work was estimated to remain. The contract contained a clause indicating that the state owned the work-in-process as the highway was being constructed. Using the cost-to-cost input method to calculate progress toward satisfaction of the performance obligation, what amount of profit or loss should AAA recognize on the " contract in Year Two?
1-13.e: Because the state controls the work-in-process as the work is being done, the contract is accounted for over time. In Year One, $18 million was spent with $72 million remaining for a total estimated cost of $90 million at that time. Because the contract price was $100 million, the company estimated that a profit of $10 million would eventually be earned. At the end of Year One, the performance obligation was 20% satisfied ($18 million divided by $90 million). Thus, AAA recognized a profit of $2 million (20% of $10 million) in Year One. In Year Two, another $45 million was spent, bringing the total cost incurred to date to $63 million ($18 million plus $45 million). The company's engineers estimated at the end of Year Two that another $42 million of cost remained to satisfy the performance obligation, for a total estimated cost of $105 million ($63 million plus $42 million). Because the contract price was $100 million and at the end of Year Two, the total estimated cost on the contract was $105 million, AAA estimated at that time that a loss of $5 million would eventually be incurred. https:/Avww.hBUnterda ional. cBrishystacleg/eand TEA par CUE HRAPONtING 330 Q = 112 @ 12:26 2024/8/15

$2,000,000 is the profit recognized in Year One of the contract. eee | :

1-13.q: Stander Construction Company signed a contract with the state of Ohio to build a short stretch of highway for $42 million. During 20X1, $8 million was spent and company officials anticipated that another $24 million would be needed to complete the work. During 20X2, another $13 million was spent and current information indicated that another $14 million would be required to finish the project. The contract contained a clause indicating that the state of Ohio owns the work-in-process as the highway is being constructed. Using the cost-to-cost input method to calculate Stander's progress toward satisfaction of the performance obligation, what amount of profit should the company recognize in 20X2?
o: Zero.
1-13.e: Because the state of Ohio controls the work-in-process as the work is being done, the contract is accounted for over time. In 20X1, Stander spent $8 million out of an estimated total of $32 million ($8 million spent and $24 million expected) so that the performance obligation was 25 percent satisfied. The anticipated profit was $10 million ($42 million contract price — $32 million estimated cost) so that a profit of $2.5 million was recognized (25 percent of $10 on million) in 20X1. By the end of 20X2, Stander had spent a total of $21 million ($8 million in 20X1 and $13 million in 20X2). Because $14 million more in costs was expected, the total estimated cost was $35 million ($21 million + $14 million) and the estimated profit had fallen to $7 million ($42 million — $35 million). The performance obligation was 60 percent satisfied ($21 million divided by $35 million) so profit to date of $4.2 million needed to be recognized (60 percent of the $7 million total) through 20X2. The company had recognized $2.5 million in 20X1, so another $1.7 million should be recognized in 20X2 ($4.2 million less $2.5 million).
wrongWhy: This is the profit to be recognized to date on the contract through the end of 20X2. It is not the amount of profit to be recognized for the year 20X2.

If the contract were being accounted for at a point in time, the profit recognized during 20X2 would be zero. However, because the state of Ohio controls the work-in-process as the work is being done, the contract is accounted for over time. Thus, some profit is recognized during each year of the contract.

1-13.q: AAA Construction Company was hired by the California state government on January 1, Year One to build a section of a new highway.
