In Year One, $18 million was spent with $72 million remaining for a total estimated cost of $90 million at that time. Because the contract price was $100 million, the company estimated that a profit of $10 million would eventually be earned. At the end of Year One, the performance obligation was 20% satisfied ($18 million divided by $90 million). Thus, AAA recognized a profit of $2 million (20% of $10 million) on the contract for Year One. In Year Two, another $39 million was spent, bringing the total cost incurred to date to $57 million ($18 million plus $39 million). Thus, AAA should have recognized a profit to date as of Year Two of $3 million (60% of $5 million). 1-13.q: ‘ According to ASC 606, under what circumstances should a long-term contract be accounted for over time?
o: The production process can be readily divided into definite stages.
The company's performance creates or enhances an asset that the customer controls as the work is being done.
Cash has been received from the customer.
The company's performance creates an asset with an alternative use to the company.
1-13.e: Under ASC 606, a company satisfies a performance obligation over time and recognizes the revenue and costs over time if at least one of the following three criteria is met: 1. The customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company's performance as the company is performing its obligations under the contract. 2. The company's performance creates or enhances an asset such as work in process that the customer controls as the work is being done. 3. The company's performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the company, and the company has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date.
wrongWhy: Though it may be that production on an individual long-term contract can be divided into definite stages, or that is not a criterion under ASC 606 for recognizing revenue, costs, and gross profit over time. -
Though it may be that cash has been received from the customer on an individual long-term contract, that is not a criterion under ASC 606 for recognizing revenue, costs, and gross profit over time.

Under ASC 606, a company satisfies a performance obligation over time and recognizes the revenue and costs over time if the company's performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the company, and the company has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. That is one of three criteria for recognizing revenue, costs, and gross profit over time. The other two criteria for over-time recognition are: (1) the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company's performance as the company is performing its obligations under the contract or (2) the company's performance creates or enhances an asset such as work in process that the customer controls as the work is being done. Only one of the three criteria needs to be met for over-time recognition to take place.

1-14.q: Fidler Company formerly estimated its credit loss expense as 1% of credit sales. It now uses the current expected credit loss (CECL) model to estimate its credit loss expense using an aging analysis along with other relevant information to determine what the balance in the allowance for credit losses account needs to be at each reporting date. It then records credit loss expense in an amount that will adjust the allowance account to the credit balance needed. The following accounts receivable aging schedule was prepared as of November 30 of the current year, the end of the fiscal year, and the percentages of estimated credit losses for ach account aging group have been developed using all available information, including historical experience, information about current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts that affect the collectibility of the outstanding balances. % estimated Age of Account Amount credit losses Under 60 days $730,000 1% -90 days 40,000 6% -120 days 18,000 9% Over 120 days 72,000 25% Net sales for the year were $5,250,000, of which $4,200,000 were credit sales. 1-14.e:
The required ending balance in the allowance account, based on the aging, is:
Under 60 days $730,000 x 0.01 = $ 7,300 -90 days 40,000 x 0.06 = 2,400 -120 days 18,000 x 0.09 = 1,620 Over 120 days 72,000 x 0.25 = 18,000 Required credit ending balance: $29,320 The allowance account had a debit balance of $14,000 before adjustment. To change a debit balance of $14,000 to a credit balance of $29,320, a $43,320 credit to the account will be required ($14,000 + $29,320). The corresponding debit will be to credit loss expense.
wrongWhy: This is the sum of 1% of the credit sales made during the year ($42,000, or 1% of $4,200,000), and the debit balance in the allowance account before adjustment ($14,000). However, the question says to use the information from the aging schedule to determine what the adjusting entry to the allowance account should be, according to the CECL model. The required balance in the allowance account is calculated first, using the A/R aging schedule, and the adjusting entry to the allowance account is whatever credit transaction is needed to change the balance in the allowance account, which in this question is a debit balance, to the required credit balance. The corresponding debit is to credit loss expense.

1-14.q: The current expected credit loss (CECL) model for estimating credit loss expense is required by generally accepted accounting principles in preference to the direct write-off method because it
o: "Allows" for discrepancies.
Is more flexible.
Is easier to implement.
Achieves a proper matching of expenses and revenues.
1-14.e: Under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model, an estimate of credit loss expense is recognized each period based upon a consistent method. This leads to a better matching of xpenses and revenues than under the direct write-off method. Under the direct write-off method, receivables are written off only when they are deemed to be losses. There is no matching of expenses and revenues. There is no allowance created and therefore the company can be flexible as to when credit loss expenses are recognized, which can lead to unacceptable earnings management activities. For those reasons, the direct write-off method does not conform to generally accepted accounting principles.
wrongWhy: Discrepancies can and usually do occur any time an accounting estimate is made, but that is not the reason the CECL model is required by generally accepted accounting principles.

The direct write-off method is actually more flexible than the CECL model, and that is one of the reasons why the direct write-off method does not conform to generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP). That flexibility can lead to unacceptable earnings management activities.
The direct write-off method is actually easier to implement because it requires fewer calculations and estimations than does the current expected credit loss (CECL) model. -testbank/part- 1/2

1-14.q: On the Statement of Financial Position, accounts receivable is valued at the
o: Current market value.
Amount payable when due.
Estimated net realizable value.
Original cost when the asset was acquired.
1-14.e: On the balance sheet the accounts receivable should be shown at the amount that is expected to be collected in the future. This is also called the net realizable value.
wrongWhy: On the balance sheet the receivables should be shown at the amount that the company xpects to collect in the future. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
On the balance sheet the receivables should be shown at the amount that the company xpects to collect in the future. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

For a company in the business of selling goods or services, accounts receivable are not purchased, so they are not assets that are acquired at a cost. Accounts receivable arise in the course of doing business when a customer receives a good or service that has not yet been paid for. On the balance sheet accounts receivable should be shown at the amount that the company expects to collect in the future. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. -testba:

1-14.q: Woody Company sold $150,000 of its accounts receivable without recourse. The purchaser assessed a finance charge of 5%. Woody should record
o: Acredit to accounts receivable of $150,000.
Acredit to liability on transferred accounts receivable of $150,000.
Interest expense of $7,500.
Adebit to cash of $150,000.
1-14.e: Whether receivables are sold with recourse or without recourse, the sold receivables need to be written off the books. This is done with a credit to accounts receivable for $150,000.
wrongWhy: Since the receivables were sold without recourse, Woody will have no recourse liability for credit losses.

A factor charges a factoring fee and deposits funds to the seller's account with the factor. The amount deposited to the seller's account is the amount of the receivables, reduced by the amount of the factoring fee and also reduced by any holdback to cover potential returned merchandise. If the seller of the receivables withdraws funds from the account, the seller will owe interest on the amount withdrawn for the period until the factor collects the receivables from the seller's customers. This question does not say whether the 5% "finance charge" represents interest, the factoring fee, or both. However, if it represents an interest rate, the interest will be charged only on the amount of funds disbursed, not the full amount of the receivables sold, because the full amount of the receivables sold is not available to be disbursed since the factor's fee and the holdback are deducted from the face amount of the receivables. Furthermore, interest will be charged for only the period of time until the receivables can be collected, not for a full year. -testba: 1/2 Ci MA PART 1 SECTION (A ) External Financail Reporting ***** Accounts Receivable 16 q aig

A factor charges a factoring fee and deposits funds to the seller's account with the factor. The amount deposited to the seller's account is the amount of the receivables, reduced by the amount of the factoring fee and also reduced by any holdback to cover potential returned merchandise. If the seller of the receivables withdraws funds from the account, the seller will owe interest on the amount withdrawn for the period until the factor collects the receivables from the seller's customers. Woody would not have been able to receive the full face amount of the receivables from the factor because of the deductions for the factor's fee, the holdback, and prepaid interest. Because Woody did not receive $150,000 in cash, the cash account should not be debited for $150,000. -testban| /2

1-14.q: Which of the following is true regarding the assignment (pledging as collateral) of accounts receivable and factoring of accounts receivable for a manufacturing firm?
o: The assignment of accounts receivable involves the invoice from the manufacturing firm to its customer being stamped with a notification that payment is to be made directly to the other party, whereas the factoring of accounts receivable does not.

The factoring of accounts receivable involves the invoice from the manufacturing firm to its customer being stamped with a notification that payment is to be made directly to the other party, whereas the assignment of accounts receivable does not.
The factoring of accounts receivable provides collateral for the manufacturing firm, whereas the assignment of receivables provides direct financing.
The lender has recourse to the manufacturing firm under factoring but not under the assignment of accounts receivable.
1-14.e: In factoring, the customer makes payment directly to the factor whereas in assignment, the customer makes payment to the original holder of the receivable.
wrongWhy: This answer is reversed. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
This answer is reversed.
The lender usually does not have recourse when receivables are factored. The lender usually has recourse when receivables are assigned. -testbank/part-

1-14.q: Credit loss expense must be estimated to satisfy the matching principle that expenses are recorded in the same periods as the related revenues. Under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model, to estimate the allowance for credit losses for a period, companies
o: Accrue a forward-looking estimate of expected credit loss when a financial asset is first recorded and increases or decreases in expected credit losses on xisting financial assets.
Accrue a percentage of credit sales.
Accrue a percentage of total accounts receivable.
Accrue either an amount based on a percentage of credit sales or an amount based on a percentage of accounts receivable after adjusting for any balance in the allowance for credit losses account.
1-14.e: In the process of valuing accounts receivable, companies measure expected credit losses on the basis of relevant qualitative and quantitative information about past events, current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts that could affect the future collectibility of outstanding balances. Factors considered include the quality of the company’s credit review system and credit policies, management's experience, nvironmental factors such as market conditions, and the regulatory environment.
wrongWhy: Accompany may use a percentage of credit sales as a component of the calculation of the amount to accrue as credit loss expense. However, under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model, a company measures expected credit losses on the basis of several other factors, as well, including relevant qualitative and quantitative information about past events and current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts that could affect the collectibility of outstanding balances.

A company may use a percentage of accounts receivable as a component of the calculation of the amount to accrue as credit loss expense. However, under the current xpected credit loss (CECL) model, a company measures expected credit losses on the -testbank/part- /2 basis of several other factors, as well, including relevant qualitative and quantitative information about past events and current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts that could affect the collectibility of outstanding balances.

Acompany may use a percentage of credit sales and/or an amount based on a percentage of accounts receivable as components of the calculation of the amount to accrue as credit loss expense. However, under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model, a company measures expected credit losses on the basis of several other factors, as well, including relevant qualitative and quantitative information about past events and current conditions, and reasonable and supportable forecasts that could affect the collectibility of outstanding balances. o: Estimate the net realizable value of accounts receivable.
Classify categories of workers.
Determine depreciation pools.
Estimate inventory obsolescence.

1-14.q: Accounts Receivable is reported on the Balance Sheet at:
o: Gross amount
Net Realizable Value (gross Ar minus allowance for credit losses)
Fair value
Historical cost only
1-14.e: Ar is reported at Net Realizable Value = Gross Ar minus the Allowance For Credit Losses. This represents the amount the company expects to actually collect. The allowance is a contra-asset account.

1-14.q: When a specific customer's account is Written Off as uncollectible, the journal entry is:
o: Debit Credit Loss Expense; Credit Ar
Debit Allowance for Credit Losses; Credit Ar
Debit Ar; Credit Cash
Debit Cash; Credit Ar
1-14.e: Write-off entry: Debit Allowance for Credit Losses (reducing the allowance), Credit Accounts Receivable (removing the specific bad debt). There is No income statement impact because the expense was already estimated when the allowance was set up.

1-14.q: When a previously written-off account is Recovered (customer pays), the First entry is:
o: Debit Cash; Credit Credit Loss Expense
Debit Ar; Credit Allowance for Credit Losses (reverse the write-off)
Debit Credit Loss Expense; Credit Allowance
Debit Allowance; Credit Cash
1-14.e: Recovery has Two entries: (1) Reverse the write-off: Debit Ar, Credit Allowance for Credit Losses. (2) Record the cash: Debit Cash, Credit Ar. The two-step process properly restores the Ar before recording the payment.

1-14.q: The Aging Of Ar method is considered More Accurate than the percentage-of-sales method because:
o: It is simpler to apply
It analyzes each age category separately with different collection probability rates
It is required by GAAP
It doesn't require management estimates
1-14.e: Aging of Ar applies Different Loss Rates to each age bucket (e.g., current = 1%, 31-60 days = 3%, >90 days = 20%). Older accounts have higher default rates. This account-by-account analysis is more accurate than a single percentage of total sales.

1-15.q: During inflationary periods, last-in, first-out inventory accounting will generally have a
o: lower cost of goods sold and higher inventory value.
higher cost of goods sold and higher inventory value.
lower cost of goods sold and lower inventory value.
higher cost of goods sold and lower inventory value.
1-15.e: The normal assumption for inventory calculations is that there is a period of inflation. Inflation causes prices to rise and with LIFO, the most recent purchases (most expensive) are considered to have been sold. This leads to a higher COGS. 1-15.q: The operations of the firm may be viewed as a continual series of transactions or as a series of separate ventures. The inventory valuation method that views the firm as a series of separate ventures is
o: Last-in, first-out.
Weighted average.
Specific identification.
First-in, first-out.
1-15.e: Under specific identification, each individual unit of inventory is kept separately from the others and when a sale is made, it is a specific item of inventory that is sold. This is viewing each transaction as separate and individual from others. Under other inventory methods, an assumption is made as to which item of inventory is sold and as a result the purchases and sales of inventory blend together into a larger stream of transactions. Specific identification is used for high-value items such as serialized electronic inventory or vehicles.

1-15.q: Goods shipped Fob Shipping Point are owned by the Buyer while in transit. This means they should be included in:
o: The seller's inventory
The buyer's inventory
Neither party's inventory
The shipper's inventory
1-15.e: Fob Shipping Point: title (ownership) passes to the Buyer when goods leave the seller's dock. The goods are in the Buyer'S Inventory while in transit, even though they haven't physically arrived yet.

1-15.q: In a period of Rising Prices, FIFO produces Higher Income than LIFO because:
o: FIFO assigns older (lower) costs to COGS, leaving higher costs in ending inventory
FIFO assigns newer (higher) costs to COGS
FIFO is the only GAAP-allowed method
FIFO uses average costs
1-15.e: FIFO in Rising prices: assigns the Oldest (lowest) costs to COGS → lower COGS → higher gross profit → higher net income → higher taxes. LIFO assigns the Newest (highest) costs to COGS → higher COGS → lower income → lower taxes (LIFO tax advantage).

1-15.q: LIFO Reserve = $50,000. LIFO Ending Inventory = $200,000. FIFO equivalent =
1-15.e: FIFO Equivalent = LIFO Inventory + LIFO Reserve = $200,000 + $50,000 = $250,000. The LIFO reserve represents the cumulative difference between FIFO and LIFO inventory values due to historical price changes.

1-15.q: Consigned goods (held by a consignee on behalf of the owner) should be included in:
o: The consignee's inventory
The consignor's inventory — they retain ownership
Neither party's inventory
The consignee's accounts payable
1-15.e: Consigned Goods: the Consignor retains ownership — they should be in the Consignor'S Inventory. The consignee merely holds and sells the goods on the consignor's behalf. The consignee does Not own the goods and should Not include them in their inventory.

1-15.q: Under the Periodic inventory system, COGS is calculated as:
o: At each sale transaction
Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Ending Inventory
Ending Inventory − Beginning Inventory + Sales
Only using the LIFO method
1-15.e: Periodic system: COGS = Beginning Inventory + Net Purchases − Ending Inventory. The ending inventory is determined by a physical count at period end. COGS is calculated once at period end, not at each individual sale.

1-15.q: Freight-in costs are:
o: Expensed immediately as shipping expense
Added to the cost of inventory
Deducted from sales revenue
Classified as a financing activity
1-15.e: Freight-In (cost to ship goods To the company) is a Product Cost — added to inventory cost. Freight-Out (cost to ship goods To customers) is a Period Cost — classified as a selling expense. This distinction is frequently tested.

1-16.q: Holly Company’s inventory is overstated at December 31 of this year. The result will be:
o: Understated retained earnings this year.
Understated income next year.
Understated income this year.
Understated retained earnings next year.
1-16.e: Net income next year will be understated because net income this year will be overstated. Income this year will be overstated because cost of goods sold this year will be understated. The formula for cost of goods sold is: Beginning inventory + Purchases = Cost of goods available for sale — Ending inventory = Cost of goods sold If ending inventory is overstated at the end of this year, cost of goods sold will be understated for this year and this year’s net income will be overstated. Because ending inventory is overstated at the end of this year, beginning inventory for next year will also be overstated. As a result, cost of goods sold will be overstated next year, and so net income next year will be understated. However, retained earnings will not be understated next year because the error is a self-correcting error.
wrongWhy: Retained earnings will be overstated this year if ending inventory is overstated.
Income will be overstated this year if ending inventory is overstated.
Retained earnings at the end of next year will not be misstated since the ending inventory error will have self-corrected after two years of passed. PART SERTION A. etna nancial Bgporing 390 2 158 ay

1-16.q: According to the FASB conceptual framework, which of the following attributes would not be used to measure inventory?
o: Net realizable value
Historical cost
Present value of future cash flows
Replacement cost
1-16.e: According to Concepts Statement No. 5 (2021), the various attributes used to measure items reported in the financial statements include historical cost, current (replacement) cost, current fair value, net realizable value, and the present value of future cash flows. Not all of those attributes are used for valuing inventory, though. The present value of future cash flows is not one of the ways inventory is valued, so it is the correct answer to the question. The present value of future cash flows is used to measure long-term payables and receivables. The conceptual framework is part of the accounting knowledge that all CMA candidates are assumed to have before beginning to study for the CMA exams.

o: The closing inventory of year 1 is overstated.
The income for year 2 will be overstated.
The cost of sales for year 2 will be overstated.
The income for year 1 is overstated.

In total, however, the income in the two years is correct. It is not allocated properly between the periods, though.

1-16.q: In accounting for inventories, generally accepted accounting principles require departure from the historical cost principle when the utility of inventory has fallen below cost. o: Lower of cost or market.

Lower of cost or net realizable value.

1-16.q: During the year 1 year-end physical inventory count at Tequesta Corporation, $40,000 worth of inventory was counted twice. Assuming that the year 2 year-end inventory was correct, the result of the year 1 error was that
o: Year 1 cost of goods sold was understated, and year 2 retained earnings was correct.
Year 1 cost of goods sold was overstated, and year 2 income was understated.
Year 1 income was overstated, and year 2 ending inventory was overstated.
Year 1 retained earnings was understated, and year 2 ending inventory was correct.
1-16.e: Cost of goods sold is calculated as follows: beginning inventory + purchases - ending inventory. If the year 1 ending inventory was overstated, that means that cost of goods sold would have been understated. Because the year 2 ending inventory was correctly counted, there is no misstatement to retained earnings at the end of year 2. This is because retained earnings is not closed at the end of a period and the error that resulted was simply an error in the allocation of the income between the two periods. The calculation of cost of goods sold in year 2 would lead to an overstated amount because beginning inventory was too high. The year 1 understatement and the year 2 overstatement cancel each other out leaving retained earnings correctly stated at the end of year 2.
wrongWhy: Year 1 cost of goods sold was understated. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
Year 2 ending inventory is correct, not overstated. See the correct answer for a complete xplanation.
Year 1 retained earnings would have been overstated because the error would cause Year 1 cost of goods sold to be understated. PART SERTION A. etna nancial qporin 220 Q

1-16.q: Which one of the following errors will result in the overstatement of net income?
o: Overstatement of beginning inventory
Overstatement of credit loss expense
Overstatement of amortization of leasehold improvements
Overstatement of ending inventory

wrongWhy: If beginning inventory is overstated, net income will be understated.

o: The income for last year is overstated.
The income for this year will be unaffected. :
The income for this year will be overstated.
The cost of sales for this year will be overstated. a
1-16.e: Because the inventory was written down incorrectly at the end of last year, when it is sold this year, its cost of sales will be too low, leading to an overstatement of gross profit and net income for this year.
wrongWhy: Income for last year was not overstated. Because the inventory was written down incorrectly last year, a loss was recorded incorrectly in last year’s income statement, leading to an understatement of last year’s net income. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The income for this year will not be unaffected. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The cost of sales for this year will not be overstated but rather, it will be understated. Because the inventory was written down incorrectly at the end of last year, when it is sold this year, its cost of sales will be too low. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Ci

Replacement cost $ 80,000 a Net realizable value less profit margin 85,000 u Weighted average cost 90,000 Net realizable value 100,000 Using this approach, the company should value its inventory at
1-16.e: Under IFRS, all inventory is valued at the lower of cost or net realizable value. The weighted average cost is $90,000 and the net realizable value is $100,000. The lower of those two amounts is $90,000, so $90,000 should be the inventory value reported on the company's balance sheet.

1-16.q: The following inventory valuation errors have been discovered for Knox Corporation. The year 1 year-end inventory was overstated by $23,000. The year 2 year-end inventory was understated by $61,000. The year 3 year-end inventory was understated by $17,000. The reported income before taxes for Knox was Year 1 - $138,000 Year 2 - $254,000 Year 3 - $168,000 Reported income before taxes for year 1, year 2, and year 3, respectively, should have been
1-16.e:
Though this is a three year question, we can solve it in the same manner we would a one year question. This is easiest to do if we create an example that first has numbers that the company used and then we make adjustments for the numbers that the company should have had. In this question it is a little bit more difficult because of the fact that we are given an income figure, but we can do it as follows: Year1 Year2 Year3 Beginning inventory 200 100 100 Purchases 300 600 500 Ending inventory 100 100 200 Cost of sales 400 600 400 Net income 138 254 168 Now, we can make the corrections to the ending inventory balances and see what income should have been. Year1 Year2 Year 3 Beginning inventory 200 77 161 Purchases 300 600 500
Ending inventory 77 161 217 Cost of sales 423 516 444 Net income 115 338 124 The question could also be answered by adjusting the reported income before taxes, as follows: Year 1: Income before taxes was reported as $138,000 and ending inventory was overstated by $23,000. An overstatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is understated and gross profit that is overstated, so corrected income before taxes was $138,000 — $23,000 = $115,000. Year 2: Beginning inventory was overstated by $23,000 due to the error in Year 1's ending inventory. An overstatement of beginning inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $23,000 due to the Year 1 error. Year 2's income before taxes was reported as $254,000 and ending inventory was understated by $61,000. An understatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $61,000 due to the Year 2 error. Combining both errors, the corrected income before taxes was $254,000 + $23,000 + $61,000 = i $338,000. Year 3: Beginning inventory was understated by $61,000 due to the error in Year 2's ending inventory. An understatement of beginning inventory leads to COGS that is understated and gross profit that is overstated, so income before taxes was overstated by $61,000 due to the Year 2 error. Year 3's income before tax was reported as $168,000 and ending inventory was understated by $17,000. An understatement of ending inventory leads to COGS that is overstated and gross profit that is understated, so income before taxes was understated by $17,000 due to the Year 3 error. Combining both errors, the corrected income before taxes was $168,000 — $61,000 + $17,000 = $124,000.
wrongWhy: This answer results from incorrectly treating an overstated ending inventory as causing understated gross profit in the year of the error and overstated gross profit in the following year; and treating understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit in the year of the error and understated gross profit in the following year. The correct calculations are exactly opposite (replace all the additions with subtractions and all the subtractions with additions).

This answer results from incorrectly treating Year 3's understated beginning inventory as causing understated gross profit and incorrectly treating Year 3's understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit. The correct calculations for Year 3 are the opposite: an understated beginning inventory causes overstated gross profit and an understated ending inventory causes understated gross profit.

This answer results from incorrectly treating Year 1's overstated ending inventory as causing understated gross profit. An overstated ending inventory causes overstated gross profit. The answer also results from incorrectly treating Year 3's understated ending inventory as causing overstated gross profit. An understated ending inventory causes understated gross profit. Ci

1-16.q: This information concerns items in Wilson's inventory. Cameras Lenses Tripods Historical cost per unit $210.00 $110.00 $53.00 Selling price per unit 217.00 145.00 73.75 Cost to distribute per unit 19.00 8.00 2.50 Current replacement cost per unit 203.00 109.00 51.00 Normal profit margin per unit 32.00 29.00 21.25 Assume that Wilson uses the LIFO cost flow assumption to value its inventory and determine its cost of goods sold. The amount that should be used to value the tripods is
1-16.e: Since Wilson uses the LIFO cost flow assumption, its inventory is valued at the lower of cost or market. In the calculation of the lower of cost or market, the designated market value used must fall within a range. The upper limit of the range, the ceiling, is the net realizable value of the inventory. The floor is the net realizable value minus a normal profit margin. The designated market value is the middle value of three numbers, subject to the ceiling and the floor limitations. The three numbers and the way they are calculated are: . the ceiling, or net realizable value (sales price minus the costs to complete and sell), . the current replacement cost, and . the floor (the ceiling or net realizable value minus a normal profit margin). Using the numbers given for the tripods, the net realizable value or the ceiling for the designated market value is $71.25 ($73.75 selling price minus $2.50 costs to sell), the replacement value is $51.00, and the floor is $50.00 (the net realizable value or ceiling of $71.25 minus a normal profit margin of $21.25). The middle value of these three numbers, which is the designated market value, is $51.00, the replacement cost. The designated market value is then compared to the historical cost and the lower of the two amounts is used to value the inventory. Since the designated market value ($51.00) is lower than the historical cost ($53.00), the inventory will be valued at its designated market value of $51.00.

1-16.q: A multinational company maintains its financial records under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP. Last year, the company determined its inventory with a carrying amount of $500,000 was impaired because demand for its product collapsed when a competitor launched a new product with innovative features. As a result, the company wrote down its inventory to $0. This year, however, government authorities unexpectedly announced that the competitor’s product was defective and the product was removed from the market. As a result, the company’s products were again in demand and the company estimated their net realizable value to be $750,000 at the end of the current quarter. How should the company record this new development in the current quarter?
o: Under IFRS, 750,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $0 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $500,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $0 write-up of the inventory.
Under IFRS, $750,000 write-up of the inventory; under U.S. GAAP, $750,000 write-up of the inventory.
1-16.e: Under IFRS, previous write-downs of inventory may be recovered up to the original cost of the inventory. Gains cannot be recognized on appreciated inventory, but previous losses can be reversed. Under U.S. GAAP, inventory recoveries in value may be recorded only if they occur within the same fiscal year as the write-down occurred. Inventory write-downs reported on an annual financial statement may not be restored in a later annual period. Since the recovery of value occurred in a different fiscal year from the write- down, the inventory may not be written back up.

1-16.q: Ending inventory is Overstated by $10,000. The effect on Year 1 Net Income is:
o: Net Income is understated by $10,000
Net Income is Overstated by $10,000
No effect on Net Income
Net Income is overstated by $20,000
1-16.e: If ending inventory is Overstated by $10,000: COGS = Beginning + Purchases − Ending. Higher ending inventory → Lower COGS → Higher Net Income. Net Income is Overstated by $10,000.

1-16.q: The Lcnrv rule requires inventory to be written down when:
o: Historical cost rises above market
Nrv falls Below cost — conservative principle
Nrv exceeds cost
The market price changes at all
1-16.e: Lcnrv: write inventory down to Nrv when Nrv < Cost. This applies the Conservatism principle — do not overstate assets. Inventory cannot be written Up above original cost (under GAAP). Only write-downs, not write-ups.

1-16.q: Under US GAAP, a previous inventory write-down to Nrv:
o: Can be reversed if Nrv recovers
Is Permanent — cannot be reversed
Is reversed automatically next period
Reduces the allowance for doubtful accounts
1-16.e: Under US GAAP, inventory write-downs to Nrv are Permanent — once written down, the new lower cost becomes the new carrying value and Cannot be reversed even if Nrv recovers. This differs from IFRS, which permits reversal up to original cost.

1-16.q: Nrv = Estimated Selling Price − Costs to Complete − Selling Costs. Item: Selling price $100, completion costs $15, selling costs $10. Nrv =
1-16.e: Nrv = $100 − $15 − $10 = $75. If the item cost $80, it would be written down to $75 (Nrv < cost). If cost was $70, no write-down needed (cost < Nrv).

1-16.q: Inventory errors are described as 'self-correcting' because:
o: The company corrects them voluntarily
The ending inventory of one period becomes the beginning inventory of the next, reversing the error
Auditors automatically find them
GAAP requires restatement
1-16.e: Self-Correcting: ending inventory Year 1 = beginning inventory Year 2. If Year 1 ending inventory is overstated: Year 1 NI overstated, Year 2 NI understated by the Same Amount. Over two years, Total net income is correct, but each year is misstated.

1-16.q: Under IFRS, if inventory previously written down to Nrv later recovers:
o: The recovery cannot be recognized under any circumstances
The write-down can be Reversed, up to the original cost
The recovery is recognized in OCI only
A new asset is created at the new higher value
1-16.e: IFRS allows Reversal of inventory write-downs when Nrv subsequently recovers. The reversal is limited to the Original Cost (cannot write Up above original cost). The reversal is recognized in the income statement as a reduction to COGS or a recovery item.

1-17.q: When using fair value accounting, it would be to a firm's benefit to report the liability at fair value when it has
o: $28 million in outstanding bonds trading at $98.
$25 million in putable bonds trading at $102.
$32 million in outstanding bonds trading at $101.
$50 million in variable rate preferred shares outstanding.
1-17.e: $28 million in outstanding bonds trading at $98 would be reported at 98% of $28 million using fair value accounting, or $27,440,000. It would be to the firm’s benefit to be able to report its liability at lower than nominal, or face, value.
wrongWhy: Putable bonds are bonds that give the bondholders the right to sell the bonds back to the issuer prior to the bonds' maturity date. Whether or not bonds are putable, if the bonds are trading at $102, it means that the fair value of the bonds is higher than the face value of the bonds. Because the bonds are liabilities, it is not to the firm’s benefit to report the liabilities at a higher amount.

If the bonds are trading at $101, it means that the fair value of the bonds is higher than the face value of the bonds. Because the bonds are liabilities, it is not to the firm’s benefit to report the liabilities at a higher amount.
Preferred shares are not reported as a liability. They are reported within owner’s equity.

Fair value.
Accumulated income minus accumulated dividends since acquisition.

1-17.q: MKT Corporation's assets on December 31, Year 1, include the following: |. U.S. Treasury Bills, acquired on October 15, Year 1, which mature on April 15, Year 2. MKT plans to hold the Treasury Bills until they mature because the company has no need for the cash earlier than the maturity date. ll. Shares of PF Company. PF has been very profitable and MKT Corporation plans to increase its ownership in PF as it believes PF has strong growth potential. II]. Bonds of ABC Corporation that mature in 3 years. These bonds will be sold, as needed, to meet MKT's current financing needs. Which of the above should be classified as available-for-sale securities?
o: Il and Ill only.
Ill only.
1 and Il only.
1, Il, and Ill.
1-17.e: Only debt securities may be classified as available-for-sale securities. Since the bonds of ABC Corporation will be sold as needed to meet MKT's current financing needs, those bonds are classified as available-for-sale. The U.S. Treasury Bills should be classified as held-to-maturity securities, because MKT has both the positive intent and the ability to hold them to their maturity. The shares of PF Company cannot be classified as available-for-sale securities because only debt securities are classified as available-for-sale securities. Equity securities are accounted for under the fair value method with unrealized gains and losses reported on the income statement, unless they are accounted for using the equity method or are consolidated.
wrongWhy: Debt securities are classified as trading securities, available-for-sale securities, and held-to- maturity securities. Equity securities are accounted for under the fair value method with unrealized gains and losses reported on the income statement, unless they are accounted for using the equity method or are consolidated. Since the bonds of ABC Corporation will be sold as needed to meet MKT's current financing needs, those bonds are classified as available-for-sale. However, the shares of PF Company cannot be classified as available-for-sale because they are equity securities.

1-17.q: A decline in the fair value below amortized cost of an available-for-sale investment in a debt security should
o: not be realized until the security is sold.

be evaluated for impairment and for determination of whether the unrealized loss is a credit loss, which is recognized in net income, or whether it is caused by other f factors, which is recognized in equity.
be accumulated in a valuation allowance resulting from the passage of time.
be treated as an unrealized loss and included in the equity section of the balance sheet as a separate item.
1-17.e: When the market value of an available-for-sale debt security accounted for at fair value with holding gains and losses recognized in equity declines below its amortized cost, the loss should be evaluated for impairment and for determination of whether the unrealized loss is a credit loss or whether it is caused by other factors. A credit loss is recognized in net income while a loss due to other factors is recognized in equity.

1-17.q: F Under U.S. GAAP, which one of the following statements best describes the effects on the financial statements resulting from a change in the accounting classification for a debt security from available-for- sale to held-to-maturity?
o: The balance sheet will be impacted by future unrealized gains/losses.
Net income will not be impacted by future unrealized gains/losses.
Comprehensive income will be impacted by future unrealized gains/losses.
No change will occur regarding the accounting treatment for unrealized gains/losses.
1-17.e: A debt security classified as held-to-maturity is accounted for at amortized cost, not fair value. Since it is not carried at fair value, future unrealized gains and losses will not be recorded and will not impact net income. A debt security transferred from the available-for-sale classification to the held-to-maturity classification is transferred at its amortized cost basis, plus or minus the amount of any remaining unrealized holding gain or loss reported in Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income. Past unrealized gain or loss at the date of the transfer remains in Accumulated OCI and is amortized over the remaining life of the security along with the amortization of any discount or premium as an adjustment to the security's yield. The amount of the adjustment to the security's yield will not be affected by the reclassification. Future unrealized gains or losses will not be recognized and will not impact net income. Note: In addition, any allowance for credit losses previously recorded on the security when it was accounted for as an available-for-sale debt security are reversed, and the debt security is evaluated for an allowance for credit losses as a held-to-maturity debt security.
wrongWhy: A debt security classified as held-to-maturity is accounted for at amortized cost. It is not adjusted to fair value at financial reporting dates. Since it is not adjusted to fair value, future unrealized gains and losses will not impact accumulated other comprehensive income or the balance sheet.

A debt security classified as held-to-maturity is accounted for at amortized cost. It is not adjusted to fair value at financial reporting dates. Since it is not adjusted to fair value, future unrealized gains and losses will not impact comprehensive income.

The accounting treatment for held-to-maturity debt securities is different from the accounting treatment for available-for-sale securities. A debt security classified as held-to-maturity is accounted for at amortized cost. It is not adjusted to fair value at financial reporting dates. On the other hand, a security classified as available-for-sale is reported at fair value on the balance sheet at each reporting date. revive [Save J [ En cossion https:www.hocentannakohal. comimyeteleaicme sean pan TMU OOO ng 390 O an 112

1-17.q: Unrealized gains on Trading securities are reported in:
o: OCI — other comprehensive income
Net Income on the income statement
Not reported until sold
Retained earnings directly
1-17.e: Trading securities: reported at Fair Value with all unrealized gains/losses flowing through Net Income each period. Trading securities are held for short-term profit, so all FV changes are included in current earnings.

1-17.q: Unrealized gains on Available-For-Sale (AFS) debt securities are reported in:
o: Net Income
OCI — Other Comprehensive Income (accumulated in Aoci on BS)
Not reported until sold
Retained earnings directly
1-17.e: AFS securities: reported at Fair Value, but unrealized gains/losses go to OCI (accumulated in Aoci in equity section of BS). They are not recognized in net income until the securities are Sold (reclassification adjustment at sale).

1-17.q: Which type of investment can be classified as Held-to-Maturity (HTM)?
o: Equity securities — stocks
Mutual fund investments
Debt securities only — bonds and notes
Any investment with maturity date
1-17.e: Only Debt Securities (bonds, notes with a fixed maturity date) can be classified as HTM. Equity securities (stocks) do Not have a maturity date and Cannot be classified as HTM. Under ASC 321, equity securities are always at fair value through net income.

1-17.q: HTM securities are measured at:
o: Fair value with changes in NI
Fair value with changes in OCI
Amortized cost — no unrealized G/L reported
Lower of cost or Nrv
1-17.e: HTM securities are measured at Amortized Cost. Because the company intends and is able to hold them to maturity (collecting face value at maturity), unrealized fair value fluctuations are irrelevant. No unrealized gain/loss is reported.

1-17.q: When an AFS security is Sold, the accumulated unrealized gain in Aoci is:
o: Reversed and forgotten
Reclassified from Aoci to Net Income (reclassification adjustment)
Retained in Aoci permanently
Added to retained earnings
1-17.e: When an AFS security is sold, the accumulated unrealized gain/loss in Aoci is Reclassified to Net Income via a reclassification adjustment. This prevents double-counting — the gain was already in OCI; now it moves to NI upon realization.

1-17.q: A bond purchased at a Premium means:
o: Purchase price < face value; generates a discount
Purchase price > face value; excess amortized as reduction to interest income
The bond has a high credit rating
Interest payments exceed the coupon rate
1-17.e: Bond at Premium: purchase price > face value. The premium is Amortized over the bond's life, Reducing interest income below the coupon rate each period. At maturity, the book value equals face value (premium fully amortized).

1-17.q: The Cecl model (Current Expected Credit Losses) requires:
o: Recognizing credit losses only when probable
Estimating Lifetime expected losses on held debt instruments at Acquisition — forward-looking
Only applying to trading securities
Recognizing losses only at maturity
1-17.e: Cecl (ASC 326): forward-looking model requiring estimation of Lifetime expected credit losses at the time of Acquisition (day 1). This replaces the 'incurred loss' model where losses were recognized only when probable. Cecl is more conservative and timely.

1-17.q: Trading securities are reported on the balance sheet at:
o: Amortized cost
Lower of cost or market
Fair value, with unrealized gains/losses in net income
Historical cost
1-17.e: Trading securities are always measured at Fair Value. All unrealized gains and losses flow through Net Income each period. The intent is short-term profit — so fair value changes are immediately income-relevant.

1-17.q: Interest income on Held-to-Maturity bonds is recognized using:
o: The cash basis — only when received
The straight-line method only
The effective interest method (preferred) or straight-line
Only when the bond matures
1-17.e: Interest income on HTM (and AFS) bonds is accrued using the Effective Interest Method (the theoretically correct approach). Straight-line is allowed if the difference is not material. Both are GAAP-acceptable.

1-17.q: A bond is purchased at a Premium (above par). Under the effective interest method for an HTM bond:
o: The premium is amortized, increasing interest income above the coupon
The premium is amortized, Reducing interest income below the coupon rate
The premium is carried unchanged until maturity
The premium is immediately expensed
1-17.e: When a bond is purchased at a Premium, the yield (effective rate) is Less than the coupon rate. As the premium is amortized, the carrying value Decreases toward par, and interest income recognized = carrying value × effective rate (less than cash received).

1-17.q: Under the effective interest method, amortization of a bond Discount:
o: Reduces the carrying value of the bond
Increases the carrying value toward par value
Has no effect on the carrying value
Reduces interest income below the coupon rate
1-17.e: Bond Discount amortization: carrying value Increases toward par with each period. Interest income recognized = carrying value × effective rate (Greater than cash coupon), and the difference = discount amortized (added to carrying value).

1-17.q: A company that Frequently sells HTM securities before maturity may be:
o: Allowed to continue classifying bonds as HTM
Required to reclassify all HTM securities to AFS and may be barred from HTM classification for 2 years
Fined by the FASB
Required to reclassify to trading securities immediately
1-17.e: HTM requires Positive Intent And Ability to hold to maturity. Frequent early sales Taint the portfolio — the company may be required to reclassify remaining HTM securities to AFS and may be barred from using HTM for a two-year 'tainting period.'

1-18.q: Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock at a historical cost of $300,000. Supreme Table recently reported their earnings for the prior year. Best Billiard's proportional share of Supreme Table's prior year net income was $10,000. Best Billiard also received $15,000 in dividends from Supreme Table in the prior year. Best Billiard uses the quity method as the accounting treatment for this investment. Based on the information presented, the proper presentation of this investment would result in Best Billiard reporting
o: a decrease in the book value of their investment in Supreme Table.
consolidated financial statements with Supreme Table.
their investment in Supreme Table at the original cost.
an increase in the book value of their investment in Supreme Table.
1-18.e: Equity securities where the investor exercises significant influence over the investee, whether publicly or privately held, are accounted for under the equity method. "Significant influence” is usually assumed when the investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee's voting stock. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the equity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee's income. In this case, that is given to us as $10,000. Additionally, the investment account is reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. In this question that amount is $15,000. The $10,000 increase in the investment account and the $15,000 decrease in the investment account lead to an overall decrease in the investment account of $5,000.
wrongWhy: The investment in Supreme Table does not require consolidation. "Significant influence" is usually assumed when the investor owns between 20% and 50% of the investee’s voting stock. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the quity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee’s income and reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. The events that are described in the question would not cause the book value of Best Billiard's investment in Supreme Table to increase during the period. Best Billiard Company owns 40% of Supreme Table Company's stock, so the investment is accounted for using the equity method. Under the equity method, the investment account is increased for the investor's share of the investee's income and reduced by the amount of dividends declared during the period. 10% ownership.
20% ownership.
50% ownership.

1-18.q: On January 1, Boggs, Inc. paid $700,000 for 100,000 shares of Mattly Corporation representing 30% of Mattly's outstanding common stock. The following computation was made by Boggs. Mattly reported net income for the year ended December 31 of $300,000. Mattly Corporation had paid cash dividends of $100,000 on July 1. If Boggs, Inc. exercised significant influence over Mattly Corporation and properly accounted for the long-term investment under the equity method, the amount of net investment revenue Boggs should report from its investment in Mattly would be:
1-18.e: Because the equity method is used, Boggs should report as investment income each period their percentage of Mattly's income. Since Mattly had $300,000 of income and Boggs owns % of Mattly, their share of the income is $90,000.

wrongWhy: Under the fair value method, investment income is recognized as dividends are received. This is not the amount of dividends received from Mattiy by Boggs. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

1-18.q: Under the equity method, when the Investee pays dividends, the investor records:
o: Dividend Income in Net Income
A Decrease in the Investment account (dividends = return of investment)
An increase in the Investment account
OCI — unrealized income
1-18.e: Equity Method dividends: Dividends received Decrease the Investment account. Dividends are a Return Of Investment — the investee is distributing previously accumulated earnings that were already recognized by the investor as equity income.

1-18.q: An investor owns 25% of an investee. Investee Net Income = $100,000. Investor records:
o: $100,000 investment income
$25,000 equity in investee earnings (increases Investment)
$25,000 dividend income
Nothing — 25% doesn't trigger equity method
1-18.e: Equity Method: investor records 25% × $100,000 = $25,000 as Equity In Investee Earnings in net income, And increases the Investment account by $25,000. This reflects the investor's proportionate share of the investee's performance.

1-18.q: Under ASC 321, equity securities without significant influence are reported at:
o: Cost (historical)
Amortized cost
Fair value through Net Income — no OCI option
Lower of cost or Nrv
1-18.e: Under ASC 321, equity investments are at Fair Value Through Net Income. Unlike debt securities where AFS (FV through OCI) is an option, equity securities Cannot be classified as AFS. All FV changes flow through NI (exception: Measurement Alternative for investments without readily determinable FV).

1-18.q: An investor acquires 30% of an investee for $500,000. Investee NI = $80,000. Dividends paid = $30,000. Ending Investment balance =
Consolidation is generally required when the investor has:
o: 20% ownership
Significant influence only
Control — generally more than 50% voting power
Any ownership over 10%
1-18.e: Consolidation is required when the investor has Control, which is presumed when ownership exceeds 50% of voting rights. However, control can exist with less than 50% (e.g., through contractual rights) or may not exist even with >50% in certain circumstances.

1-18.q: The equity method is required when the investor has Significant Influence, which is Presumed at:
o: 5–10% ownership
10–20% ownership
20–50% ownership
Over 50% ownership
1-18.e: Significant influence is Presumed when ownership is between 20% and 50%. Below 20% = no significant influence (FV method). Above 50% = control (consolidation). The 20–50% range triggers the equity method.

1-18.q: An investor decreases ownership from 45% to 15% by selling shares. The remaining investment should be:
o: Continued on equity method
Switched to FV method; recognize gain/loss on derecognition
Switched to consolidation
Retained at equity method cost
1-18.e: Decreasing from 20–50% to Less Than 20%: the investor loses significant influence and Switches From Equity Method to Fair Value Method. A gain/loss is recognized on the equity method investment balance at the transition date.

1-18.q: Under the equity method, the Initial investment in the investee is recorded at:
o: Fair value of the investee's identifiable net assets
The investor's proportionate share of book value
The Purchase Price (cost) paid for the investment
Par value of shares acquired
1-18.e: The equity method investment is initially recorded at Cost — the purchase price paid. Subsequently, the carrying value is adjusted for the investor's share of investee net income, dividends, and OCI.

1-18.q: Under the equity method, when the Investee reports a Net Loss, the investor:
o: Ignores the loss — only net income is shared
Records its proportionate share of the loss as a debit to equity income
Writes off the investment immediately
Records a gain equal to the loss
1-18.e: The investor records its proportionate share of the Investee'S Loss as a Debit (reduction) to Equity in Investee Income and a Credit (reduction) to the Investment account. The investor's carrying value decreases.

1-18.q: Under the equity method, if the investor's share of Losses exceeds the investment balance:
o: The investor continues recording losses indefinitely
The investor stops recording losses once the investment reaches zero (unless guarantees exist)
The investment account becomes negative
The investee must reimburse the investor
1-18.e: Under the equity method, once the investment is reduced to Zero, the investor stops recording additional losses — Unless the investor has committed to funding losses, has guaranteed the investee's debt, or has other exposure. Excess losses are disclosed but not recorded.

1-18.q: Intercompany Upstream profit (investee sells to investor) under the equity method is:
o: Recognized in full when earned by the investee
Eliminated to the extent of the investor's ownership percentage
Eliminated 100% regardless of ownership
Not recorded unless the goods are resold to outsiders
1-18.e: Under the equity method, unrealized intercompany profits are eliminated to the extent of the Investor'S Ownership Percentage. If investor owns 30%, eliminate 30% of the unrealized profit. Both upstream (investee to investor) and downstream (investor to investee) are treated similarly.

1-18.q: Under ASC 321, unrealized gains on equity securities reported at fair value flow through:
o: Other comprehensive income (Aoci)
Net income
Retained earnings directly
They are not recognized until realized
1-18.e: Post-ASU 2016-01 (effective 2018), equity securities with no significant influence are reported at Fair Value with unrealized gains And losses going through Net Income each period. The AFS category for equity securities was eliminated.

1-18.q: The Cost Method is used for equity investments when:
o: The investor owns 20-50% of the investee
Significant influence exists
Ownership is less than 20% And there is no readily determinable fair value (e.g., private company, using measurement alternative)
Consolidation is required
1-18.e: The measurement alternative (sometimes called modified cost method) under ASC 321 is available for equity securities Without a readily determinable fair value (e.g., private companies). These are measured at cost minus impairment, plus or minus observable price changes from orderly transactions.

1-18.q: Equity method investments are tested for impairment when:
o: Every year regardless of conditions
Only when the investment is sold
When there are indicators of a loss in value that is other than temporary
Quarterly under US GAAP
1-18.e: Equity method investments are reviewed for impairment when Indicators suggest a loss in value that is Other Than Temporary. If impaired, the investment is written down to fair value, with the loss recognized in income. This is different from fair value securities.

1-18.q: When an investor Sells Down from 35% to 12% ownership in an investee:
o: Continue equity method for the remaining 12%
Switch from equity method to fair value (ASC 321) for the remaining shares
Immediately consolidate the investee
No change — the equity method continues until shares reach zero
1-18.e: When ownership drops Below 20% (or significant influence is lost), the equity method is Discontinued. The remaining shares are reclassified to fair value under ASC 321, measured at fair value as of the date influence is lost. Any carrying value difference = gain or loss.

1-18.q: Joint Ventures are accounted for under US GAAP using:
o: Proportionate consolidation
Full consolidation
The equity method
The cost method
1-18.e: Under US GAAP, Joint Ventures (where the investor has joint control) are typically accounted for using the Equity Method (proportionate consolidation is Not allowed under US GAAP, unlike IFRS which permits it for jointly controlled operations).

1-19.q: MNO purchased all of XYZ's 100,000 outstanding common shares for $40 per share on August 31. On that date, XYZ's balance sheet showed total assets of $5,000,000 and total liabilities of $2,000,000. The fair value of XYZ's identifiable net assets on that date was $550,000 greater than their carrying amount. The amount that should be reported on MNO's consolidated balance sheet on August 31 for goodwill is:
1-19.e: Goodwill is the amount by which the cost of an acquisition is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired on the acquisition date. The acquisition cost is $4,000,000 (100,000 shares x $40 per share) and fair value is $3,550,000 ($5,000,000 total assets + $550,000 greater than carrying amount - $2,000,000 total liabilities). Thus, goodwill is $450,000 ($4,000,000 - $3,550,000).
wrongWhy: $550,000 is the amount by which the fair value of the identifiable net assets is greater than their carrying amount on the acquisition date. Goodwill is the amount by which the cost of an acquisition is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired on the acquisition date. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Goodwill is the amount by which the cost of an acquisition is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired on the acquisition date. The cost of the acquisition was greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired, so goodwill is not zero. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

$1,000,000 is the amount by which the acquisition cost is greater than the carrying amount (book value) of the net assets on the acquisition date ($4,000,000 acquisition cost — $3,000,000 carrying amount of net assets). Goodwill is the amount by which the cost of an acquisition is greater than the fair value of the net assets acquired on the acquisition date. 1-19.q: The purpose of consolidated financial statements is to present the financial position and the results of operations of a parent company and its subsidiaries as if the group were a single company. To accomplish this goal, the financial statements of the majority-owned subsidiaries must be
o: consolidated, if the investor company has significant influence over the operating and financial policies of an investee company, usually indicated by ownership of 20% to 50% of the outstanding voting stock.
consolidated, unless the noncontrolling interest in the subsidiary is very large.
consolidated, unless the subsidiary engages in "non-homogeneous" operations.
consolidated, if the investor company has a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary or subsidiaries. this
1-19.e: If the investor company has a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary or subsidiaries, the parent company must prepare consolidated financial statements that include the operating results of the subsidiary or subsidiaries over which it has a controlling financial interest.
wrongWhy: If an investor company has significant influence over an investee or investees, the investor company should report the investment using the equity method of accounting for investments.

1-19.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary that would require consolidation of the subsidiary’s financial statements with those of the parent?
o: Noncontrolling shareholders' substantive participating rights can prevent the majority shareholder from having a controlling financial interest.
Acontrolling financial interest is usually established by owning more than 50% of the outstanding voting shares of another entity.
Acontrolling financial interest can exist with less than a majority ownership percentage
Consolidation is required when a parent owns a majority voting interest in a subsidiary ven if a subsidiary operates under foreign restrictions that limit the parent’s control.
1-19.e: This statement is incorrect, so it is the correct answer to the question. If a majority-owned subsidiary operates under foreign restrictions, controls, or other governmentally imposed uncertainties that limit the parent's ability to control the subsidiary, the parent may not have a controlling financial interest. In that case, under the voting interest model the financial statements of the subsidiary should not be consolidated with those of the parent, even though the parent owns a majority voting interest in the subsidiary.
wrongWhy: This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest. If noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights, they can block or participate in significant financial and operating decisions, thereby preventing the majority shareholder from having a controlling financial interest.

This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest in a subsidiary that would require consolidation. The usual condition for a controlling financial interest under the voting interest model is ownership by one reporting entity of more than % of the outstanding voting shares of another entity.

This statement is a characteristic of the voting interest model for determining a controlling financial interest. A controlling financial interest can exist with less than a majority ownership percentage. Ci PART SERTION A. aetna nancial gporin 220 2 er

Cash Accounts receivable Inventory Total current assets Property, plant & equipment (net) Total assets Current liabilities Long-term debt Total liabilities Common stock Additional paid-in capital Retained earnings Total shareholders’ equity Total liabilities & shareholders’ equity Jones Corporation ___($000)_ Book Fair Value Value $ 50 $ 50 _150 _100 $300 $250 $800 $850 $150 $150 $350 $350 $150 $150 _220 $450 $800 The amount of goodwill or gain on a bargain purchase resulting from this purchase, if any, would be
o: Zero.
$100,000 goodwill.
$150,000 goodwill.
1-19.e: Goodwill or gain on a bargain purchase is the difference between the total fair value of the acquired company (the fair value of the amount paid for the controlling interest plus the fair value of the noncontrolling interest, if any) and the fair value of the acquired company's identifiable net assets (total assets minus total liabilities). If the fair value of the acquired company is greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill. If the fair value of the acquired company is less than the fair value of the net assets acquired, the difference is a gain ona bargain purchase. Because the fair value of the amount paid for the acquired company was greater than the fair value of the identifiable net assets acquired, the difference is goodwill.
