1-19.q: In the process of preparing consolidated financial statements, which one of the following items does not need to be eliminated?
o: Gain on sale of a fixed asset to a subsidiary.
Profit on inventory sold to a nonaffiliate.
Profit in beginning inventory acquired from a parent.
Dividends receivable from a subsidiary. this
1-19.e: While the profit on inventory that is sold between affiliated companies that will be consolidated needs to be eliminated, the profit that is made on inventory sold to unaffiliated companies should not be eliminated in the consolidation process.
wrongWhy: Any gain on the sale of fixed assets sold to a subsidiary needs to be eliminated in the consolidation process.
The profit on inventory that is sold between companies that will be consolidated needs to be eliminated.
Dividends receivable from a subsidiary need to be eliminated in the consolidation process. PART SEATON A. etna nancial agporing 330 Q

1-19.q: In the context of consolidated financial statements, which of the following statements about noncontrolling interest is correct?
o: Noncontrolling interest is recorded as a liability on the consolidated balance sheet.
Noncontrolling interest is presented in the equity section of the consolidated balance sheet, representing the portion of a subsidiary not owned by the parent.
Noncontrolling interest does not impact the consolidated income statement since it only affects the balance sheet.
Noncontrolling interest represents the total value of all subsidiary companies owned by the parent company. this
1-19.e: In the consolidated balance sheet, the noncontrolling interest—the portion of the subsidiary that does not belong to the parent company—is shown as a separate caption in the stockholders’ equity section.
wrongWhy: Noncontrolling interest is not recorded as a liability on the consolidated balance sheet. Instead, it is included in the equity section of the consolidated balance sheet because it represents the equity interest in a subsidiary not attributable to the parent company.

Noncontrolling interest does affect the consolidated income statement. The net income attributable to the noncontrolling interest is separately identified and deducted from the consolidated net income to show the net income attributable to the parent company only.

Noncontrolling interest represents the portion of the subsidiary's net assets that are not owned by the parent company, not the total value of all subsidiary companies. The total value of all subsidiary companies is reflected in the consolidated financial statements. PART SERTION A. Femna Enansal Bgporing 230 Q 9g

1-19.q: Which of the following statements about Variable Interest Entities (VIEs) is correct?
o: AVIE is a legal entity that is financially controlled by a single company holding the majority voting interest.
Acompany can be considered the primary beneficiary of a VIE if it only has the obligation to absorb losses of the VIE that could be significant.
The definition of a parent in a consolidation excludes a VIE's primary beneficiary unless the beneficiary holds a majority voting interest.
The primary beneficiary of a VIE is required to consolidate the VIE's financial statements with its own regardless of the amount of ownership interest held. this
1-19.e: According to ASC 810-10, the financial statements of VIEs that have a primary beneficiary must be consolidated with the financial statements of the primary beneficiary regardless of the amount of ownership held by the primary beneficiary. This means that consolidation is based on the financial contro! and obligation to absorb losses or receive benefits from the VIE, not on the ownership interest percentage.
wrongWhy: This statement is incorrect because a VIE is typically controlled by one or more companies that do not hold a majority voting interest. The essence of a VIE is that control is achieved through other financial arrangements rather than voting rights.

This statement is incorrect because being the primary beneficiary of a VIE requires both the power to direct the activities that most impact the VIE’s economic performance and the obligation to absorb losses or receive benefits that could be significant. Both conditions must be met, not just one.

This statement is incorrect because the definition of a parent in a consolidation does include a VIE’s primary beneficiary, even if the beneficiary does not hold a majority voting interest. Control over the VIE is established through financial arrangements, not through voting rights. Ci /2

1-19.q: Financial statements of an investor and an investee should be consolidated when
o: acompany is financially controlled by another company that does not hold a majority voting interest in it.
noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights.
noncontrolling shareholders collectively hold more than 50% of the voting stock of an investee.
all of the affiliated companies are in the same industry. this
1-19.e: When a company or other legal entity is financially controlled by another company even though the controlling company does not hold a majority voting interest in it, the financial statements of the two companies must be consolidated under the variable interest entity (VIE) consolidation model.
wrongWhy: If noncontrolling shareholders have substantive participating rights, they have the right to block or participate in significant financial and operating decisions made in the ordinary course of business, such as development of budgets. In that case, even if the investor company holds a majority of the outstanding voting shares, the investor company does not have a controlling financial interest and the financial statements should not be consolidated.

If noncontrolling shareholders collectively hold more than 50% of the voting stock of an investee, then the investor, with less than 50% ownership, probably does not have a controlling financial interest over the investee, and the investee’s financial statements should probably not be consolidated with the investor’s financial statements. The investment would probably be accounted for under the equity method.
Whether the affiliated companies are in the same industry or different industries is not relevant for determining whether their financial statements should be consolidated. Ci /2

1-19.q: Under the acquisition method, identifiable assets and liabilities of the acquired company are recorded at:
o: Book value of the acquiree
Historical cost from the acquiree's records
Fair Value at the acquisition date
Lower of cost or fair value
1-19.e: Under the Acquisition Method, identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed are recognized at their Fair Values at the acquisition date. This ensures the balance sheet reflects current values, not the acquiree's historical book values.

1-19.q: Company A pays $600,000 to acquire 100% of Company B. B's fair value of net assets = $450,000. Goodwill =
1-19.e: Goodwill = Purchase Price − FV of Net Assets = $600,000 − $450,000 = $150,000.

1-19.q: Goodwill recognized in a business combination is:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over its useful life (max 15 years)
Not amortized — tested for Impairment annually
Expensed immediately in the year of acquisition
1-19.e: Goodwill has an Indefinite life and is Not amortized under US GAAP (ASC 350). Instead, it is tested for Impairment at least annually. If impaired, the loss is recognized in net income and cannot be reversed.

1-19.q: In consolidated financial statements, intercompany sales and purchases are:
o: Reported separately as a line item
Eliminated — they are internal transactions within the economic entity
Added together for full disclosure
Reported in OCI
1-19.e: Intercompany sales/purchases are Eliminated on consolidation because the consolidated entity cannot buy from or sell to itself. Including them would double-count revenue and COGS. All intercompany transactions are eliminated.

1-19.q: Non-Controlling Interest (Nci) on the consolidated Balance Sheet is:
o: A liability — the parent owes the minority shareholders
Shown as a Separate Component Of Equity
An intangible asset
Not reported on the consolidated BS
1-19.e: Nci represents the equity interest of Minority shareholders (those Not controlling the company). Under US GAAP (ASC 810), Nci is presented as a Separate Component Of Shareholders' Equity in the consolidated balance sheet.

1-19.q: A 'bargain purchase' in a business combination occurs when:
o: The purchase price exceeds fair value of net assets (goodwill)
The fair value of net assets Exceeds the purchase price — a gain is recognized
The company pays exactly book value
The acquisition price is negotiated below market
1-19.e: Bargain Purchase: FV of net identifiable assets > purchase price. The 'negative goodwill' is recognized as a Gain In Net Income immediately. The acquirer re-measures all assets and liabilities before recognizing the gain to ensure accuracy.

1-19.q: After a business combination, the parent company prepares:
o: Separate financial statements for each entity only
Consolidated financial statements treating parent and subsidiary as one entity
Only the parent's statements
Two separate sets of statements
1-19.e: After a business combination, the Parent prepares Consolidated financial statements — combining the parent and all subsidiaries as One Economic Entity. This gives a complete picture of the resources and obligations under the parent's control.

1-19.q: Under the acquisition method, Direct Transaction Costs (e.g., legal and advisory fees) related to a business combination are:
o: Capitalized as part of the purchase price (goodwill)
Expensed as incurred in the period of the acquisition
Added to the fair value of acquired assets
Deducted from the consideration paid
1-19.e: Under ASC 805 (acquisition method), Direct Transaction Costs (legal, accounting, advisory fees) are Expensed As Incurred. Only the fair value of Consideration Transferred is used to measure the acquisition. This is a change from the old purchase method.

1-20.q: The value of property, plant, and equipment that is included in total assets on the statement of financial position is
o: Appraisal or market value.
Replacement cost.
Cost minus accumulated depreciation.
Acquisition cost.
1-20.e: The carrying value of property, plant and equipment, also called its net book value, is determined as the historical cost minus accumulated depreciation. The net book value of a fixed asset is the combination of the two accounts: (1) the account where the cost is originally recorded, which does not change, and (2) the account where the accumulated depreciation is recorded, which does change.
wrongWhy: Property, plant and equipment is not carried at appraisal or market value on the balance sheet.
Property, plant and equipment is not carried at replacement cost on the balance sheet.
Property, plant and equipment is not carried at acquisition cost on the balance sheet.

1-20.q: Lambert Company acquired a machine on October 1 that was placed in service on November 30. The cost of the machine was $63,000, of which $20,000 was given as a down payment. The remainder was borrowed at 12% annual interest. Additional costs included $2,500 for shipping, $4,000 for installation, $3,000 for testing, and $1,290 of interest on the borrowed funds. How much should be reported for this acquisition in the machine account on Lambert Company's statement of financial position as of November 30?
1-20.e: The amount used to record fixed assets includes all of the costs necessary to get the asset ready and available for use. This includes the cost of the asset itself, shipping, installation and testing. It does not include the interest on the borrowed funds. Per ASC 835-20-05-1, the historical cost of acquiring an asset includes all the costs necessary to bring it to the condition and location necessary for its intended use. If an asset requires a period of time to carry out the activities necessary to bring it to the condition and location necessary for its intended use, any interest cost incurred during that period as a result of money spent for the asset is a part of the historical cost of acquiring the asset. So if an asset is constructed internally over a period of several months, interest applicable to the funds used during the construction period is capitalized. However, the asset in this question was not constructed internally. It was purchased. Furthermore, the question does not say that the activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use required two months. The fact that the company did not place the asset into service immediately does not make the interest that was incurred during the 2-month delay in putting it into service capitalizable. The company may have gotten the equipment well in advance of when it planned to use it in order to allow for possible shipping and other delays so they could be sure it would be ready when planned production began. Since the question does not specifically say that two months of work was required to get the equipment ready for use, the interest should not be capitalized. Adding together all of the items to be included we get $72,500 ($63,000 + $2,500 + $4,000 + $3,000).
wrongWhy: The cost of testing the asset should also be included in the cost of the asset. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
The shipping, testing and installation should also be included in the cost of the asset. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This includes the interest on the borrowed funds. The interest on the borrowed funds should not be included in the asset cost. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. https:iiwww. NGeMnlerhational. coi yeoeasietnd EEO par APE BBTPO NG 330 O a 112

1-20.q: A steel press machine is purchased for $50,000 cash and a $100,000 interest bearing note payable. The cost to be recorded as an asset (in addition to the $150,000 purchase price) should include all of the following except
o: Freight and handling charges.
Assembly and installation costs.
Insurance while in transit.
Interest on the note payable.
1-20.e: Any cost that is incurred in order to get an asset ready and available for use should be included in the cost of that asset. Interest may be able to be capitalized as part of the cost of a fixed asset only if the company has constructed the fixed asset for its own use, because in that case, the interest may be necessary in order to get the asset ready and available for use. The situations in which interest can be capitalized on an internally constructed asset are limited by the accounting standards. Guidance in the FASB's Accounting Standards Codification® on capitalization of interest on internally constructed assets is in ASC 835-20. Since this question involves only the purchase of an asset and not internal construction of the asset, the interest cannot be capitalized. Interest that will be incurred on the note payable is a financing decision and will be recognized as interest expense when it is incurred.
wrongWhy: Any cost that is incurred in order to get an asset ready and available for use should be included in the cost of that asset. Freight and handling are costs necessary to get the asset ready and available for use.

Any cost that is incurred in order to get an asset ready and available for use should be included in the cost of that asset. Assembly and installation are costs necessary to get the asset ready and available for use.

Any cost that is incurred in order to get an asset ready and available for use should be included in the cost of that asset. Insurance while in transit is a cost necessary to get the asset ready and available for use. CC ttps:iwww he dndnakional cB Ainr yA Atad Gand BENE perl AAA) RgPOting 330 Q ane 112

1-20.q: Which cost is Capitalized as part of Pp&E?
o: Training employees to use the new machine
Installation costs to bring the machine to working condition
Administrative overhead allocated to the project
Marketing costs for new product made by the machine
1-20.e: Installation Costs that are necessary to bring the asset to its Intended Use are Capitalized as part of Pp&E cost. Training, admin overhead, and marketing are Period Costs expensed when incurred.

1-20.q: Land is Not depreciated because:
o: It is too expensive to track
Land has an Indefinite useful life — it does not wear out or become obsolete
GAAP does not allow it
It is always sold within one year
1-20.e: Land has an Indefinite useful life — it does not wear out, become obsolete, or be consumed. Only assets with Finite useful lives are depreciated. Land improvements (parking lots, fencing) Are depreciated because they have limited lives.

1-20.q: Interest incurred during the Self-Construction of an asset is:
o: Always expensed immediately
Capitalized — added to the cost of the asset during construction
Recorded as a deferred charge
Recorded in OCI
1-20.e: Capitalized Interest: when constructing an asset for own use, interest on related borrowings is Capitalized (added to asset cost) during the construction period. Once the asset is ready for use, interest is expensed as incurred.

1-20.q: Net Book Value (Nbv) of a fixed asset =
o: Historical Cost only
Historical Cost − Accumulated Depreciation
Fair Value at reporting date
Replacement Cost
1-20.e: Net Book Value (carrying value) = Historical Cost − Accumulated Depreciation. Nbv represents the unrecovered cost of the asset remaining on the balance sheet. It decreases each period as depreciation is recorded.

1-20.q: An Asset Retirement Obligation (Aro) relates to:
o: Annual maintenance costs
Estimated Future costs to dismantle/restore an asset — capitalized into Pp&E at inception
Tax deductions for depreciation
Financing costs for the asset
1-20.e: Aro: the estimated cost to Dismantle, Remove, and Restore a site is recognized as a Liability (Aro) and Capitalized into the Pp&E cost when the obligation arises. The asset (and obligation) are then recognized — the obligation is accreted over time.

1-20.q: Repair and maintenance costs on existing equipment are:
o: Capitalized as a betterment
Added to the cost of the asset
Expensed in the period incurred
Deferred and amortized over the asset's remaining life
1-20.e: Routine repair and maintenance costs (oil changes, cleaning, minor fixes) are Period Costs — expensed when incurred. Only Betterments (improvements that extend useful life, add capacity, or improve quality beyond original specifications) are capitalized.

1-20.q: The Cost of Pp&E includes all costs necessary to bring the asset to its intended location and condition. Which of the following is capitalized?
o: Insurance on the asset After it is placed in service
Cost of training employees to use the new machine
Freight and installation costs to set up the machine
Repair of existing equipment
1-20.e: Capitalize all costs to Acquire and Prepare the asset: purchase price, freight, installation, testing, and site preparation. Exclude: insurance after placed in service (operating expense), employee training (expense), repairs of existing assets.

1-20.q: A Lump-Sum (basket) purchase of land, building, and equipment for $900,000 when the appraised values are land $300,000, building $400,000, equipment $300,000 (total $1,000,000). The cost allocated to land is:
When Similar productive assets are exchanged and the exchange Lacks commercial substance:
o: Recognize the full gain on the exchange
Record the new asset at fair value of asset given up
Record no gain (defer it) — new asset = book value of old + cash paid
Recognize gain or loss based on book value difference
1-20.e: Exchange Without commercial substance: No Gain recognized (gain deferred — reduces basis of new asset). New asset = Nbv of old asset + cash paid (boot). Loss is always recognized immediately, even without commercial substance.

1-20.q: When Dissimilar assets (or exchanges With commercial substance) are exchanged:
o: No gain or loss is recognized
Record new asset at book value of old asset
Recognize full gain or loss — record new asset at fair value of asset given or received
Record at the lower of fair value or book value
1-20.e: Exchange With commercial substance: recognize Full Gain Or Loss. Record the new asset at Fair Value (of asset given up or received, whichever is more reliable). This reflects the economic substance of the exchange.

1-20.q: Under US GAAP, Government Grants received to acquire Pp&E are:
o: Recognized as revenue immediately
Deducted from the cost of the asset (reduces capitalized cost) Or deferred as unearned income and recognized over asset's life
Always treated as equity contributions
Expensed in the period received
1-20.e: US GAAP (ASC 958 for NFPs; general guidance for for-profit entities) generally allows either: reduce the Asset'S Cost by the grant, or record as Deferred Income amortized over the asset's life. Under IFRS (Ias 20), the same two treatments are explicitly permitted.

1-20.q: A Betterment (capital improvement) to an existing asset is:
o: Expensed immediately as a repair
Capitalized — it extends useful life or improves functionality beyond original specifications
Disclosed in notes only
Amortized separately over 5 years regardless of asset life
1-20.e: Betterments (improvements, additions, replacements that Enhance the asset beyond its original capability or extend its useful life) are Capitalized. Ordinary repairs and maintenance that merely Maintain the asset = Expensed.

1-20.q: Under IFRS, Component Depreciation requires:
o: Depreciating the entire asset as a single unit
Identifying significant components with different useful lives and depreciating each separately
Applying a single depreciation rate to all assets
Revaluing components annually to fair value
1-20.e: IFRS (Ias 16) Requires component depreciation for assets with significant components having Different useful lives (e.g., aircraft engines vs. fuselage). US GAAP permits but does not require it. Each component has its own depreciation schedule.

1-20.q: Under the Revaluation Model (IFRS only), when Pp&E is revalued Upward above original cost:
o: The increase is recognized in net income
The increase goes to Other Comprehensive Income (Revaluation Surplus in equity)
The asset is written up and a gain recognized immediately
Depreciation stops after revaluation
1-20.e: Under IFRS revaluation model: Upward revaluations above original cost → credit to Revaluation Surplus in Other Comprehensive Income (equity). Only revaluation Reversals of previous downward revaluations can go to income. US GAAP does Not allow upward revaluation.

1-20.q: For a Self-Constructed asset, overhead is:
o: Never allocated — only direct costs are capitalized
Allocated to the asset using a reasonable method (e.g., proportionate share of actual overhead)
Expensed as incurred regardless of capitalization
Added only if the overhead is directly traceable to construction
1-20.e: Self-constructed assets are capitalized at the Full Cost of construction: direct materials, direct labor, And a Reasonable Allocation of overhead. Both incremental overhead and a share of fixed overhead may be allocated. The amount capitalized should not exceed fair value.

1-20.q: Pp&E acquired by issuing the company's Own Stock is recorded at:
o: Par value of the stock issued
Book value per share × shares issued
Fair value of the stock issued (or fair value of the asset received, whichever is more reliably measurable)
The most conservative (lower) of the two fair values
1-20.e: Non-cash asset acquisitions are recorded at Fair Value — whichever is more reliably measurable: fair value of the Stock Issued or fair value of the Asset Received. Par value is irrelevant for this measurement.

1-20.q: Land Improvements (e.g., parking lots, fencing, landscaping) differ from Land because:
o: Land improvements are not capitalized — they are expensed
Land improvements are Depreciable — they have limited lives; land itself has an indefinite life
Land improvements are classified as intangible assets
Land and land improvements are depreciated together
1-20.e: Land has an Indefinite useful life — never depreciated. Land Improvements (parking lots, fences, drainage, paving) have Finite lives and Are depreciated. This is a classic exam distinction.

1-21.q: Nella Corporation computes depreciation to the nearest whole month. A new piece of quipment was placed in operation on July 1, 20X1. It was expected to produce 400,000 units of product during its estimated useful life of eight years. Total cost was $300,000; salvage value was estimated to be $30,000. Nella employs a calendar year for financial reporting purposes. Actual production for the period of July 1 through December 31, 20X1 was 34,000 units. If Nella had used the units-of-production method of depreciation, the amount of depreciation computed for this equipment for book purposes in 20X1 would have been
1-21.e: Under the units-of-production method the depreciable amount (original cost - salvage value) is multiplied by the percentage of the total expected output that was produced during the period. In this question the depreciable amount is $270,000 ($300,000 cost — $30,000 salvage value) and the percentage of production done this period was 8.5% (34,000 units out of an expected ). Therefore, the depreciation expense for 20X1 was $22,950 ($270,000 = 0.085).
wrongWhy: This answer fails to take into account the salvage value when calculating the depreciable amount. Also, this answer takes only 1/2 of the first years depreciation. 1-21.q: Nella Corporation computes depreciation to the nearest whole month. A new piece of equipment was placed in operation on July 1, 20X71. 1-21.e: Under the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, the depreciation expense is calculated as the depreciable amount (original cost - salvage value) multiplied by a fraction. For the amount of annual depreciation, the numerator of the fraction is the number of years remaining in the asset's useful life, and the denominator is the sum of all of its xpected years of life. For an asset with an 8-year life, the denominatoris8+7+6+5+4+3+2+1=36. Since Nella calculates depreciation to the nearest whole month and this asset was purchased on July 1, the amount of depreciation in the first year (20X1) will be only half of a year's depreciation. Therefore, in 20X1 the fraction will be 4/36. This is the same as taking half of the first year's depreciation in 20X1, or 0.5 x 8/36. In 20X2 it will be 7.5/36. This fraction is multiplied by the depreciable amount, which is the cost minus the salvage value. 1-21.q: Baby Products Ltd. does its financial reporting under IFRS. You have recently been hired as a Certified Management Accountant for the firm. The CFO asks you to assist in determining the correct amount of Other Comprehensive Income based on the following line items: Foreign currency translation loss €25,000 Unrealized gain on available-for-sale debt securities 32,000 Foreign currency remeasurement gain 6,000 Revaluation increase on fixed assets 12,000 Unrealized loss on trading debt securities 4,000 Revaluation loss on intangible assets 15,000 Deferred pension gain 23,000 Deferred gain on a cash flow hedge 11,000
1-21.e: Comprehensive Income includes everything on the Profit or Loss Statement plus some specific items that do not appear on the Profit or Loss Statement that are called “Other Comprehensive Income” and are recognized in Equity. Under IFRS, an item of property, plant and equipment whose fair value can be measured reliably is carried at a revalued amount equal to its fair value at the date of the revaluation less any subsequent accumulated depreciation and subsequent accumulated impairment losses. Revaluation increases are recognized as Other Comprehensive Income unless they reverse a previous revaluation decrease. (Revaluation decreases are recognized in Profit or Loss unless they reverse a previous revaluation increase.) Under IFRS, Other Comprehensive Income includes the following items for Baby Products Ltd.: Foreign currency translation loss €(25,000) Unrealized gain on available-for-sale debt securities 32,000 R ial § 49-900 Ci /2 Deferred gain on a cash flow hedge 11,000 Other Comprehensive Income € 53,000 The following items are not reported as Other Comprehensive Income because they are recognized in Profit or Loss: Foreign currency remeasurement gain Unrealized loss on trading debt securities Revaluation loss on intangible assets Note the difference between foreign currency translation gains and losses and foreign currency remeasurement gains and losses. Foreign currency translation gains and losses are reported in Other Comprehensive Income, whereas foreign currency remeasurement gains and losses are reported in Profit or Loss.
wrongWhy: This is the net of all of the transactions represented by the line items given in the question. However, three of the items should not be included because they are reported in Profit or Loss, not as Other Comprehensive Income: the foreign currency remeasurement gain, the unrealized loss on trading debt securities, and the revaluation loss on intangible assets. Note the difference between foreign currency translation gains and losses and foreign currency remeasurement gains and losses. Under IFRS, revaluation increases of fixed assets are permitted (unlike under US GAAP, which does not permit revaluation increases of fixed assets). Under IFRS, revaluation increases are reported as Other Comprehensive Income.

This is the foreign currency translation loss, the unrealized gain on AFS debt securities, the revaluation increase on fixed assets, the revaluation loss on intangible assets, the deferred pension gain, and the deferred gain on a cash flow hedge. 1-21.q: Maple Industries purchased a lathe on June 1, Year 1, the beginning of the fiscal year. Its second full year of ownership was for the fiscal year from June 1, Year 2 through May 31, Year 3. In order to answer this question we will need to calculate the depreciation expense in the second year under the double declining balance method for the lathe. Under double declining depreciation, the depreciation expense is calculated as follows: (the book value of the asset at the beginning of the period = twice the straight-line percentage). In this question the lathe has an eight year useful life. With an eight year useful life the depreciation under the straight-line method would be 12.5% per year. This means that we will use 25% for the depreciation calculation. Also, remember that this method uses the beginning book value of the asset. Therefore, in order to calculate the depreciation in the second year, we will also need to calculate the depreciation expense for the first year for the lathe. Remember also that the cost of the asset includes all of the costs necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use. In the first year the depreciation expense was $10,800 ($43,200 x 0.25), which reduced the carrying value to $32,400 for year 2. In year 2, the depreciation expense was $8,100

1-21.q: Aston Company acquired a new machine at a cost of $200,000 and incurred costs of $2,000 to have the machine shipped to its factory. Aston also paid $4,500 to construct and prepare a site for the new machine and $3,500 to install the necessary electrical connections. Therefore, Aston should include the $200,000 cost, the $2,00 in shipping, the $4,500 in construction of a new site and $3,500 in electrical connections. This totals $210,000. Under the double declining balance method depreciation is calculated as the book value of the asset multiplied by twice what the straight-line percentage would be. The asset has a useful life of 5 years so under the straight-line method depreciation would be 20% per year. Twice this is 40% so Aston should take % of the book value of the asset as depreciation expense. The book value was $210,000 for the first year and 40% of this is $84,000. Salvage value is used only at the end to make sure that the asset is not depreciated below the salvage value.

1-21.q: Anewly acquired plant asset is to be depreciated over its useful life. The best rationale for this process is the
o: Matching principle.
Monetary unit assumption.
Materiality requirement.
Revenue recognition principle..
1-21.e: According to the FASB's Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts No. 8 (2021), Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting, Chapter 4, Elements of Financial Statements, expenses are outflows or other using up of assets of an entity or incurrences of its liabilities (or a combination of both) from delivering or producing goods, rendering services, or carrying out other activities. Therefore, the matching principle states that expenses should be recognized not when payment is made but when the work for which the payment is made contributes to revenue. Depreciation is the systematic and rational allocation of the cost of an asset when conomic benefits are expected to arise over several accounting periods and the association with income can be only indirectly determined. It is a process of trying to match the revenues that the asset generates with the expense of the asset.
wrongWhy: The monetary unit assumption is not the rationale for depreciating an asset over its useful life. Money serves as the standard unit of measurement to measure economic activity and the transactions a company undertakes. For a given country, the currency in use in that country is the monetary unit assumption. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Activity method based on units of production..

1-21.e: Under the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, the depreciation expense is calculated as the depreciable amount (original cost - salvage value) multiplied by a fraction. 1-21.e: Under IFRS, the amount of an impairment loss is the difference between the carrying amount of the asset and the recoverable amount. The recoverable amount is the higher of (1) the fair value of the asset if sold minus the cost to sell, and (2) the asset's value in use, which is the present value of the future cash flows to be generated by the asset in use, including its residual value. The net selling price, or the fair value if sold less costs to sell, is £12,000. The recoverable amount is the greater of £15,000 or £12,000, so the recoverable amount is £15,000. The impairment loss—the difference between the carrying value of the £20,000 and the recoverable amount of £15,000—is £5,000.

1-22.q: How does IFRS differ from U.S. GAAP with respect to accounting for development costs?
o: U.S. GAAP treats development costs as part of goodwill, whereas IFRS treats these costs as an intangible asset.
U.S. GAAP requires capitalization of development costs, whereas IFRS makes capitalization of these costs optional.
U.S. GAAP requires expensing of all development costs and IFRS requires capitalizing all development costs.

U.S. GAAP does not allow capitalization of development costs unless they are for materials, equipment, or facilities that have alternative future uses. IFRS allows capitalization of development costs but only if technical feasibility has been stablished.
1-22.e: Under U.S. GAAP, research and development costs are expensed as incurred, per ASC 730- -25-1. Development costs may not be capitalized unless they are for materials, equipment, or facilities that have alternative future uses, for R&D or otherwise, according to ASC 730-10-25-2. IFRS does permit capitalization of development costs, but only if technical feasibility has been stablished. IAS 38.57, specifies that development costs may be recognized as an intangible asset if and only if an entity can demonstrate all of the following: (a) technical feasibility of completing the asset so it will be available for use or sale; (b) its intention to complete the asset and use or sell it; (c) its ability to use or sell the intangible asset; (d) how the intangible asset will generate probable future economic benefits; (e) the availability of adequate technical, financial and other resources to complete the development and to use or sell the intangible asset; (f) its ability to measure reliably the expenditure attributable to the intangible asset during its development.
wrongWhy: U.S. GAAP does not treat development costs as part of goodwill, and IFRS does not treat these costs as an intangible asset except under very specific circumstances.
U.S. GAAP does not require capitalization of development costs, and IFRS does not make capitalization of these costs optional.
IFRS does not require capitalizing all development costs. Ci /2

1-22.q: Pie Baker, Ltd. purchased a secret fruit pie recipe for $75,000. An additional $10,000 was spent in securing the secret recipe and safeguarding its contents. Pie Baker expects to keep the recipe a secret indefinitely. Because of taste changes, the industry has found that recipes have been used for an average of 8 years. Based on this information, Pie Baker should
o: Capitalize the $85,000 cost and then amortize it over the period the recipe is to remain a secret.
Capitalize the $85,000 cost and amortize it over 8 years.
Expense the $85,000 cost because the secret formula cost should not be capitalized.
Capitalize the $85,000 cost and then amortize it over 40 years.
1-22.e: This is an example of a process patent, since it is a trade secret. If a patent is purchased from an inventor or other owner, the purchase price represents its cost and the cost is to be capitalized. Other costs incurred in connection with securing a patent, attorney's fees and other unrecovered costs of a successful legal suit to protect the patent, can be capitalized as part of the patent cost. However, research and development costs related to the development of the product, process, or idea that is subsequently patented must be expensed as incurred. The cost of a patent should be amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. In this case, the expected useful life of the recipe is 8 years. The fact that the recipe will be kept a secret indefinitely is irrelevant. Therefore, the cost of the recipe, $85,000, should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years.
wrongWhy: This is an example of a process patent. If a patent is purchased from its original developer, the cost of the patent should be capitalized and amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. Thus, the purchase price should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years. The length of time the recipe is to remain a secret is irrelevant.

This is an example of a process patent. If a patent is purchased from its original developer, the cost of the patent should be capitalized and amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. Thus, the purchase price should be capitalized and amortized over a period of 8 years.

The maximum amortization period for a patent (and a secret recipe is a process patent) is 20 years, and that would apply only if the patent were owned from the date it was granted and was expected to be useful for its maximum legal life of 20 years. In this case the patent (i.e., recipe) has been purchased from the original developer. Furthermore, it is expected to have a useful life of only 8 years. The cost of the patent should be amortized over its legal life or its useful life, whichever is shorter. In this case, that is 8 years. Ci /2

1-22.q: An employee of M, a public company, developed a new product that has just been patented. The development costs of this product were negligible, but the patent rights are almost certainly worth many millions of dollars. Which accounting concept would prevent the company from recognizing the value of this patent as a non-current asset in its balance sheet?
o: Going concern
Materiality
Historical cost
Conservatism
1-22.e: Because this was an internally generated asset, there is no transaction with which to measure the true value of the patent. In this case, the development costs were almost zero. But even if the development costs had been substantial, they would not have been capitalized as an asset on the balance sheet. A patent is an intangible asset, and the only costs of patents that can be capitalized as assets on the balance sheet are costs incurred in connection with securing the patent such as registration fees and attorney's fees for filing the patent application. Research and development costs related to the development of a product, process, or idea that is subsequently patented must be xpensed as incurred.
wrongWhy: The going concern concept says that a company's balance sheet must reflect the value of that company assuming it will remain in existence for, and beyond, the foreseeable future. The going concern concept would not prevent the company from recognizing the value of the patent as a non- current asset in its balance sheet.

Materiality is a constraint that accounting is subject to. An item of information is material and should be reported if it is judged to be significant enough that it would have an effect on a decision maker. Whether or not an item of information is material depends upon the relative size of the item, the precision with which it can be estimated, and the nature of the item. Materiality would not prevent the company from recognizing the value of the patent as a non-current asset in its balance sheet.

The conservatism concept means that when in doubt, choose the solution that will be least likely to overstate assets and income. However, there would be no cause for doubt in the decision of whether or not to recognize the value of the patent as a non-current asset in the company's balance sheet. Ci /2

1-22.q: Which one of the following statements is correct about the reconciliation of U.S. GAAP and International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)?
o: Internally generated goodwill may not be capitalized under U.S. GAAP, but it may be capitalized under IFRS.
The costs of research must be expensed under U.S. GAAP but are capitalized under IFRS if they meet specific criteria.
AIl costs of research and development must be expensed under both U.S. GAAP and IFRS.
The costs of development must be expensed under U.S. GAAP but are capitalized under IFRS if they meet specific criteria.
1-22.e: Costs of internal development must be expensed as incurred under U.S. GAAP. Under IFRS, internal development expenditures are capitalized if technical and economic feasibility of a project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria, including intent to complete the asset and ability to sell the asset in the future.
wrongWhy: The cost of internally-generated intangible assets are not recorded on the balance sheet under ither standard.

This statement is not true. Both U.S. GAAP and IFRS require research costs to be expensed as incurred. However, note that development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. Under U.S. GAAP, development costs are generally expensed as incurred. Under IFRS, development costs are capitalized when the technological and economic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated.

Internal costs related to the research phase of research and development are expensed as incurred under both standards. However, development costs are accounted for differently under the two standards. https:thwmw. ton A Pe Sana, Fdemal Financial Reporting 330Q 148 1/2

1-22.q: All of the following are specifically identifiable intangible assets except
o: Leaseholds.
Copyrights.
Patents and trademarks.
Goodwill.
1-22.e: Goodwill is not a specifically identifiable intangible asset. Rather, it is an unidentifiable intangible asset because it is not known specifically what the asset arises from.
wrongWhy: Leasehold improvements are specifically identifiable intangible assets.
Acopyright is a specifically identifiable intangible asset.
Patents and trademarks are specifically identifiable intangible assets. Ci

1-22.q: According to ASC 730-10-25-2, Research and Development-Elements of Costs to Be Identified with Research and Development Activities, expenditures for equipment for research and development
o: must be expensed in the period incurred, unless the equipment has alternative uses in other R&D projects or otherwise.
may be expensed in the period incurred or capitalized if the probability of future benefits can readily be determined.
must be capitalized in the period incurred and amortized over the estimated life of the asset.
must be expensed in the period incurred, unless the costs are for testing a prototype.
1-22.e: Research and development costs are usually expensed as they are incurred. However, if the quipment will have an alternative use on other R&D projects or otherwise, the asset may be capitalized and depreciated. When it is used in R&D projects, the depreciation expense should be recorded as R&D expense and not as depreciation expense.
wrongWhy: R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise. The determination of future benefits is not the criterion used to determine if the asset may be depreciated.

R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise.

R&D costs are usually expensed in the period in which they are incurred. Capitalization and depreciation may take place only if the asset has an alternative use on R&D projects other than the R&D project for which it was originally acquired or otherwise. Whether the costs are for testing a prototype is not the criterion used for determining whether the asset may be capitalized. Ci

1-22.q: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the accounting treatment of research and development costs (R&D) under U.S. GAAP and IFRS?
o: U.S. GAAP requires R&D to be expensed, and IFRS requires research costs to be xpensed but allows for the capitalization of the development costs.
Neither U.S. GAAP nor IFRS allow for costs of R&D to be capitalized.
U.S. GAAP allows for the capitalization of the costs of R&D, and IFRS requires R&D costs to be expensed.
Both U.S. GAAP and IFRS allow for costs of R&D to be capitalized.
1-22.e: The accounting treatment for research costs is similar in U.S. GAAP and IFRS: both sets of standards require research expenses to be expensed. However, development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. Under U.S. GAAP, internal development costs are usually expensed as incurred. They may be capitalized if and only if a specific U.S. GAAP standard allows capitalization for that asset. Under IFRS, internal development costs may be capitalized under certain circumstances when the technological and conomic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated.
wrongWhy: It is true that neither U.S. GAAP nor IFRS allows for research costs to be capitalized. However development costs are treated differently under the two sets of standards. U.S. GAAP requires development costs to be expensed as incurred, but IFRS does allow for development costs to be capitalized under certain circumstances. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

U.S. GAAP does not allow for the capitalization of the costs of either research or development. IFRS requires research costs to be expensed, but under IFRS, development costs may be capitalized if technical and economic feasibility of a project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

U.S. GAAP does not allow for costs of either research or development to be capitalized. IFRS does not allow research costs to be capitalized, although development costs may be capitalized under IFRS when the technological and economic feasibility of the project can be demonstrated in accordance with specific criteria. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information. Ci /2

1-22.q: Under US GAAP, Research & Development (R&D) costs are:
o: Always capitalized as intangible assets
Expensed as incurred — no capitalization allowed
Capitalized only if the project succeeds
Deferred until commercial production begins
1-22.e: Under GAAP (ASC 730), All R&D costs are Expensed as incurred — no capitalization is allowed except in specific situations (software development after technological feasibility, in-process R&D in a business combination). This is a key difference from IFRS.

1-22.q: Goodwill arising from a business combination is:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over its estimated useful life
Not amortized — tested for impairment Annually
Expensed immediately at acquisition
1-22.e: Goodwill has an Indefinite life and is Not amortized (public companies). Instead, it is tested for Impairment at least annually. Private companies may elect to amortize goodwill over 10 years (simplified method under ASU 2014-02).

1-22.q: A patent with a legal life of 20 years and estimated economic useful life of 8 years is amortized over:
o: 20 years
8 years
14 years (average)
Not amortized — indefinite life
1-22.e: A limited-life intangible is amortized over its Useful Life, which is the Shorter of its legal life and economic life. Patent: shorter of 20 years (legal) and 8 years (economic) = 8 years.

1-22.q: Internally developed trademarks and brand names are:
o: Capitalized at the amount spent developing them
Recognized at fair value annually
Not capitalized under GAAP — internally generated intangibles generally expensed
Amortized over 20 years
1-22.e: Internally Generated intangibles (brand names, mastheads, customer lists, internally developed goodwill) are Not recognized as assets under GAAP — the costs are expensed. Only Purchased intangibles and those acquired in business combinations are capitalized.

1-22.q: Under IFRS, development phase costs are:
o: Always expensed — same as GAAP
Capitalized when criteria for feasibility are met (technical feasibility, intent to complete, ability to sell/use, future economic benefits)
Capitalized only if government approved
Treated identically to research costs
1-22.e: IFRS: research phase = expensed. Development phase = Capitalized when specific criteria are met (ASC vs IFRS key difference). GAAP expenses all R&D. Under IFRS, once technical feasibility is established and other criteria met, development costs must be capitalized.

1-22.q: Goodwill impairment under ASU 2017-04 is measured as:
o: The excess of book value of the reporting unit over its fair value
The excess of goodwill over its replacement cost
Calculated only using a separate test for identifiable assets
Reversed in subsequent periods if value recovers
1-22.e: ASU 2017-04 (simplified goodwill impairment test): Impairment loss = carrying value of the reporting unit (including goodwill) Minus its fair value. If carrying value > FV → impairment loss = the difference (limited to carrying amount of goodwill). No reversal permitted.

1-22.q: An intangible with Indefinite useful life is:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over its legal life
Not amortized — tested for impairment annually
Amortized over 10 years
1-22.e: Indefinite-lived intangibles (goodwill, certain trademarks) are Not amortized. Tested for Impairment at least annually. Finite-lived intangibles are amortized over useful life.

1-22.q: Computer software for External Sale — costs after Technological Feasibility is established are:
o: Expensed as R&D
Capitalized and amortized
Same as internal-use software
Written off at product launch
1-22.e: Fas 86 (ASC 985-20): before technological feasibility = Expense. After feasibility until release = Capitalize. Amortize using the greater of straight-line or ratio of current to total expected revenue.

1-22.q: A Franchise Right with a 10-year contract life is:
o: Not amortized
Amortized over 10 years
Capitalized indefinitely
Expensed at purchase
1-22.e: Franchise right with specific contract period = finite-lived intangible. Amortize over the lesser of useful life or contract period (10 years). If indefinitely renewable at minimal cost, may be indefinite-lived.

1-22.q: A customer list acquired in a business combination is:
o: Expensed — customers are not owned
Capitalized as finite-lived intangible and amortized
Added to goodwill
Indefinite-lived
1-22.e: Acquired customer lists = separately identifiable intangibles under ASC 805. Capitalized at fair value as Finite-Lived and amortized over estimated useful life (expected customer retention period).

1-22.q: A copyright with 70-year legal life and 10-year economic life is amortized over:
o: 70 years
10 years
40 years
Longer of legal or economic life
1-22.e: Amortize over Useful Life = shorter of legal or economic life. Economic useful life (10 years) is shorter — amortize over 10 years.

1-22.q: An indefinite-lived intangible (other than goodwill) is impaired when:
o: Carrying value exceeds replacement cost
Carrying value exceeds Fair Value
Held for more than 10 years
Annual testing shows any decline
1-22.e: Compare Carrying Value to Fair Value directly. If Cv > FV → impairment loss = Cv − FV. Similar to simplified goodwill test.

1-23.q: Suppose that a company pays one of its liabilities twice during the year, in error. What are the effects of this mistake?
o: Assets, net income, and owners' equity will be unaffected.
Assets and liabilities will be understated.
Assets and net income and owners' equity will be understated, and liabilities are overstated.
Assets, liabilities, and owners' equity will be understated.
1-23.e: The journal entry to record this event is a debit to a liability and a credit to cash. If this entry is made a second time the liabilities and cash will be understated, but there will be no impact on income or owners’ equity.
wrongWhy: Assets will be affected by this error. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
Liabilities will be understated and net income and owners' equity will not be affected by this error.
Owners' equity will not be affected by this error. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

1-23.q: A company has $100 million of debt that is due in March Year 3. In December Year 2, the company entered into a non-cancelable agreement with its lender to refinance the debt with the same interest rate, and the full principal is due in December Year 5. How should the debt be classified on the December Year 2 balance sheet of the company?
o: Considered as an off-balance sheet liability.
Classified as a long-term liability.
Classified as a contingent liability.
Classified as a current liability.
1-23.e: Because the company has entered into a non-cancelable agreement to refinance the debt that is coming due in March of the next year, the debt is classified on the December Year 2 balance sheet as a long-term liability.
wrongWhy: This is not an off-balance sheet liability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
This is not a contingent liability. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

Even though the debt matures during the next 12 months, because the company has entered into a non-cancelable agreement to refinance it, the debt will be shown on the balance sheet as a long-term liability.

1-23.q: Trade accounts payable are valued on the statement of financial position at the
o: Current market value.
Current cost.
Net settlement value.
Historical cost.
1-23.e: Accounts payable are recorded on the balance sheet at the amount that will be needed to settle the liability. This is called the net settlement value.
wrongWhy: Accounts payable are not recorded at their current market value on the balance sheet. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
Accounts payable are not recorded at their current cost on the balance sheet. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
Accounts payable are not recorded at the historical cost on the balance sheet. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

1-23.q: Long-term debt is reclassified as current liability when:
o: Management decides to pay it early
It matures within One Year of the Balance Sheet date
Interest payments are past due
The credit rating declines
1-23.e: Long-term debt becomes a Current liability when it is Due Within One Year of the Balance Sheet date. The principal amount maturing within 12 months must be shown as current to properly reflect the company's near-term cash obligations.

1-23.q: A debt covenant is violated before year-end. The lender has not waived the acceleration clause. This debt should be classified as:
o: Non-current — covenant violations are normal
Current — the lender could demand immediate repayment
Only disclosed in notes, not reclassified
Current only if the lender actually demands payment
1-23.e: When a Covenant is violated and the lender can demand immediate repayment, the debt should be classified as Current. The acceleration risk means the debt could require cash within 12 months. Classification follows economic substance.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, if long-term debt matures in 6 months but the company completes a refinancing agreement (3-year term) One Month After the BS date:
o: Can be classified as long-term because refinancing occurred
Must remain current — refinancing was Not completed before BS date
Can be long-term if disclosed in notes
Is classified based on management intent
1-23.e: Under GAAP: refinancing must be Completed (not just agreed upon) Before the BS date to classify debt as long-term. If refinancing occurs after the BS date — even before FS issuance — the debt remains Current under GAAP (IFRS differs here).

1-23.q: A lender grants a covenant waiver for 18 months from the Balance Sheet date. The violated long-term debt should be classified as:
o: Current — violations are always current
Non-Current — waiver covers more than 12 months
Current — waivers don't change classification
Non-current only if management intends to refinance
1-23.e: If the lender grants a Waiver covering at least 12 months from the BS date (here 18 months > 12 months), the acceleration risk is eliminated for that period. The debt can remain Non-Current because the lender has given up the right to demand payment for the waiver period.

1-23.q: Under IFRS, if a company refinances violating long-term debt Before the FS are authorized for issue (but after BS date), the debt:
o: Must always be current
May be classified as non-current since refinancing completed before FS authorized
Is always non-current
Must be disclosed but not reclassified
1-23.e: IFRS allows the refinancing to be considered if completed before the FS Are Authorized For Issue (even after BS date). This is a Key difference from GAAP, which only considers refinancing completed Before the BS date itself.

1-23.q: A Demand Note (callable by lender at any time) is classified as:
o: Long-term
Current — lender can demand payment within the year
Long-term if company does not expect demand
Non-current if secured by long-term assets
1-23.e: Demand notes callable At Lender'S Discretion = Current liabilities. The lender could legally demand full repayment anytime. Intent does not override the contractual right.

1-23.q: Reclassifying $500,000 of long-term debt to current due to covenant violation affects working capital by:
o: Decreasing working capital by $500,000
Increasing working capital
No effect
Decreasing current assets by $500,000
1-23.e: Working capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Moving $500,000 to Current Liabilities increases current liabilities → working capital Decreases by $500,000.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, short-term debt Can be classified as long-term if, before the FS are Issued, the company:
o: Signs a letter of intent
Obtains a Firm refinancing commitment (or actually refinances long-term)
Has board approval
Discloses intent in notes
1-23.e: ASC 470-10: must Complete refinancing or have Firm Commitment before FS are Issued (not just authorized). Intent alone is insufficient.

1-23.q: The Key difference between IFRS and US GAAP on covenant-violated debt is:
o: They are identical
Under IFRS, waiver before FS are Authorized For Issue (not just issued) keeps debt long-term
Under GAAP, waiver must be before FS authorization
IFRS always requires current classification
1-23.e: IFRS: waiver before FS Authorized For Issue = long-term. US GAAP: waiver before FS Issued. These dates can differ — IFRS allows a slightly longer window.

1-23.q: Current Portion Of Long-Term Debt =
o: 12 months of scheduled payments
Principal due within one year from BS date (or operating cycle if longer)
Callable portion
Annual interest payment
1-23.e: Current portion = debt principal due within One Year from the balance sheet date. Reclassified from long-term to current regardless of original issuance date.

1-23.q: A Subjective Acceleration Clause triggers when:
o: Borrower misses a payment
There is a Material Adverse Change in borrower financial condition
Lender needs liquidity
Market rates change
1-23.e: Subjective acceleration clauses use vague 'material adverse change' triggers. If acceleration probability is remote → debt may remain long-term. If reasonably possible or probable → reclassify to current.

1-23.q: For a waiver to keep covenant-violated debt as long-term, the waiver must cover:
o: At least 30 days
At least 6 months
More Than 12 Months from the balance sheet date
Indefinitely
1-23.e: Waiver must defer acceleration for More Than 12 Months from the BS date to maintain long-term classification.

1-23.q: A company violates a covenant September 30 (year-end). Lender grants waiver October 15 (before FS issued). Under US GAAP:
o: Current — violation existed at BS date
Long-term — waiver obtained before FS issued
Long-term only if waiver was before year-end
Current — waivers must be before year-end
1-23.e: US GAAP: waiver (for 12+ months) obtained before FS Issued = long-term, even if after the balance sheet date.

1-23.q: A $1,000,000 note maturing in 3 years has $200,000 due in next 12 months. Balance sheet shows:
o: $1,000,000 current
$200,000 current, $800,000 long-term
$1,000,000 long-term
All current — single note
1-23.e: $200,000 = Current Portion of long-term debt (due within 12 months). $800,000 = Long-Term. Split even for a single note.

1-23.q: Bridge Financing is classified as:
o: Long-term immediately — intended to be refinanced
Current unless a firm long-term commitment exists before FS issuance
Long-term with board approval
Current only if expiring within 30 days
1-23.e: Bridge financing = current until a Firm Commitment for permanent long-term financing exists (before FS issuance). Intent alone → current.

1-23.q: Minimum disclosure when short-term debt is excluded from current liabilities includes:
o: No disclosure needed
Description, terms of new arrangement, and amount excluded
Amount only
Auditor confirmation
1-23.e: Must disclose: (1) Description of refinancing, (2) Terms of new arrangement, (3) Amount excluded from current liabilities.

1-23.q: Under IFRS (Ias 1), covenant-violated long-term debt is classified as current Unless:
o: Company intends to cure
Lender grants waiver — at any time
Waiver granted Before FS authorized for issue (for 12+ months)
Violation is technical/immaterial
1-23.e: IFRS: must obtain waiver Before FS Authorized For Issue (for 12+ months post-BS). Under GAAP, cutoff is before FS Issued — potentially a later date.

1-24.q: The selling price of a new company's units is $10,000 each. The company sold 80 units in the first year of operation and 100 units in the second year. Actual payments for warranty claims were $10,000 and $65,000 in years one and two, respectively. The amount charged to assurance-type warranty ? expense during the second year of operation is:
1-24.e: The amount that should be recognized as assurance-type warranty expense in Year 2 is calculated as the number of sales made in Year 2 multiplied by all of the expected future warranty costs for those sales. The company sold 100 units and the expected future total two-year warranty cost is $1,000 for each sale: $250 in the year of purchase and $750 in the year following purchase (it does not matter that the costs are split over two future periods). Therefore, during its second year of operation, the company should recognize warranty expense of $100,000 (100 units sold x $1,000). The information about costs incurred and paid is relevant only if the question asks about the assurance-type warranty liability.
wrongWhy: This is the amount of warranty expenditures expected to be incurred in Year 2 from both Year 1 and Year 2 sales: (80 x $750) + (100 x $250). The amount of the warranty expense recognized for Year 2 should be the number of sales made in Year 2 multiplied by all of the expected future warranty costs for those sales.

This is the amount of warranty claims that were expected to be incurred in Year 2 for the sales made in Year 2 (100 units sold x $250). The amount of the expense recognized for Year 2 should be for all future costs after the point of sale, no matter which year the costs are incurred in.

This is the actual payments made for warranty claims in Year 2. The amount of the warranty expense recognized for Year 2 should be the number of sales made in Year 2 multiplied by all of the expected future warranty costs for those sales. https:iwww. 1-24.q: East Corp. manufactures stereo systems that carry a two-year assurance-type warranty against defects. Based on past experience, assurance warranty costs are estimated at 4% of sales for the warranty period. During 20X9, stereo system sales totaled $3,000,000, and assurance warranty costs of $67,500 were incurred. In its income statement for the year ended December 31, 20X9, East should report assurance warranty expense of:
1-24.e: The assurance warranty expense is the total value of the sales multiplied by the estimated future assurance warranty costs: $3,000,000 x 4% = $120,000.
wrongWhy: This is the remaining warranty liability after the incurrence of $67,500 in warranty costs.
This is half of the total warranty expense, but the entire warranty expense should be expensed in the period that the sales were made.
This is the amount of warranty costs incurred. This is not the amount that should be expensed.

1-24.q: z Equip Corp., a manufacturer of small commercial heating units, follows the generally accepted method of accounting for assurance-type warranties in accounting for estimated future warranty costs. The company recently designed and manufactured a new model, 250 units of which were sold (with a one-year warranty) for $6,000 per unit during November of the current year. Estimated future warranty costs ($150 per unit, based on past experience) were not r accounted for at the time of sale, and the company incurred no warranty cost during November and December of the current year. The year-end adjusting entry required at December 31 of the current year to account for estimated future warranty costs would be to
o: Debit assurance-type warranty expense for $37,500 and credit assurance-type warranty liability for $37,500.
Debit sales for $37,500 and credit assurance-type warranty liability for $37,500.
Debit contract liabilities and credit cash or accounts receivable for $37,500.
Debit sales for $37,500 and credit contract liabilities for $37,500.

This will be done with a debit to the assurance-type warranty expense account and a credit to the assurance-type warranty liability account. The amount will be the estimated amount of warranty costs to be incurred in the future. The company sold 250 units and the expected warranty costs are $150 per unit, for a total of $37,500. This is the amount of the journal entry.
