1-29.q: Preferred and common stock differ in that:
o: Failure to pay dividends on common stock will not force the firm into bankruptcy, while failure to pay dividends on preferred stock will force the firm into bankruptcy.
Common stock dividends are a fixed amount, while preferred stock dividends are not.
Preferred stock dividends are deductible as an expense for tax purposes, while common stock dividends are not.
Preferred stock has a higher priority than common stock with regard to earnings and assets in the event of bankruptcy.
1-29.e: In the case of bankruptcy or other liquidation, preferred shareholders do have a priority over common shareholders in the distribution of the assets of the company.
wrongWhy: The failure to pay dividends on either common stock or preferred stock will not force the company into bankruptcy.

Common dividends are not a fixed amount. Preferred dividends are not always a fixed amount, though they generally are stated in terms of a certain percentage of the preferred stock's par value which is effectively a fixed amount, subject to declaration each period by the board of directors, of course.
Neither preferred dividends nor common dividends are tax deductible.

1-29.q: Each share of 8% cumulative preferred stock in a company that meets its dividend obligations has all of the following characteristics except
o: Dividend payments that are not tax deductible by the company.
No principal repayments.
Voting rights in corporate elections.
Aclaim that is superior to the claim of common stockholders in the case of liquidation.
1-29.e: Preferred shares do not have the right to vote in corporate elections. Since the question asks for which of the items is not a characteristic of this type of share, this is the correct answer.
wrongWhy: The dividends that are paid to preferred shareholders (and to common shareholders, as well) are not tax deductible. Since the question asks for which is not a characteristic, this answer is incorrect because this is a characteristic of preferred shares.

Preferred shares (and common shares) do not have a principal repayment. Since the question asks for which is not a characteristic, this answer is incorrect because this is a characteristic of preferred shares.

In the case of a liquidation preferred shares have a superior claim to the assets of the company compared to common shareholders. Since the question asks for which is not a characteristic, this answer is incorrect because this is a characteristic of preferred shares.

1-29.q: Cumulative preferred stock means:
o: Preferred shareholders get extra dividends above stated rate
Missed dividends Accumulate and must be paid before common dividends
Dividends grow each year
Preferred shareholders get voting rights
1-29.e: Cumulative: if a dividend is Not paid in a period, it Accumulates as 'dividends in arrears.' These arrears must be paid to preferred shareholders in full Before any dividends can be paid to common shareholders.

1-29.q: Dividends In Arrears on cumulative preferred stock are:
o: Recorded as a liability on the Balance Sheet
Not a liability until declared — but must be Disclosed in notes
Deducted from Retained Earnings immediately
Recognized as an expense on the income statement
1-29.e: Dividends in arrears are Not a liability — no declaration has been made. They are Not recorded on the balance sheet. However, they Must Be Disclosed in the notes to the financial statements, so users know the obligation before common dividends can be paid.

1-29.q: In liquidation, which is paid Last?
o: Secured creditors
Unsecured creditors
Preferred shareholders
Common shareholders
1-29.e: Liquidation priority: Secured creditors → Unsecured creditors → Preferred shareholders → Common Shareholders (last). Common shareholders bear the most risk and receive any residual value — which could be zero if prior claims consume all assets.

1-29.q: Mandatorily Redeemable preferred stock is classified as:
o: Equity — preferred stock
A Liability on the Balance Sheet — substance over form
Long-term investment
Other comprehensive income
1-29.e: Mandatorily redeemable preferred stock is classified as a Liability because the company is Obligated to redeem (buy back) it at a specified date or upon a specific event. Economic substance = debt obligation. This follows the Substance Over Form principle.

1-29.q: 1,000 shares of 8%, $50 par preferred stock. Dividends in arrears for 3 years. Total preferred dividends owed before common shareholders can receive anything =
1-29.e: Annual preferred dividend = 1,000 × $50 × 8% = $4,000. Dividends in arrears = 3 years × $4,000 = $12,000. Plus current year $4,000 = $16,000 total that preferred shareholders must receive before common gets anything.

1-29.q: Participating preferred: holders can receive:
o: Extra voting rights
Additional Dividends beyond stated rate after common gets base amount
Convert to common anytime
Demand redemption at par
1-29.e: Participating preferred can 'participate' in Extra dividends beyond stated rate, sharing excess dividends with common holders.

1-29.q: Convertible preferred: conversion is at:
o: Company's option only
Holder'S option at specified conversion ratio
Maturity only
Only when preferred dividend is in arrears
1-29.e: Convertible preferred: Holder has the right to convert at specified Conversion Ratio. Typically exercised when economically beneficial.

1-29.q: Callable preferred: the Company has the right to:
o: Force holder to convert
Redeem shares at specified call price after a set date
Vote on dividend amounts
Convert to debt
1-29.e: Callable: Issuer can Call (redeem) shares at specified Call Price (usually at or above par) after a certain date.

1-29.q: Non-Cumulative preferred: if dividends skipped Year 1, Year 2 requirement before common dividends:
o: Pay both Year 1 and Year 2
Pay Current Year (Year 2) only — Year 1 dividends lost
Pay common without any preferred
Pay 50% of skipped
1-29.e: Non-cumulative: skipped dividends Lost. Only current year preferred dividend must be paid before common.

1-29.q: Liquidation Preference means preferred holders receive:
o: Higher dividend rate
Their Liquidation Amount before common holders in bankruptcy/wind-down
Voting rights in liquidation
First access to operating assets
1-29.e: Liquidation priority: Creditors → Preferred (at liquidation preference: par + arrears) → Common (whatever remains, possibly nothing).

1-29.q: Preferred dividends are deducted in Basic EPS because:
o: They are tax-deductible
They reduce income Available To Common shareholders
They are fixed obligations
Preferred is classified as liability
1-29.e: Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Avg Common Shares. Deducted because EPS measures earnings attributable to Common shareholders.

1-29.q: $100 par, 7% cumulative preferred, 1,000 shares. Dividends skipped 2 years. Year 3 minimum preferred dividends =
1-29.e: Annual = 1,000 × $100 × 7% = $7,000. Arrears (2 years) + current = 3 × $7,000 = $21,000 before common receives anything.

1-29.q: Under IFRS, Mandatorily Redeemable preferred is classified as:
o: Permanent equity
Financial Liability — obligation to deliver cash
Mezzanine financing
Equity unless dividends are cumulative
1-29.e: IFRS (Ias 32) and US GAAP (ASC 480): mandatorily redeemable preferred = Liability. Dividends on such instruments = Interest Expense.

1-29.q: Preferred stock Issuance journal entry credits:
o: Retained earnings
Preferred Stock at par + Apic-Preferred at excess
Common Stock Apic
Bonds Payable if redeemable
1-29.e: Dr Cash, Cr Preferred Stock (par value × shares), Cr Apic-Preferred (excess). Mirrors common stock issuance.

1-29.q: Convertible preferred in Diluted EPS is Antidilutive when:
o: Preferred dividends exceed earnings
Including converted shares Increases (rather than decreases) diluted EPS
Preferred has been outstanding < 1 year
Conversion ratio is < 1:1
1-29.e: Antidilutive = inclusion would Increase diluted EPS (less negative effect). Exclude from calculation.

1-29.q: Participating preferred: 1,000 shares × $100 par, 6% base. Common: 5,000 shares × $10 par. Total dividends $20,000. After $6,000 base to preferred, remaining $14,000 shared per share among all 6,000 shares. Preferred Additional dividend =
1-29.e: Additional per share = $14,000 ÷ 6,000 = $2.333. Preferred additional = 1,000 × $2.333 = $2,333. Total preferred = $6,000 + $2,333 = $8,333.

1-30.q: On December 1, Charles Company's board of directors declared a cash dividend of $1.00 per share on the 50,000 shares of the company's common stock outstanding. The company also held 5,000 shares of treasury stock on December 1. Shareholders of record on December 15 are eligible for the dividend, which is to be paid on January 1. On December 1, the company should
o: Debit retained earnings for $50,000.
Make no accounting entry.
Debit retained earnings for $50,000 and paid-in capital for $5,000.
Debit retained earnings for $55,000.

The dividend is paid only to shares that are outstanding. This means that the shares held as treasury stock do not receive the dividend. The company declared a $1 per share dividend and the company has 50,000 shares outstanding, so retained earnings should be debited for $50,000.
wrongWhy: On the date of declaration the company should make a journal entry to reduce retained earnings and to recognize a dividend payable. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The debit to retained earnings is correct, but there is no debit to paid-in-capital. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The debit to retained earnings is incorrect because it includes the treasury shares. Treasury shares do not receive the dividend. The dividend is paid only to shares outstanding, and treasury shares are not outstanding. 1-30.q: Brand Corporation has 3,000,000 shares of $10 par value stock authorized, of which 2,000,000 shares are issued and outstanding. The Board of Directors of Brand plans to declare a 2-for-1 stock split on November 30 to be issued on December 30. The stock is currently selling for $30 per share. Will the company be required to amend its articles of incorporation before declaring the stock split?
o: No, because the number of new shares to be issued in the stock split will not exceed the number of authorized but unissued shares.
No, because the number of authorized shares will automatically be doubled by the declaration of the 2-for- stock split.
Yes, because a 2-for-1 stock split will result in more shares outstanding than are currently authorized.
Yes, because the par value of each share will be reduced by half by the stock split.
1-30.e: The company presently has 2,000,000 shares of $10 par value stock issued and outstanding. After the shares distributed in the 2-for-1 stock split have been issued, the company will have 4,000,000 shares of $5 par value stock issued and outstanding. Only 3,000,000 shares are authorized, so prior to declaring the stock split, the company will need to obtain approval from its shareholders to increase the number of authorized shares and then file an amendment to its articles of incorporation with the state where the company is incorporated to increase the number of authorized shares.
wrongWhy: The number of new shares to be issued in the stock split will exceed the number of authorized but unissued shares. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

The number of authorized shares is not automatically increased by the declaration of a stock split. If a company wants to split its stock and if the number of resulting new shares would exceed the number of authorized but unissued shares, the company must seek shareholder approval to increase the number of authorized shares and then file the necessary amendment to its articles of incorporation with the state where the company is incorporated. Brand has 2,000,000 shares issued and outstanding currently. A 2-for-1 stock split would increase the number of outstanding shares to 4,000,000, which is greater than the number of authorized shares, so Brand will need to take the necessary steps to amend its articles of incorporation before it will be able to declare the 2-for-1 stock split.

It is true that the company will be required to amend its articles of incorporation prior to declaring the stock split. It is also true that the par value of each share will be reduced by half by the stock split. However, the reduction of the par value of each share is not the reason the company will be required to amend its articles of incorporation prior to declaring the stock split. an asset of the company.

1-30.q: P The maximum number of shares that a corporation may issue is the definition of the number of its
o: issued shares.
authorized shares.
unissued shares.
outstanding shares.
1-30.e: The number of authorized shares is defined as the maximum number of shares the company can issue. It is initially specified in the company’s articles of incorporation or charter, and it can be changed only by filing an amendment to the articles of incorporation or charter with the state where the company is incorporated. An amendment to the articles of incorporation normally requires prior shareholder approval. If a company wants to issue new stock, split its stock, declare a stock dividend, or issue stock to its management or employees under stock option grants, and if the number of resulting new shares would exceed the number of authorized but unissued shares, the company must seek shareholder approval to increase the number of authorized shares and then file the necessary amendment to its articles of incorporation
wrongWhy: The maximum number of shares that a corporation may issue is not the definition of the number of its issued shares. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
The maximum number of shares that a corporation may issue is not the definition of the number of its unissued shares. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

increase in shareholders’ equity.

1-30.topic: Treasury Stock and Classification of Shares

1-30.q: Treasury stock is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: An asset — the company owns its own shares
A Reduction from shareholders' equity (contra-equity)
A current liability
Other comprehensive income
1-30.e: Treasury stock is a Contra-Equity account — deducted from total shareholders' equity. It is Not an asset. A company cannot own itself. The repurchase reduces equity because cash left the company and shares were retired from circulation (though not formally cancelled).

1-30.q: When treasury stock is Reissued at a price Above its cost, the excess is:
o: Recognized as a gain in Net Income
Added to Retained Earnings
Credited to Apic-Treasury Stock
Credited to Common Stock
1-30.e: Reissue above cost: Dr. Cash (proceeds) / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost) / Cr. Apic-Treasury (excess). The Excess above cost goes to Apic — not income. There are No Gains or Losses on treasury stock transactions.

1-30.q: A company has: Authorized 500,000 / Issued 300,000 / Treasury 30,000. Outstanding shares =
1-30.e: Outstanding = Issued − Treasury = 300,000 − 30,000 = 270,000 shares. Outstanding shares represent shares currently held by External shareholders (not the company itself).

1-30.q: When treasury stock is reissued below its cost, the shortfall (cost > proceeds) is first charged to:
o: Net Income (loss)
Retained Earnings
Apic-Treasury Stock (if balance exists), then Retained Earnings
Common Stock account
1-30.e: Reissue below cost: Dr. Cash (proceeds) + Dr. Apic-Treasury (if available) + Dr. Re (if Apic-Treasury exhausted) / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost). The shortfall reduces Apic-Treasury First, then Retained Earnings if Apic-Treasury is insufficient.

1-30.q: A gain or loss on treasury stock transactions is recognized:
o: In Net Income as a gain/loss
In OCI
Never — treasury stock transactions are equity transactions, not income
In Retained Earnings as a direct adjustment
1-30.e: No Gain Or Loss is recognized on treasury stock transactions (purchase or reissuance). They are Equity Transactions — excess above cost goes to Apic; shortfall reduces Apic then Re. Reporting gains/losses on own-stock transactions would allow manipulation of earnings.

1-30.q: Under the Cost Method, treasury stock is recorded at:
o: Par value
Cost (price paid to repurchase) — most common under US GAAP
Book value
Average issued price
1-30.e: Cost method (most common): treasury stock = Cost Paid. Contra-equity at cost. No entry to individual equity components until resale or retirement.

1-30.q: When treasury stock is formally Retired (cost method), any excess of cost over par+Apic is charged to:
o: Apic then Retained Earnings
Operating expense
OCI
Investment loss
1-30.e: Retirement: Dr Common Stock (par), Dr Apic (per share), Dr Retained Earnings (if cost > par+Apic), Cr Treasury Stock (cost). If cost < par+Apic → credit Re. No gain on income statement.

1-30.q: Constructive Retirement of treasury stock means:
o: Stock physically destroyed
Accounted for As If retired using par value method — even if may be reissued
Reached end of useful life
Donated to charity
1-30.e: Par Value Method (constructive retirement): treasury stock immediately recorded as if retired. Less common than cost method.

1-30.q: Donated treasury stock received from a shareholder is recorded at:
o: Zero — no consideration
Fair Value at donation date
Par value
Book value per share
1-30.e: Donated shares → Fair Value at donation date. Dr Treasury Stock (FV), Cr Donated Capital (Apic component).

1-30.q: Purchasing treasury stock Reduces EPS denominator because:
o: Treasury shares earn no dividends
Treasury shares Not Outstanding — excluded from weighted average share count
Company net income decreases
Classified as liabilities
1-30.e: EPS denominator = outstanding shares. Treasury shares (issued but not outstanding) Excluded → fewer shares → EPS increases (assuming unchanged net income).

1-30.q: Share Repurchase journal entry:
o: Dr Investment in own shares, Cr Cash
Dr Treasury Stock (contra-equity), Cr Cash
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Cash
No entry until cancellation
1-30.e: Repurchase: Dr Treasury Stock (contra-equity at cost paid), Cr Cash. Treasury stock reduces total equity.

1-30.q: Treasury stock on balance sheet is presented as:
o: Current asset
Long-term investment
Deduction From Total Stockholders' Equity
OCI component
1-30.e: Treasury stock = Contra-Equity. Appears in Se section as Deduction. Not an asset — company cannot hold itself.

1-30.q: If treasury stock repurchase price Exceeds book value per share, remaining shareholders' book value per share:
o: Increases — fewer shares
Decreases — paid more than book value
Unchanged
Increases equal to excess paid
1-30.e: Paying More than book value per share: total equity decreases more per share than each repurchased share's book value → remaining book value per share Decreases.

1-31.q: Under ASC 606, recognition of revenue does not occur until the
o: entity has signed a binding contract. og _
performance obligation has been satisfied.
cash is collected.

performance obligation is satisfied and it is probable that the company will be able to collect substantially all of the consideration that it is entitled to receive for the goods or services that will be transferred to the customer under a valid contract.
1-31.e: Revenue should be recognized in the accounting period in which the performance obligation is satisfied and in an amount that reflects the consideration the entity expects to be entitled to receive under the contract. The contract must be valid, meaning it creates enforceable rights and obligations and meets the following criteria: (1) the parties have approved the contract and are committed to perform their obligations under the contract; (2) the rights of each party can be identified; (3) the payment terms can be identified; (4) the contract has commercial substance; and (5) it is probable that the company will be able to collect substantially all of the consideration it is entitled to receive for the goods or services that will be transferred to the customer. A performance obligation is satisfied when the customer obtains control of the asset, which is the good or service that is transferred to the customer. The company must assess the collectibility of the contract, including an assessment of the customer's credit risk. Based on that assessment, the company must conclude that it is probable (meaning “likely to occur,” which generally means there is a 75% to 80% probability of occurrence) that the company will be able to collect substantially all of the consideration it is entitled to receive for the goods or services that will be transferred to the customer under the contract.
wrongWhy: A contract is an agreement between two or more parties that creates enforceable rights and obligations. While an approved contract is required before revenue can be recognized, the contract need not be in writing or signed. The approval may be oral, in writing, or simply implied by the entity's customary business practices.

Revenue is recognized when the performance obligation is satisfied by transferring goods or services to the customer in an amount that reflects the consideration the company expects to be entitled to receive in exchange for those goods or services. Before revenue can be recognized, the company must also assess the collectibility of the contract, including an assessment of the customer's credit risk. Based on that assessment, the company must conclude that it is probable (meaning “likely to occur,” which generally means there is a 75% to 80% probability of occurrence) that the company will be able to collect substantially all of the consideration that it is entitled to receive for the goods or services transferred to the customer under the contract.

’ The collection of the cash is not required for the recognition of revenue. Furthermore, the collection of cash does not, by itself, imply that revenue can be recognized. If the company has received a portion of the consideration due from the customer but the contract does not qualify as a valid contract under the requirements of ASC 606, any consideration received should be accounted for as a contract liability.

1-31.q: An airline should recognize revenue from an airline ticket in the period in which
o: The related flight takes place.
The ticket is issued.
Passenger reservations are booked.
Passenger reservations are confirmed.
1-31.e: Under ASC 606, revenue should be recognized to depict the transfer of goods or services to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration the company expects to be entitled to in exchange for those goods and services. Revenue is recognized when the performance obligation is satisfied. The performance obligation for an airline ticket is satisfied when the flight takes place, so the revenue from the airline ticket should be recognized at that time.

Revenue should not be recognized when the reservations are booked. Under ASC 606, revenue is recognized when the performance obligation is satisfied.
Revenue should not be recognized when the reservations are confirmed. Under ASC 606, revenue is recognized when the performance obligation is satisfied.

1-31.q: On May 28, Markal Company purchased a tooling machine from Arens and Associates for $1,000,000, payable as follows: 50 percent at the transaction closing date and 50 percent due June 28. The cost of the machine to Arens is $800,000. Markal paid Arens $500,000 at the transaction closing date and took possession of the machine. On June 10, Arens determined that a change in the business environment has created a great deal of ’ uncertainty regarding the collection of the balance due from Markal, and the amount is probably a credit loss. Arens and Markal have a fiscal year end of May 31. The revenue recognized by Arens and Associates on May 28 is
1-31.e: On the date of the sale (May 28), Markal obtained control of the tooling machine, so Arens' performance obligation with respect to the sales contract was satisfied. At that time, Arens expected the consideration it would be entitled to in exchange for the machine to be $1,000,000, as there was no indication that the receivable would not be collectible. Therefore, on May 28, the entire $1,000,000 selling price is recognized as revenue.
wrongWhy: This is the cost of goods sold under the sales contract, not the revenue recognized.

On the date of the sale (May 28), Markal obtained control of the tooling machine, so Arens' performance obligation with respect to the sales contract was satisfied. At that time, Arens expected the consideration it would be entitled to in exchange for the machine to be the full selling price, as there was no indication that the receivable would not be collectible.
This is the gross profit on the sales contract, not the revenue that was recognized. https:/Avww.t6dUnterAa ional. cB rishystacleg/eanh TES O a par UCU HRAPOMtING 330 Q said 112

Therefore, revenue for 20X1 should include wholesale products that were received by customers before year end and in-store sales only. wrongWhy: This answer results from including the $75,000 of wholesale products shipped December 31, 20X1 but not received until Jan. 3, 20X2 as revenue in 20X1. : Because wholesale products are shipped FOB Destination, they belong to IER until the customer receives them. The products received by the customers on Jan. 3, 20X2 belonged to IER at year end, so they should not be included in revenue recognized by IER for the year ended December 31, 20X1.
This is the in-store retail sales only. Wholesale products that were received by customers before year end should also be recognized as revenue in the current year.

This answer results from recognizing as revenue in 20X1 all of the items listed in the question. Because wholesale products are shipped FOB Destination, they belong to IER until the customer receives them and are recognized as revenue only at that time. Items out on consignment also belong to IER and are not recognized as revenue because they have not yet been sold. Therefore, wholesale products shipped that were not received by customers before year end ($75,000 and $500) as well as wholesale products out on consignment at year end ($10,000) should not be recognized as revenue by IER for the year ended December 31, 20X1.

1-31.q: Accompany provides fertilization, insect control, and disease control services for a variety of trees, plants, and shrubs on a contract basis. For $50 per month, the company will visit the subscriber's premises and apply appropriate mixtures. If the subscriber has any problems between the regularly scheduled application dates, the company's personnel will promptly make additional service calls to correct the situation. Some subscribers elect to pay for an entire year ’ because the company offers an annual price of $540 if paid in advance. For a subscriber who pays the annual fee in advance, the company should recognize the related revenue
o: When the cash is collected.
At the end of the fiscal year.
Evenly over the year as the services are performed.
At the end of the contract year after all of the services have been performed.
1-31.e: This is an example of a contract where the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company’s performance as the company is performing its contract obligations. Revenue should be recognized over time as the company performs its obligations. Consideration received in advance is recorded as a contract liability.
wrongWhy: The collection of cash is not the event that triggers revenue recognition. This is an example of a contract where the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company’s performance as the company is performing its contract obligations. Revenue should be recognized over time as the company performs its obligations.

This is an example of a contract where the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company’s performance as the company is performing its contract obligations. Revenue should be recognized over time as the company performs its obligations.

This is an example of a contract where the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the company’s performance as the company is performing its contract obligations. Revenue should be recognized over time as the company performs its obligations.

1-31.q: The practice of recording consideration received from a customer before the performance obligation in the contract has been satisfied as a contract liability is an example of applying the
o: Going-concern assumption.
Monetary-unit assumption.
Historic cost principle.
Revenue recognition principle. a
1-31.e: According to ASC 606, Revenue Recognition, when the seller receives consideration from a customer before transferring to the customer control of the goods or services represented by the payment, the seller has not yet satisfied the performance obligations in the contract and therefore cannot recognize revenue. Instead, the receipt represents the seller's obligation to satisfy the performance obligations in the contract and so is recorded as a contract liability.
wrongWhy: The going concern assumption is the assumption that the business will continue operating for the foreseeable future. This is not relevant to the issue of consideration received from a customer before the performance obligation in the contract has been satisfied.

The monetary-unit assumption is the assumption that the financial statements are presented in a monetary unit. This is not relevant to the issue of consideration received from a customer before the performance obligation in the contract has been satisfied.

The historical cost principle is the principle that assets are recorded at the amount that was paid to acquire them. This is not relevant to the issue of consideration received from a customer before the performance obligation in the contract has been satisfied.

1-31.q: A conditional contract asset is
o: aright to receive consideration because the company has partially satisfied the performance obligations in the contract but it must satisfy another performance " obligation or obligations before it can invoice the customer.
revenue recognized when the right of return exists.
a receivable that management believes may be a credit loss.
cash consideration received by the seller before any performance obligations in the contract have been satisfied.
1-31.e: A conditional contract asset is a conditional right to receive consideration because the company has satisfied one of, or some of, the performance obligations in the contract and thus recognizes revenue for the performance obligations that are satisfied, but it must satisfy another performance obligation or obligations before it can invoice the customer. Conditional rights to receive consideration should be reported on the balance sheet as contract assets.
wrongWhy: When the right of return exists, the contract consideration is variable consideration.

A conditional contract asset is not a receivable that management believes may be a credit loss. It is not a receivable at all because it does not represent an invoice that has been issued to a customer.
Cash consideration received by the seller before any performance obligations in the contract have been satisfied is a contract liability.

1-31.q: After a successful promotion aimed at members of a specific national association, Gorham Publishing Company received a total of $90,000 for three-year subscriptions to a monthly publication beginning April 1, 20X5 and properly recorded this consideration received in the contract liabilities account. Assuming Gorham records adjustments only at the end of the calendar year, the adjusting entry required to reflect the proper balances in the accounts at December 31, 20X5, would be to:
o: Debit subscription revenue for $67,500 and credit contract liabilities for $67,500.
Debit contract liabilities for $30,000 and credit subscription revenue for $30,000.
Debit contract liabilities for $22,500 and credit subscription revenue for $22,500.
Debit contract liabilities for $67,500 and credit subscription revenue for $67,500.
1-31.e: When the subscriptions were recorded the journal entry was a debit to cash for $90,000 and a credit to contract liabilities for $90,000. At the end of 20X5, Gorham needs to recognize revenue for the portion of the performance obligation that has been satisfied. Since there are months in the subscription period and nine months have passed, Gorham should recognize 25% of the consideration received as revenue. This is done by debiting contract liabilities for $22,500 ($90,000 x 0.25) and crediting subscription revenue for $22,500. Note: The correct amount can also be calculated by dividing $90,000 by 36 to find the amount of consideration received to be recognized as revenue each month and multiplying the monthly revenue by nine months. $90,000 + 36 x 9 = $22,500.
wrongWhy: This would be the answer if the initial entry had been to credit revenue and not to credit contract liabilities. However, in the question we are told that the initial entry was a credit to contract liabilities. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This answer assumes that 1/3 of the subscription period has passed. Since the subscriptions started April 1, only 9 months have passed, and that is 1/4 of the subscription period (9 months + 36 months). See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

This answer assumes 75% of the subscription period has passed instead of 25%. See the correct answer for a complete explanation. . < Previous Save htips:/Awww.Noeintd rast onal. CBRE PEANesitn eS BO bart ARCA) BRRPOTtING 330 Q a8 112

1-31.q: When the right of return exists, the contract consideration is
o: provisional and revenue cannot be recognized until the return privilege period has expired.
the amount the seller expects to be entitled to receive.
reported as a contract liability.
variable consideration and the contract price excludes the consideration for products expected to be returned or amounts expected to be refunded.
1-31.e: When the right of return exists, the contract consideration is variable. The contract's transaction price should exclude consideration related to products expected to be returned or amounts expected to be refunded. The seller should recognize revenue at the amount it expects to be entitled to receive, which is the revenue only for goods or services not expected to be returned or refunded.
wrongWhy: "Provisional" is not used to refer to contract consideration when the right of return exists, and the seller does not wait to recognize revenue until the return privilege period has expired.

The amount the seller expects to be entitled to receive is the amount of revenue the seller recognizes from the contract when the right of return exists. The revenue recognized is not the same as the contract consideration.
Contract consideration when the right of return exists is not reported as a contract liability.
1-31.topic: Right of Return and Consigned Goods

1-31.q: DEF is the consignee for 1,000 units of product X for ABC Company. ABC should recognize the revenue from these 1,000 units when
o: DEF sells the goods and informs ABC of the sale.
The agreement between DEF and ABC is signed.
DEF receives the goods from ABC.
ABC ships the goods to DEF.
1-31.e: The consignor recognizes revenue only when control transfers. Usually the transfer of control occurs and thus the revenue is recognized when the goods are sold to the final customer.
wrongWhy: The consignor does not recognize revenue when the agreement is signed. The consignor recognizes revenue only when control transfers. Usually the transfer of control occurs and thus the revenue is recognized when the goods are sold to the final customer.

The consignor does not recognize revenue when the consignee receive the goods. The consignor recognizes revenue only when control transfers. Usually the transfer of control occurs and thus the revenue is recognized when the goods are sold to the final customer.

The consignor does not recognize revenue when the goods are shipped to the consignee. The consignor recognizes revenue only when control transfers. Usually the transfer of control occurs and thus the revenue is recognized when the goods are sold to the final customer.
1-31.topic: Right of Return and Consigned Goods

1-31.q: The Core Principle of ASC 606 is to recognise revenue:
o: When cash is received
When an invoice is sent
When control of the good or service transfers to the customer in the amount of expected consideration
Only for long-term contracts
1-31.e: ASC 606 core principle: recognise revenue when (or as) Control of goods/services Transfers to the customer, in the amount of consideration the entity Expects To Receive. Control transfer (not risks and rewards) is the key trigger.

1-31.q: A good or service is a Distinct performance obligation when:
o: It has a high selling price
The customer can benefit from it on its own And it is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract
It is always delivered separately
Management decides to treat it separately
1-31.e: Distinct = Two criteria Both met: (1) Customer can benefit from it on its own (or with other available resources), And (2) Separately identifiable from other promises in the contract. Both criteria must be met simultaneously.

1-31.q: A company receives $24,000 for a 2-year software subscription on January 1. Under ASC 606, revenue recognised in Year 1 =
o: $0 — recognised at renewal
1-31.e: The subscription is a performance obligation satisfied Over Time (customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefit). $24,000 / 24 months × 12 months = $12,000 recognised in Year 1. The remaining $12,000 is deferred (contract liability).

1-31.q: A contractor completes 40% of a long-term construction project. Under ASC 606 over-time recognition, revenue recognised =
o: 0% — recognised on completion
40% of transaction price
100% — recognised when contract signed
50% as an estimate
1-31.e: Long-term construction typically qualifies for Over Time recognition (entity performs and customer controls the asset as it is built, or entity has enforceable right to payment for work completed). Recognise based on progress: 40% complete = 40% of total transaction price.

1-31.q: Cash received Before a performance obligation is satisfied creates a:
o: Contract asset
Accounts receivable
Contract liability (deferred revenue)
Revenue immediately
1-31.e: Cash received Before performance = Contract Liability (Deferred Revenue). The company owes the customer a good or service. Revenue is recognised only When the performance obligation is satisfied (when control transfers).

1-31.q: A software bundle: Software Ssp $6,000, Training Ssp $2,000, Support Ssp $4,000. Total Ssp $12,000. Contract price $9,000. Revenue allocated to Software =
1-31.e: Relative Ssp: Software allocation = (6,000/12,000) × $9,000 = $4,500. The $3,000 discount is spread proportionally across all POs based on their standalone selling prices.

1-31.q: Variable consideration in Step 3 should be included in the transaction price Only when:
o: The amount is exactly known
It is highly probable that including it will Not result in a significant revenue reversal
The customer requests it
Management approves it
1-31.e: Variable consideration (bonuses, rebates, penalties, returns) is Constrained — include only to the extent it is Highly Probable that a significant revenue reversal will not occur when uncertainty resolves. This prevents recognition of revenue that might need to be reversed.

1-31.q: A company builds a custom machine for a client. The machine has No alternative use and the company has an enforceable right to payment for work completed. Revenue is recognised:
o: At a point in time when delivered
Over time as construction progresses
Only when full payment received
Only when client accepts the machine
1-31.e: Over-time recognition applies when: the entity's performance Does Not Create an asset with Alternative Use to the entity And the entity has an Enforceable Right To Payment for performance completed to date. Both conditions met here → recognise Over Time.

1-31.q: The Five-Step Model of ASC 606 in order:
o: Identify contract→Allocate→Identify POs→Determine price→Recognize
Identify contract→Identify POs→Determine price→Allocate to POs→Recognize when/as satisfied
Determine price→Identify POs→Allocate→Recognize→Disclose
Identify customer→Determine price→Identify POs→Recognize→Allocate
1-31.e: Step 1: Identify Contract. Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations. Step 3: Determine Transaction Price. Step 4: Allocate to POs by standalone selling price. Step 5: Recognize when/as each Po is satisfied.

1-32.q: A bond with a coupon rate Below the market rate is issued at:
o: Par — no adjustment needed
A Premium — above face value
A Discount — below face value
Face value plus accrued interest
1-32.e: Coupon Rate < Market Rate → investors demand a higher return than the coupon provides → they pay Less than face value → Discount. The discount compensates investors for accepting a below-market coupon payment.

1-32.q: Under the effective interest method, interest expense is calculated as:
o: Face value × coupon rate
Carrying value × market rate
Face value × market rate
Carrying value × coupon rate
1-32.e: Effective Interest Method: Interest Expense = Carrying Value (beginning of period) × Market Rate. This produces a constant effective interest Rate (not constant dollar expense). As the carrying value changes, so does the dollar amount of interest expense.

1-32.q: Bond: Face $200,000, coupon 6%, market rate 8%, carrying value at start of period $185,000. Interest expense for the period =
1-32.e: Interest Expense = Carrying Value × Market Rate = $185,000 × 8% = $14,800. Cash paid = $200,000 × 6% = $12,000. Discount amortised = $14,800 - $12,000 = $2,800. New Cv = $185,000 + $2,800 = $187,800.

1-32.q: For a Discount bond, the carrying value over the bond's life:
o: Remains constant at face value
Decreases from face to zero
Increases from issue price toward face value at maturity
Equals the market rate throughout
1-32.e: Discount bond Cv Increases toward face value each period as the discount is amortised. At maturity, Cv = face value exactly. This is because discount amortisation Adds to the carrying value each period (interest expense > cash paid).

1-32.q: A company retires bonds with carrying value $102,000 by paying $98,000 cash. The result is:
o: $4,000 loss on retirement
$4,000 gain on retirement
No gain or loss — par value transaction
$4,000 addition to Apic
1-32.e: Gain/Loss = Cv - Cash Paid = $102,000 - $98,000 = $4,000 Gain. Gain because the company paid Less than what was on the books. Recorded in income from continuing operations.

1-32.q: When a bond is issued at a Premium, interest expense each period is:
o: Equal to the cash coupon payment
Greater than the cash coupon payment
Less than the cash coupon payment
Equal to face value × market rate
1-32.e: Premium bond: coupon rate > market rate. Interest Expense = Cv × market rate. Cash paid = face × coupon rate. Since market rate < coupon rate, interest expense < cash paid. The difference (cash > expense) is premium Amortisation, reducing the carrying value each period.

1-32.q: At the maturity date of a bond, the carrying value always equals:
o: The original issue price
Zero
The face (par) value
The market price on that date
1-32.e: At maturity, carrying value = Face Value regardless of whether the bond was issued at par, premium, or discount. All premium/discount has been fully amortised by maturity. The final journal entry: Dr. Bonds Payable (face) / Cr. Cash (face).

1-32.q: Under ASC 835-30, Debt Issuance Costs are presented as:
o: Intangible asset
Contra-Liability — deducted from carrying amount of debt
Current asset
Deduction from Apic
1-32.e: ASU 2015-03: debt issuance costs = Direct Deduction from debt carrying amount (contra-liability). Amortized using effective interest method.

1-32.q: Convertible Bond (no bifurcation required under US GAAP) is recorded as:
o: Split debt + equity by fair value
Entirely As Debt — no equity component
Equity if conversion expected
Expensed as financing cost
1-32.e: US GAAP (ASC 470-20, traditional): convertible bond = Entirely Debt at issuance. No equity component. Differs from IFRS (Ias 32) which requires splitting into debt + equity.

1-32.q: Bonds With Detachable Warrants: proceeds allocated by:
o: Bond par value only
Relative Fair Values of bonds (without warrants) and warrants
Face and exercise price
Management discretion
1-32.e: Bonds + detachable warrants = two separate instruments. Allocate by Relative Fair Values. Warrant portion → Apic (equity). Bond portion → potentially creates discount.

1-32.q: Zero-Coupon Bond interest expense each period:
o: Zero — no cash interest
Accrued at effective rate on Carrying Value — increasing Cv to par at maturity
Face minus issue price ÷ years
Capitalized as issuance cost
1-32.e: Zero-coupon: no cash coupons. Interest = Cv × effective rate (effective interest method). Cv increases from issue price to par at maturity.

1-32.q: Serial Bonds differ from term bonds because:
o: Serial have no maturity
Serial mature in installments; term matures all at once
Serial are callable anytime
Term have variable rates
1-32.e: Serial bonds: principal paid in Installments over multiple dates. Term bonds: entire principal at One date. Serial reduces credit risk through gradual repayment.

1-32.q: Debtor gain on Troubled Debt Restructuring recognized only when:
o: Creditor accepts reduced payment
Total future undiscounted cash flows under new terms < Carrying Value of old debt
Any reduction in payments
Loss is always recognized
1-32.e: Debtor Tdr gain: only if total Future Undiscounted Cash Flows < old carrying value. Reduce carrying value to future cash flows; future interest = zero.

1-32.q: Early Extinguishment gain/loss =
o: Par value minus repurchase price
Carrying Value (net of discount/premium/issuance costs) minus Reacquisition Price
Face minus book value
Only if all bonds retired
1-32.e: Gain/loss = Reacquisition Price minus Net Carrying Value (face ± unamortized premium/discount − unamortized issuance costs). Recognized immediately in continuing operations.

1-32.q: Notes Payable with below-market interest rate must be:
o: Recorded at face value — stated rate contractual
Discounted to PV using imputed market rate — discount recognized as interest expense over life
Expensed immediately for discount
Recorded at face if under 12 months
1-32.e: Apb 21: non-arm's-length interest rates → record at Present Value using imputed market rate. Discount amortized as interest expense (effective interest method).

1-32.q: Fair Value Option for bonds payable (ASC 825): unrealized gains/losses go to:
o: OCI
Net Income each period
Not recognized until settlement
Recognized only at maturity
1-32.e: Under fair value option: changes in bond FV → Net Income. Controversial: if company's creditworthiness worsens → bond FV decreases → Gain recognized.

1-32.q: Callable Bond: call price is usually:
o: Below par — discount for early repayment
At or Above Par — call premium compensates bondholders
Equal to book value on call date
Market rate at call date
1-32.e: Call price = at par or slight Premium (call premium) to compensate bondholders for loss of expected future interest at early redemption.

1-32.q: Bond covenant violation: accounting impact on financial statements:
o: Write-down to fair value
Possible Reclassification to current if lender can accelerate
Gain — company may renegotiate
Notes disclosure only
1-32.e: Covenant violation may give lender Right To Accelerate → if not waived for 12+ months → debt reclassified as Current.

1-32.q: Effective interest method — Bond: face $100,000, coupon 5%, effective 6%, Cv start = $95,000. Interest expense =
1-32.e: Interest = Cv × effective rate = $95,000 × 6% = $5,700. Cash paid = $100,000 × 5% = $5,000. Discount amortized = $700. New Cv = $95,700.

1-32.q: Under IFRS 9, most bonds payable are measured at:
o: Fair value through profit or loss by default
Amortized Cost (effective interest method) unless fair value option elected
Historical cost without amortization
Fair value with changes in OCI
1-32.e: IFRS 9 default for financial liabilities = Amortized Cost (effective interest method). Fair value option (Fvtpl) available if it reduces accounting mismatch.

1-33.q: Basic EPS is calculated as:
o: Net Income / Total Shares Issued
(Net Income − Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding
Net Income / Total Authorized Shares
(Net Income + Preferred Dividends) / Common Shares Issued
1-33.e: Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding. Preferred dividends are deducted because EPS measures earnings available to Common shareholders. Weighted average shares accounts for shares issued or repurchased during the year.

1-33.q: Net Income $500,000. Preferred dividends $50,000. Weighted avg common shares 225,000. Basic EPS =
A company has 100,000 shares on Jan 1. Issues 24,000 new shares on Jul 1. Weighted average shares =
1-33.e: Weighted average = (100,000 × 6/12) + (124,000 × 6/12) = 50,000 + 62,000 = 112,000 shares. The 24,000 new shares are only outstanding for half the year (Jul–Dec), so they're weighted at 6/12.

1-33.q: A 2-for-1 stock split declared on Nov 1 is treated in the EPS calculation as:
o: Weighted for 2 months only
Effective only for future periods
A retroactive adjustment applied to All periods presented as if it occurred at the start
Added only to Q4 shares
1-33.e: Stock splits and stock dividends are Retroactively Adjusted — treated as if they occurred at the Beginning of the earliest period presented. No weighting needed for the split itself; simply double all share counts in All prior periods.

1-33.q: Options to purchase 20,000 shares at $30/share. Average market price $40/share. Net dilutive shares using the treasury stock method =
1-33.e: Shares issued = 20,000. Proceeds = 20,000 × $30 = $600,000. Shares repurchased = $600,000 / $40 = 15,000. Net dilutive shares = 20,000 − 15,000 = 5,000. These 5,000 shares are added to the diluted EPS denominator.

1-33.q: Options with an exercise price of $50 and an average market price of $40 are:
o: Dilutive — include in diluted EPS
Anti-dilutive — Exclude from diluted EPS
Always included regardless
Only included in Basic EPS
1-33.e: Exercise price ($50) > Average market price ($40) → anti-dilutive. The treasury stock method would produce Negative net new shares (proceeds would buy More shares than options exercised), which would Increase EPS. Anti-dilutive securities are Always Excluded from diluted EPS.

1-33.q: Diluted EPS is Always:
o: Greater than or equal to Basic EPS
Less than or equal to Basic EPS
Equal to Basic EPS
Irrelevant if no preferred stock exists
1-33.e: Diluted EPS ≤ Basic EPS. Adding potential shares to the denominator Reduces EPS (assuming dilutive securities). It can Equal Basic EPS if there are no dilutive securities, but it can never Exceed Basic EPS. Anti-dilutive securities are excluded precisely to maintain this relationship.

1-33.q: Convertible Bonds in diluted EPS (if-converted): adjustment to numerator and denominator:
o: Bond interest only
After-Tax interest expense added to numerator And converted shares added to denominator
Converted shares to denominator only
Pre-tax interest to numerator
1-33.e: If-converted for bonds: (1) Add back After-Tax interest (numerator); (2) Add converted shares (denominator). Only included if dilutive.

1-33.q: Convertible Preferred in diluted EPS (if-converted): adjustment:
o: Add preferred dividends + converted shares
Add Back preferred dividends to numerator And add converted shares to denominator
Add converted shares only
Subtract preferred dividends and add converted shares
1-33.e: If-converted for preferred: (1) Add back preferred dividends (that were subtracted in basic); (2) Add converted common shares. No tax adjustment — preferred dividends not tax-deductible.

1-33.q: Stock options are Antidilutive when:
o: Outstanding less than 6 months
Exercise Price Exceeds average market price (out-of-the-money)
Held by executives
Company reports net loss
1-33.e: Antidilutive: exercise price > average market price. Treasury stock method: 'buy back' more shares than received → negative net dilutive shares → antidilutive → Exclude. Also antidilutive with net loss.

1-33.q: Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) included in diluted EPS using:
o: If-converted method
Treasury Stock Method — unamortized compensation as theoretical 'proceeds'
Only when fully vested
Excluded — compensation instrument
1-33.e: Unvested RSUs: Treasury Stock Method. Unamortized compensation treated as theoretical proceeds for buyback. Net shares (Rsu shares minus theoretical buyback) added to denominator.

1-33.q: Two-Class Method for EPS used when company has:
o: Two classes of common stock only
Participating Securities that share dividends/undistributed earnings with common
Both US and foreign shareholders
Convertible debt
1-33.e: Two-class required for Participating Securities (participating preferred, certain RSUs) entitled to share earnings with common shareholders. Both basic and diluted EPS computed per class.

1-33.q: Contingently Issuable Shares included in diluted EPS when:
o: Contingency period not started
Conditions Met or Would Be Met if reporting period were end of contingency period
Shareholders approved
Board declared
1-33.e: Contingently issuable: include if conditions Have Been Met as of period-end or Would Be Met if that date were the contingency end.

1-33.q: EPS must be presented on Face of income statement for:
o: All companies
Public Companies only — private may disclose in notes
Only companies with convertible securities
Only profitable companies
1-33.e: EPS on income statement face Required Only For Public Companies (SEC registrants). Private companies not required. All public must show both basic and diluted EPS.

1-33.q: 10% stock Dividend affects prior period EPS by:
o: Only shares after dividend date adjusted
Retroactive adjustment — as if it occurred at beginning of earliest period presented
Q4 only
Only new shares after declaration included
1-33.e: Stock dividends and splits treated Retroactively — as if at beginning of Earliest Period Presented. All prior period EPS restated for comparability.

1-33.q: Weighted average: 100,000 shares Jan 1. Repurchase 12,000 shares May 1. Weighted average shares =
Multiple dilutive securities — inclusion Order in diluted EPS:
o: Convertible bonds first (highest dilution)
Most Dilutive first (lowest incremental EPS) — stop when next would be antidilutive
All at once — no ordering
Longest remaining term first
1-33.e: Include from Most to Least dilutive (lowest incremental EPS to highest). Stop when adding next security would increase EPS above current diluted level.

1-33.q: EPS from Discontinued Operations is:
o: Included in continuing operations EPS
Reported Separately — on income statement face or in notes
Not disclosed in EPS
Uses different share count
1-33.e: Discontinued operations EPS presented Separately. Reconciles: Continuing Operations EPS ± Discontinued EPS = Total EPS.

1-33.q: When company reports Net Loss, diluted EPS:
o: Always less than basic (more negative)
Equals basic EPS — all dilutive securities are antidilutive in loss period
Is zero
Uses convertible preferred only
1-33.e: Net loss: All potentially dilutive securities are antidilutive (their inclusion reduces the loss per share — less negative, not more dilutive). Diluted EPS = Basic EPS for loss periods.

1-33.q: Interim Period EPS denominator uses:
o: Shares outstanding at quarter-end
Weighted Average shares for Year-To-Date period
Weighted average for that quarter only
Annualized share count
1-33.e: Interim EPS: denominator = weighted average shares for the Year-To-Date period (not just the quarter). Ensures consistency with annual EPS.
